user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last 13 weekly observations of fed_assets show a choppy, essentially flat-to-slightly-down pattern rather than a clear trend. The series oscillates in a narrow band (~4.477M to ~4.502M), with the most recent print (4,482,349) below the prior week (4,488,840) but above several earlier troughs. The 4-week change (-4,238) and 13-week change (-6,990) are both mildly negative but tiny relative to the ~$4.49T balance sheet level (roughly -0.1% to -0.16%), well within noise for a series that isn't on an announced runoff cap in this data. Notably, the policy language explicitly states the Committee is reinvesting principal payments and rolling over maturing Treasuries at auction — i.e., holdings are being maintained at sizable levels, not run off. This is a reinvestment/hold-steady stance, not active QT or QE. Given the policy language commits to maintaining holdings and the recent measured moves are small and non-directional (bouncing week to week), I read this as balance-sheet stability with mild noise rather than a sustained expansion or contraction. My prior call, if it leaned toward a directional move, should be tempered — the data supports 'flat' more than a confident trend call at this horizon.
Would change its mind if: A clear multi-week acceleration in assets_change_4w/13w in one direction, or a policy statement announcing a new runoff cap or asset purchases.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_pct_change_13w, assets_13w_avg

=== curve_slope ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 2s10s spread has been essentially flat-to-modestly range-bound at 1.15-1.19pp over the last 10 readings, with the latest print (1.18) right at the middle of that recent cluster and near the top of its own short-term wobble. Longer-term momentum shows mild flattening pressure (63d change -0.22pp) but the very short-term change (21d -0.05pp) is small, and the last few prints (1.16, 1.18, 1.17, 1.18) show no clear directional trend, just noise. The spread sits only 0.03pp above its 12-month low and in just the 5th percentile of its 12-month range (slope_range_position 0.05), indicating the curve is near its most flattened point of the past year — but it isn't breaking further to new lows currently, it's stabilizing there. The policy language shift (from a hike to a "maintain" statement) reduces near-term front-end upward pressure, which could allow the curve to hold or steepen slightly rather than flatten further. With no fresh hiking signal and the spread hovering near-term without a fresh breakdown, I expect the spread to stay essentially unchanged into month-end, perhaps with a very slight steepening bias given the pause language, but not enough to call a strong directional move.
Would change its mind if: A break below 1.15pp (new 12-month low) or a sustained move above 1.20pp would confirm renewed flattening or steepening respectively.
Leaned on: slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d, slope_range_position, slope_above_12m_low

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: up, conviction 0.65
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The NFCI series shows a clear, persistent uptrend: from -0.47 thirteen weeks ago to -0.28 now, a monotonic rise with no reversals. The 4-week change (+0.07) and 13-week change (+0.17) both confirm steady tightening momentum, and the index is now at its 52-week high (range_position = 1.00), having moved fully off its 52-week low. Despite this, the level remains well below zero (distance_from_zero = -0.28), so conditions are still looser than the historical average — but the direction of travel is unambiguously toward tightening. The new policy language flagging closer monitoring of "global economic and financial developments" and their implications for the outlook suggests officials are alert to this tightening trend, though the unchanged language about accommodative holdings suggests no active reversal of easy policy. Given the consistency of the trend across all horizons (4w, 13w, and the full 13-observation series), I expect the tightening momentum to continue into month-end, likely pushing nfci modestly higher (less negative) again, continuing the pattern of the last several prints. This is a clear signal given the monotonic nature of the move.
Would change its mind if: A flattening or reversal in the weekly nfci prints, or a drop back toward the 52-week low, would indicate the tightening trend has stalled.
Leaned on: nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_range_position, nfci_above_52w_low, nfci_distance_from_zero

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last observation shows breakeven_10y at 1.41, down slightly from 1.42 the prior print but still up from the 1.32-1.36 trough seen a few readings back. My prior view (if it called for continued decline) was likely wrong in the very short term: the last four readings (1.34, 1.36, 1.40, 1.42, 1.41) show a clear bounce off the 12-month low, with only a marginal one-tick pullback at the end. However, the medium-term trend measurements (be_change_63d -0.07, be_change_252d -0.20) confirm the broader trajectory over the past year has been downward, and breakeven remains 0.59pp below the 2% target with range_position of only 0.20 (near the bottom of its 12-month band). The policy language also turned more negative, explicitly noting inflation compensation "declined further" versus merely "remains low" previously — a hawkish-to-dovish shift in Fed characterization that lags but confirms the downtrend. Given the tension between the recent 5-observation bounce and the still-negative statement language plus deeply sub-target level, I lean toward flat-to-slightly-down for the coming month: the bounce may stall given the confirmed narrative of "declined further," but momentum has clearly turned upward in the very recent data.
Would change its mind if: A continued rise above 1.42-1.45 over multiple readings, or dovish policy language reverting to acknowledge stabilization/improvement, would flip this to up.
Leaned on: be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_change_252d, be_distance_from_target, be_range_position

=== inflation ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.40
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 16 days ago.
Report:
  Last month's release (headline 1.24% y/y, up sharply from 0.64%) confirmed the acceleration I expected: the prior 0.44→0.64→1.24 sequence reflects energy base effects rolling off, not a genuine reheating. The most recent monthly print (mom -0.05%) actually turned negative, well below the 3-month average of -0.01% and sharply below the outgoing +0.25%, so headline momentum has clearly stalled even as the y/y rate sits at a 12-month high. That yoy jump is a pure base-effect artifact: headline_3m_annualized is -0.14%, meaning if recent flat/negative monthly prints persist, y/y could plateau or even dip slightly next month once compared against a rising year-ago base. Core PCE tells a very different, more stable story: 1.19% y/y, essentially flat over three months (core_yoy_change_3m -0.04pp), sitting 0.81pp below target with core_3m_annualized only modestly positive at 0.83%. There is no core acceleration to validate the headline's y/y surge. Policy language corroborates this, attributing the shortfall to energy/import price effects expected to be transitory, with inflation compensation measures still soft. Given negative recent monthly prints and a decelerating momentum trend against a rising comparison base, I expect the next headline y/y print to be flat-to-slightly-down rather than continuing its recent sharp climb.
Would change its mind if: A strong positive monthly headline CPI print (>0.2% m/m) driven by broad-based (not just energy) gains would indicate genuine reacceleration, changing this call.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, headline_mom, headline_3m_annualized, mom_gap_vs_outgoing, core_pce_yoy, core_yoy_change_3m, core_3m_annualized

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: down, conviction 0.55
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 23 days ago.
Report:
  The unemployment rate just printed a fresh 12-month low at 4.80%, down from 5.00% the prior two readings and continuing a clear downtrend: -0.20pp over 3 months, -0.40pp over 6 months, -0.90pp over 12 months. The series shows no reversals of consequence — every multi-horizon change is negative, and the rate sits at the 12-month low with zero distance above it (unrate_above_12m_low = 0). The Sahm-style gap is trivially small (+0.17pp) and irrelevant here since the signal is loosening-detection, not tightening; there is no loosening signal at all in this data. Policy language corroborates: the updated statement upgrades from "labor market indicators...move toward" to "shows further improvement" and "diminished appreciably," and the Committee proceeded to raise rates, citing "considerable improvement in labor market conditions." This is a textbook tightening trend with strengthening momentum, not deceleration — the pace of decline hasn't been decelerating (3m pace of -0.2/3mo is actually faster than the 6m average pace of -0.067/mo... roughly consistent). Level is still 0.8pp above the assumed 4% natural rate, leaving room for further tightening before convergence. Given the consistent multi-horizon negative trend, the fresh low, and corroborating policy language, I expect the unemployment rate to tick down or hold near this level next month, not rise.
Would change its mind if: A monthly print showing unemployment rate rising back above 4.9-5.0%, or clear deceleration in job gains language reversing to "diminished progress."
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_6m, unrate_change_12m, unrate_12m_low, unrate_above_12m_low, unrate_distance_from_natural

=== term_premium ===
Call: down, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 10y yield has fallen further, closing at 1.94, the lowest print in this series and a fresh move below the prior cluster around 2.00-2.07. The 21-day change is -0.38pp, a sharp decline, while the 63-day change is a smaller -0.11pp, showing the downtrend has accelerated recently even as the medium-term trend is only mildly lower. The 252-day change is still +0.21pp, so on a 1-year view yields are net higher, but the range position of 0.32 shows the yield sits in the lower third of its 12-month range (1.68-2.50), consistent with a genuine pullback rather than noise. The 30s10s slope at 0.81pp is a normal-to-wide long-end slope, not signaling acute stress, and policy language shows inflation compensation measures declining further, which typically coincides with lower nominal term premium via lower breakevens/duration demand. My prior view (whatever it was) should be checked against this fresh drop to 1.94 — the data now clearly show renewed downward pressure at the long end, driven by softening inflation compensation and continued reinvestment/balance-sheet policy keeping duration demand supported. Given the acceleration in the last 21 days and the low range position, I lean toward continued softness into month-end, though the 63-day trend is milder, tempering conviction.
Would change its mind if: A rebound in dgs10_level back above ~2.05-2.10 within the next few observations, or a reversal in the 21-day change trend, would falsify continued decline.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_range_position, slope_30s10s

=== What the panel says it was never handed ===
  curve_slope lacks financial_conditions — Would clarify if broader tightening/loosening pressures are pushing long-end yields independent of Fed statement language.
  curve_slope lacks term_premium — Would show if long-end richness/cheapness is driving the 10s10y move rather than pure policy expectations.
  inflation_expectations lacks inflation — Realized CPI trend would clarify whether the recent breakeven bounce reflects genuine reflation or noise.
  inflation_expectations lacks term_premium — Distinguishing breakeven moves driven by term premium vs true expectations would sharpen the read.
  term_premium lacks inflation_expectations — Magnitude of breakeven decline would clarify how much of the yield drop is real-rate vs inflation-premium driven.
  term_premium lacks financial_conditions — Broader risk-off or flight-to-quality signals would confirm whether the drop reflects safe-haven demand.

user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: up, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The latest print (4,489,796) is up from the prior reading (4,483,629) and continues a choppy but modestly positive drift. The 4-week change is +7,447M and the 13-week change is +12,708M (+0.28%), both positive, while the 52-week change is only +3,071M, indicating the balance sheet has been roughly flat over the year but has ticked up in recent weeks. Assets remain about 14,908M below the 52-week high, so this is not a resumption of aggressive QE, just noisy fluctuation around a plateau. My prior 'flat' call was roughly right in a stochastic sense—assets have oscillated in a ~20,000M band without a sustained trend—but the most recent 4w and 13w figures both lean positive, so a purely flat call undersells the short-term tilt. Policy language is unchanged: reinvestment continues, no new runoff cap or purchase program announced, consistent with balance-sheet management rather than active expansion or contraction. Given the small magnitude of moves relative to the ~$4.5T base and no policy language shift, I lean toward a slight uptick continuing into next month, but with low-moderate conviction since this could easily reverse as it has in prior weeks.
Would change its mind if: A reversal showing assets_change_4w turning negative by a few thousand million, or explicit policy language announcing renewed runoff caps or purchases.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_pct_change_13w, assets_52w_high, assets_below_52w_high

=== curve_slope ===
Call: down, conviction 0.45
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 2s10s spread has continued grinding lower, now at 0.96pp — a fresh 12-month low, sitting at the very bottom of its range (slope_range_position 0.00). My prior flat call was wrong: the trend that was already visible (slope_change_63d -0.34, slope_change_252d -0.41) has persisted into a new low rather than stabilizing. The last two prints (0.96, 0.96) show the pace of flattening decelerating somewhat versus the sharper drops earlier in the series, but there's no sign of reversal — no bounce, no steepening impulse. The 21-day change (-0.21pp) confirms the flattening is still active over the past month, not just a stale historical drift. Policy language shows the Fed moving from a hike to a hold, describing similarly moderate growth and gradual policy — this doesn't obviously argue for either curve direction from language alone, but the front end (dgs2 +0.78%) still reflects tightening expectations pressing against the back end (dgs10 +1.74%), keeping the spread compressed near cycle lows. With the spread pinned at the 12-month low and the multi-horizon momentum uniformly negative, the balance of evidence favors continued flattening or at best stabilization at a low level, not a rebound.
Would change its mind if: A rise back above ~1.05-1.10pp (clear break from the 12-month low) would signal steepening resumption; continued prints at/below 0.96 confirms flattening.
Leaned on: slope_2s10s, slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d, slope_change_252d, slope_12m_low, slope_range_position, slope_above_12m_low

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior call (up, 0.65) has been overtaken by the data: since then the index has essentially flattened and even ticked down slightly (last three prints: -0.27, -0.27, -0.28), and nfci_change_4w is +0.00. That is precisely the stalling-out pattern I said would change my mind. Looking at the broader window, the 13-week trend (+0.17) still shows a multi-month tightening move off the -0.56 low, and the level sits at nfci_range_position 0.96 — near the top of its 52-week range — so conditions remain comparatively taut relative to recent history. But the momentum that drove that move has clearly dried up over the most recent weeks, and the index is still well below the zero average (distance -0.28), meaning conditions are actually loose in absolute terms despite the tightening trend. Policy language reiterates that asset holdings should keep financial conditions "accommodative," consistent with the still-negative level, while the new language about "monitoring global developments" suggests watchfulness rather than a clear new tightening or easing impulse. Given the flattening in the short-run prints, I don't see a basis for continued tightening into month-end; the most defensible call is flat, with only slight lean given the possible tiny downtick in the last print.
Would change its mind if: A resumption of consecutive week-over-week increases in nfci, or a clear break above -0.27 (the 52w high), would revive the tightening call.
Leaned on: nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_range_position, nfci_distance_from_zero

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: up, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last 10 readings show a clear uptrend: from 1.25 to 1.42, with the most recent five prints monotonically rising (1.32→1.36→1.39→1.41→1.42). My prior "flat" call was wrong — the series continued climbing rather than stalling, and the 21d change of +0.00 masks the fact that this flat reading sits at the top of a steady multi-month climb rather than reflecting genuine stagnation; the 63d (-0.19) and 252d (-0.37) changes are stale lagging artifacts of the earlier decline before the recent recovery. Breakeven is now 0.24pp above its 12m low and sits at the 32nd percentile of its 12m range — still historically low and 0.58pp below the 2% objective, but the trajectory is unambiguously upward over the last several readings. Notably, the policy language actually moved dovish/backward-looking ("declined further," "little changed... in recent months") — but this is describing the older declining trend and lags the actual data, which has since turned up. Given the persistent step-by-step increase across the last five observations and no sign of reversal, I'm shifting to up. Conviction is moderate given the still-low absolute level and directional tension with the lagging policy language.
Would change its mind if: A print back below 1.39-1.40, or renewed multi-reading decline, would flip this back to flat/down.
Leaned on: be_above_12m_low, be_range_position, be_distance_from_target

=== inflation ===
Call: down, conviction 0.40
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 14 days ago.
Report:
  My prior "flat" call was wrong: headline y/y jumped from 0.44 to 0.64 to 1.24 before pulling back to 0.85 last print. The most recent monthly readings (-0.11, -0.05, -0.13) show three consecutive negative m/m prints, and the 3m annualized headline pace is now -1.14%, well below the outgoing 0.27% m/m pace — a sharp deceleration in momentum even though the y/y rate remains elevated relative to its 12-month range (position 0.71). This looks like energy-driven volatility: the y/y spike to 1.24 was likely a base-effect/energy pop that is now unwinding, consistent with headline falling back to 0.85 despite core staying subdued. Core PCE, the better persistence read, ticked up from ~1.19 to 1.41 y/y — a genuine but modest increase, and core_3m_annualized (+1.40%) is running close to that y/y level, suggesting no strong core acceleration, just noise around a low, sub-target trend. Policy language reinforces this: inflation compensation "declined further," expectations "little changed," and inflation still expected to "remain low in the near term." Given three straight negative headline monthly prints and negative 3m annualized headline momentum, the more likely near-term path is headline cooling further from 0.85, though the recent volatility in this series limits confidence.
Would change its mind if: A strong positive m/m headline CPI print (>0.2%) driven by broad-based, non-energy gains would signal reacceleration and reverse this call.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, headline_mom, headline_3m_annualized, mom_gap_vs_outgoing, core_pce_yoy, core_3m_annualized, yoy_range_position

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.35
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 21 days ago.
Report:
  The last print ticked up to 4.90% from a 12-month low of 4.80%, breaking a string of new lows and validating the risk I flagged last time (rising back above ~4.9-5.0%). That said, this is a single-month uptick of just 0.10pp against a 12-month decline of 0.60pp and a 3-month/6-month change still net -0.20pp — the broader trend remains one of gradual tightening (falling unemployment), not a reversal. The Sahm-style gap (current vs 12m low) is only 0.10pp, far below levels that would signal a genuine turn. Range position is low (0.13), meaning the rate sits near the bottom of its 12-month range, and distance from the ~4% natural rate is still meaningfully positive at 0.90pp, meaning slack has room to keep shrinking. Policy language continues to affirm "further improvement," "additional decline in underutilization," and "considerable improvement in labor market conditions" — no language suggesting stalling or reversal. Given one soft print doesn't overturn a still-intact downtrend, but the tick-up is real evidence of possible flattening in the pace of decline, I read this as a stabilization/pause rather than continued sustained decline. I'll shift toward flat-to-slightly-down, tempering prior conviction given the miss.
Would change its mind if: A print rising further to 5.0%+ with sahm_gap widening past 0.3-0.5pp, or policy language shifting from "improvement" to "diminished progress."
Leaned on: unrate_change_12m, unrate_12m_low, sahm_gap, unrate_range_position, unrate_distance_from_natural

=== term_premium ===
Call: down, conviction 0.45
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The previous "down" call was correct: dgs10 has drifted from ~1.78 to 1.74, with negative 21d (-0.26pp), 63d (-0.49pp) and 252d (-0.29pp) changes all confirming a sustained decline. The yield sits near the bottom of its 12-month range (range_position 0.13, only 0.11pp above the 12m low of 1.63%), well below the 12m high of 2.50%. This pattern—persistent multi-horizon declines and a low range position—points to continued compression in the long-end term premium rather than reversal. The 10s30s slope remains moderately steep (0.87pp), suggesting the long end isn't being pinned by heavy issuance pressure at this moment; if anything, the modest recent uptick from 1.71 to 1.76 to 1.74 shows some choppiness but no clear reversal. Policy language reinforces this: market-based inflation compensation "declined further" and long-term survey expectations are little changed, consistent with continued downward pressure on nominal long-end yields via lower embedded premia rather than any flight-to-quality-driven spike. There's no clear balance-sheet or issuance signal here to counter the trend. Given the consistency across short, medium, and long lookbacks, and the low range position, I maintain a moderate-conviction call for continued softness in the 10-year yield into next month-end, though the recent short-term stabilization tempers strong conviction.
Would change its mind if: A rebound in dgs10_level back above ~1.95-2.00 within the next few observations, or a clear reversal in the 21-day change trend, would falsify continued decline.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_change_252d, dgs10_range_position, dgs10_above_12m_low, slope_30s10s

arbitrated.notes:
The direct curve_slope read (2s10s at 1.06, flat vs. prior) is itself the best-informed slope input: it's fresh, and it explicitly reconciles the recent bounce off lows against a stalled medium-term flattening trend (63d -0.12pp, 252d -0.38pp, range position 13th percentile). I anchor on this: no durable steepening yet, longer-run bias still flattening, but near-term likely range-bound.

Cross-checking with front-end and long-end drivers that hit 2s and 10s asymmetrically: labor_tightness shows unemployment rising three straight months off cycle lows (5.10% from 4.80%), a loosening labor signal that argues for front-end yields to stay anchored/fall as rate-cut expectations build — a steepening force via the 2y. Inflation is accelerating sharply (headline CPI y/y 1.17%, conviction 0.60), which independently pushes up inflation-expectations and nominal yields, but this hits both ends via level, not obviously the slope, unless breakevens (up, 0.35) disproportionately lift the long end's inflation compensation, which would also be a modest steepening force. term_premium is stale/conflicted (down lean after a round-trip spike to 1.94 back to 1.83) and financial_conditions and balance_sheet are level/duration-relevant but don't cleanly map to slope without term_premium's missing balance_sheet linkage flagged in the gaps.

Netting it: labor loosening (dovish front end) + firming inflation/breakevens (long-end inflation premium) both lean the same direction — modest steepening pressure — even though the direct curve_slope read says "flat now, flattening bias longer-run." Given the fresh curve_slope print is itself inconclusive/range-bound rather than confidently flattening, and two independent drivers argue for incremental steepening, I lean slightly toward steepening being the more probable break, but with low conviction given the curve series itself hasn't moved.

discounted.why: Whipsawed (1.78→1.94→1.83) and stale relative to fresher curve_slope print; conflicting signal, low reliability for slope.
Level/duration driver, no clean front-vs-long differentiation; policy language unchanged so low slope relevance.
Level driver hitting both ends; use inflation_expectations/breakevens instead for slope-relevant long-end read.

arbitrated.falsifier: A curve_slope print breaking cleanly below 1.00 (confirming flattening) or unemployment reversing back to 4.9-5.0% would flip this to a flattening call.

risks.text: Curve_slope series itself shows no confirmed steepening; call rests on cross-driver inference, not direct evidence.
Fed language still backward-looking; a hawkish surprise on inflation could flatten curve if front end repriced up faster.
Term_premium's recent round-trip shows long-end yields are volatile and could resume rising, muddying steepening thesis.

trade.rationale: Modest steepener: labor loosening softens front-end anchor while firming inflation/breakevens lift long-end inflation premium; direct slope print stays range-bound so conviction kept low.

why.curve_slope: Direct reading is flat/range-bound; medium-term flattening bias persists but no fresh breakout signal.
why.labor_tightness: Unemployment rising 3 straight months from cycle low — a genuine loosening-off-lows signal, front-end dovish/steepening force.

arbitrated.notes:
My mandate is the 2s10s slope, not the level. The direct curve_slope reading (0.85→0.88→0.91) gives a modest steepening tilt but with real caveats: it's still near 12-month lows, 21d/63d changes are still negative, and the analyst itself flags this as retracement-within-flat rather than confirmed steepening. I weight this as the anchor but discount its conviction toward the low end.

The cross-reads matter here because front-end and long-end are being pulled by different forces. Term_premium reports the 10y pinned near 12-month lows (1.49%, range position 0.03) with sustained negative 21d/63d/252d momentum and a flight-to-quality tone — this is a long-end anchoring force that argues against steepening, or even for renewed flattening, since the back end isn't selling off. Labor_tightness is flat/range-bound with no trend — this removes a common front-end-tightening steepener catalyst (no Fed hawkish repricing at the front end from labor data). Financial_conditions tightening modestly is a long-end-relevant but ambiguous signal — tighter conditions can compress term premium (flattening) or reflect Fed normalization expectations (front-end selloff, flattening) — either way it doesn't support steepening. Inflation ticking up modestly could pressure the front end via rate expectations, which would flatten, not steepen, if the long end stays anchored by falling term premium.

Reconciling: the direct slope reading's own uptick is real but shallow and sits against a backdrop of a still-falling, anchored long end (term_premium) and no front-end tightening catalyst (labor flat). The slope call's positive tilt is more consistent with "bottoming/stabilizing" than a durable steepening trend. I lean modestly steepening but with low conviction, since the term-premium anchor could just as easily reassert flattening.

discounted.why: Level driver, not directly slope-relevant; effect on long-end supply ambiguous and unconfirmed by policy language.
Ambiguous slope implication — tightening could flatten via either front or long end equally.

arbitrated.falsifier: A break of 10y yield decisively back above 1.65-1.70 (term premium rebuilding) alongside slope holding above 0.95 would confirm real steepening; a slope reversal below 0.88 would flip to flattening.

risks.text: Slope call is thin and could easily be overwhelmed by a parallel level move either analyst is tracking.
Term premium's anchored-long-end view directly contradicts curve_slope's steepening tick; regime is genuinely ambiguous.
Fed language still gradualist; a hawkish surprise on labor or inflation could flatten curve via front-end repricing.

trade.rationale: Small steepener reflecting curve_slope's shallow uptick, sized down given term_premium's anchored long-end pull and no front-end tightening catalyst from labor.

why.curve_slope: Direct slope reading ticks up (0.85→0.91) but still near 12m lows with negative 21d/63d trend; shallow steepen.
why.term_premium: 10y pinned near 12m low, sustained negative momentum; long end anchored, capping steepening, arguing flattening risk.
why.labor_tightness: Flat, range-bound; no front-end tightening catalyst to drive steepening or flattening.

This is corroborated by term_premium, which shows dgs10 grinding lower (1.52→1.46, near 12-month lows) even as curve_slope notes dgs2 rising — long end anchored/falling while front end firms or holds, which is flattening pressure from both ends of the trade (long yields down, short yields up/steady). Labor tightness is flat/range-bound at cycle lows in unemployment, which if anything supports continued front-end firmness (Fed still perceived as tight-biased, consistent with the hawkish FOMC dissent curve_slope cites), reinforcing rather than contradicting the flattening call. Balance sheet is now called down (runoff resuming) — a shrinking balance sheet is a duration/term-premium support factor for long yields (upward pressure), which would be a mild counterweight to flattening, but the analyst has only moderate conviction (0.45) and the weekly series is described as "choppy," so I discount this input's power to reverse the well-evidenced spread compression. Financial conditions and inflation expectations are flat with low-to-moderate conviction and don't bear directly on the front-vs-long distinction. I side with curve_slope's own direct read, cross-confirmed by term_premium's long end behavior, and expect continued mild flattening into month-end, not a reversal. Given the language, this looks like a flat-hold reinvestment regime with noisy week-to-week fluctuations rather than genuine QE or QT, and the latest uptick is within the noise band seen elsewhere in the series (e.g., prior 4482009 peak, 4461393 trough). Call: flat, conviction 0.35

The 21d and 63d changes are both modestly negative (-0.10, -0.14), and the 252d change (-0.69) shows a large multi-quarter flattening trend, though its pace has clearly decelerated versus earlier in the year. The tentative 2-print bounce off the 0.76 low is the main reason for caution against extrapolating further flattening mechanically. Given proximity to 12-month lows and stabilizing short-term momentum, I lean toward continued flatness with a slight flattening bias rather than a clean reversal.

Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.

The 4-week change is essentially flat (-0.01) and the 13-week change is negligible (+0.01), confirming no meaningful momentum in either direction. The index sits well below zero (-0.37), indicating financial conditions remain looser than historical average, consistent with the accommodative-policy language cited. Range position (0.51) shows the index is mid-pack within its own 52-week range, not at an extreme. My prior flat call was correct: the index neither broke above -0.34 nor below -0.38 sustainably — it drifted marginally within the band and settled at -0.37, the loose end of the recent range but not a new low. With no change in policy language and no directional momentum in the underlying data, I see no basis for a directional call this month. The persistent flatness across 13 data points is itself informative: this is a low-volatility regime for financial conditions, and absent new shocks, the index is most likely to stay near -0.37 at month end.

Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last reading ticked up to 1.47 from 1.46, continuing a very narrow range that has held between roughly 1.46-1.49 for the last ten observations. My prior "flat" call holds up: nothing has broken the 1.44-1.55 band I set as the falsifier, and the 21d change (+0.03pp) is a modest bounce within noise, while 63d (-0.07) and 252d (-0.14) still show a mild downward drift over longer windows. Breakevens remain firmly below the 2% objective (-0.53pp distance) and sit near the middle of their 12-month range (0.54 range position), not near either extreme. The policy language shift — from "declined" to "remain low" — is notable: it signals the Committee now sees the earlier decline as having stabilized at a low level rather than continuing to fall, which is consistent with the recent sideways chop in the data rather than a fresh leg down. Survey-based expectations are described as unchanged, reinforcing a lack of momentum in either direction. Absent a break of the range, I see no basis for a directional call. I'll maintain flat, with slightly higher conviction than before given the language change corroborates stabilization rather than renewed decline.

Call: up, conviction 0.35
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 16 days ago.
Report:
  The last release brought a rebound: headline_mom came in at +0.18% (essentially at my ~0.20% threshold), reversing the prior -0.05% dip that had made me flat last time. That confirms the "flat" call was reasonable pending resolution, and the new print leans back toward the uptrend rather than a genuine reversal. Headline yoy ticked up to 1.06% from 0.87%, though the 3m-annualized headline (1.66%) sits below the latest yoy, and headline_yoy_change_3m is essentially flat (-0.02pp), suggesting the trend is not accelerating sharply — it's more a bounce than a breakout. Core PCE continues its steadier climb, now 1.60% y/y, up from 1.55%, with core_yoy_change_3m +0.08pp — modest but persistent upward drift, still 0.40pp below target. Core-minus-headline (+0.54pp) shows core still running hotter, the more persistent signal. Policy language reinforces a still-low inflation regime, with market-based inflation compensation reiterated as "remain low" (little changed in framing) and inflation still described as running below target. Given the mom rebound plus continued core drift upward, I lean modestly toward a higher headline print next month, but momentum is not decisive — no clear acceleration signal, just gradual grind higher.
Would change its mind if: A drop back below 0% mom or renewed sub-1.0% yoy headline would confirm this bounce was noise, not a resumed uptrend.
Leaned on: headline_mom, outgoing_mom, mom_gap_vs_outgoing, headline_cpi_yoy, core_pce_yoy, core_yoy_change_3m, headline_yoy_change_3m, core_minus_headline

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.35
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 23 days ago.
Report:
  The latest print (4.90%) ticked up from the prior month's 4.80% but remains well within the 12-month range (4.80–5.10%). My prior "flat" call is validated: the series continues to oscillate in a tight 0.30pp band with no directional break. The 3-month change is a modest +0.10pp, but the 6-month change is exactly 0.00pp, and the 12-month change is -0.20pp — signs of a stable-to-slightly-improving labor market, not a loosening one. The Sahm-rule-style gap (current vs. 12m low) is only 0.10pp, far below any threshold that would signal recessionary loosening. The level itself (4.90%) is within 0.9pp of the ~4% natural rate estimate, consistent with a historically tight labor market. Updated policy language reinforces this: the Fed now describes labor market strengthening and strong [date] job gains, replacing prior language about slowing improvement. This is a hawkish-for-labor shift, suggesting the loosening narrative from last month was noise, not trend. With unemployment bouncing between 4.8-5.1% for over a year without breaking either bound, and rhetoric now more positive, I see no basis for calling a rise. I maintain a flat call, leaning very slightly toward stability given the reversion pattern and improved policy tone.
Would change its mind if: A print breaking decisively above 5.10% (new 12m high) or below 4.80% for two consecutive months would confirm a genuine trend rather than range noise.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_6m, unrate_change_12m, sahm_gap, unrate_range_width, unrate_distance_from_natural, unrate_12m_low, unrate_12m_high

=== term_premium ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.25
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior call (down, 0.55) was roughly right on trend but the most recent prints show stabilization, not continued decline. The 10y sits at 1.58, up slightly from the 1.55-1.56 trough a few observations back, with 21d change now +0.03pp (flipped positive) even though 63d (-0.23) and 252d (-0.61) remain firmly negative. The yield is still low in its 12-month range (range_position 0.21, just 0.21pp above the 12m low of 1.37), consistent with a still-suppressed term premium versus the past year. However, the recent uptick from 1.55 to 1.58, with a brief spike to 1.62, suggests the downtrend has lost momentum and may be leveling off or turning modestly higher near-term. The 10s30s slope (+0.65) and 10s2s slope (+0.78) show no compression signaling imminent flight-to-quality demand at the long end. Policy language shift (inflation compensation "declined" vs "remain low") reflects lower breakevens, which could keep nominal long yields anchored but doesn't point to fresh downward pressure on term premium. Given the flattening trajectory and modest recent uptick, I lean toward flat-to-slightly-up rather than continued decline, with low-moderate conviction given the still-negative medium/long-term trend.
Would change its mind if: A break back below ~1.50 would confirm the downtrend resumed; a sustained move above 1.65 would confirm reversal to rising premium.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_change_252d, dgs10_range_position, slope_30s10s, slope_10s2s

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: down, conviction 0.40
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The latest print (4,452,002) is down sharply from the prior observation (4,473,032), and the short-run trend metrics confirm renewed softness: assets_change_4w is -5,905M and assets_change_13w is -14,480M, both negative, with the series now 52,702M below its 52-week high. My prior call of "up" was wrong — I had leaned on noisy week-to-week bounces in a series that has been oscillating in a narrow band around 4.46-4.48tn, but the newest data point breaks to the low end of that range and the 4w/13w changes have flipped negative exactly the trigger I set for reverting to a down call. That said, the policy language is unchanged: the Committee describes reinvestment of principal and rollover of maturing Treasuries at auction, i.e., a balance-sheet-maintenance stance, not an announced runoff program with explicit monthly caps. So the drift is small in absolute terms (-0.32% over 13 weeks) and could still be portfolio/settlement noise around a flat reinvestment policy rather than a deliberate shift to contraction. Given the confirmed negative short-term pace and breach of the recent range, but with policy language still describing reinvestment (not runoff) and the magnitude modest, I lean down but with only moderate conviction.
Would change its mind if: A rebound in assets_change_4w back to positive, or explicit statement language announcing an end to reinvestment/onset of runoff caps, would change this view.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_pct_change_13w, assets_below_52w_high

=== curve_slope ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.40
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 2s10s spread has stabilized around 0.81-0.83pp over the last several prints after a steady multi-month flattening (252d change -0.58pp). The last 4 readings (0.83, 0.81, 0.82, 0.83, 0.83) show the slide has essentially halted, and 21d change is now slightly positive (+0.05pp) even as 63d change remains mildly negative (-0.04pp). My prior flat call was correct — the spread neither pushed above 0.85-0.90 nor broke below 0.76, it just consolidated in a narrow 0.81-0.83 band. The spread sits at only 0.10 on the 12-month range (near the low end), meaning further flattening has limited room before hitting new lows, while any hawkish repricing of the front end (dgs2 up sharply relative to dgs10, per the derived measurements) has been the dominant force behind the multi-month compression. Policy language shows a shift toward acknowledging strengthened case for a hike, which likely pressures the front end more than the long end — a flattening bias — but the last few prints show that pressure has already been absorbed without further inversion. Absent a fresh catalyst, I expect near-term consolidation rather than a clear directional break in either direction.
Would change its mind if: A sustained push above 0.85-0.90 over multiple prints would signal reversal to steepening; renewed prints below 0.80 with downward momentum would confirm resumed flattening.
Leaned on: slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d, slope_change_252d, slope_range_position, slope_12m_low

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: up, conviction 0.40
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The NFCI series has crept up from -0.37 to -0.34 over the last several weeks, a small but persistent drift toward tighter conditions. The 4-week change (+0.02) and 13-week change (+0.01) are both positive, and the latest print (-0.34) matches the top of the recent range and sits at the high end of the 52-week band (range_position 0.64). This is the same -0.34 level I flagged last time as the threshold for a tightening signal, and the index has now held at or near that level for the last two readings, which per my own falsifier criterion counts as a sustained move. That said, the index remains well below zero (distance_from_zero -0.34), so conditions are still historically loose — this is a mild loosening-of-accommodation trend, not tight conditions in absolute terms. Policy language is unchanged and still emphasizes maintaining "accommodative financial conditions," which caps how much further tightening momentum is likely to build near-term. Given the shallow but real upward drift and the touch of the -0.34 threshold, I lean toward continued modest tightening (higher index) rather than flat, though the move is small enough that conviction is limited.
Would change its mind if: A drop back below -0.36 for two consecutive weeks would negate the tightening drift and confirm renewed easing.
Leaned on: nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_range_position, nfci_distance_from_zero

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: up, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "flat" call is now overtaken by the data: breakeven_10y has moved from 1.54 to 1.60 in the latest print, the highest of the 10-observation window, and sits at 0.78 of its 12-month range (near the high end, +0.42pp above the 12m low). The 21d/63d/252d changes are all positive and modestly accelerating (+0.13, +0.17, +0.21pp), indicating a persistent upward drift rather than noise. This clears the falsifier I set previously (sustained break above 1.55 over two+ readings — confirmed by 1.56/1.54/1.60 clustering above that level). Still, breakeven remains 0.40pp below the 2% objective, so this is a recovery within a low regime, not overshoot risk. Policy language describes market-based inflation compensation as "remaining low" and long-term survey expectations "little changed" — a static, backward-looking description that lags the recent uptick and doesn't yet contradict it. Given the consistent multi-horizon rise and position near the top of the range, I now lean toward continued modest upward drift into month-end, though the move is not large enough to warrant high conviction given how compressed the range is and the still-below-target level.
Would change its mind if: A drop back below 1.55 sustained over two+ readings would signal the uptrend has stalled and reverted to range-bound behavior.
Leaned on: be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_change_252d, be_range_position, be_above_12m_low

=== inflation ===
Call: up, conviction 0.55
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 15 days ago.
Report:
  The prior "up" call was validated: headline CPI y/y jumped from 1.06% to 1.55%, a +0.47pp move over 3 months and the highest print in the 12-month window (range position 1.00). The mom print of +0.26% is above the outgoing pace and above the 3m average, so momentum is not decelerating. Core PCE also continues to grind higher, 1.60→1.70% y/y, with a steady multi-month uptrend from the ~1.2% base seen earlier in the window — core is still 0.30pp below target but closing the gap consistently, and core_yoy_change_3m of +0.13pp confirms persistence rather than noise. Longer-window measures (13w -10,172, 52w -35,013, pct_13w -0.23%) still show a mild downtrend and the balance sheet remains well below its 52w high (-47,369), consistent with a slow drift lower/flat rather than active runoff. The policy language is unchanged — still describing simple reinvestment/rollover policy at existing levels, with no mention of caps or runoff, which is generic boilerplate rather than confirmation of QT pace. Given the choppy weekly prints (bouncing within a ~30k band) and the 4w measure now positive while 13w/52w still negative, the signal is genuinely mixed. I'm revising toward flat, since the most recent momentum indicator contradicts the longer trend and neither dominates convincingly.
Would change its mind if: A clear run of 3+ consecutive weekly declines resuming, or explicit statement language announcing runoff caps, would confirm renewed downtrend.

Call: up, conviction 0.45

Both 21d and 63d changes are positive (+0.15, +0.14 pp), confirming a steepening trend has taken hold over the past one-to-three months, even though the 252-day change remains negative (-0.39 pp), showing the broader-year trend was flattening before this recent reversal. The spread is now 0.22 pp above its 12-month low and sits at the 31st percentile of its 12-month range — still on the low side historically, but the momentum is clearly upward. My prior call of "flat" was too cautious in retrospect: the 21d/63d momentum was already positive last time and has persisted, with the level rising from ~0.93 to a recent 0.98-1.00 band. This is a real, if modest, steepening move. The slight pullback from 1.00 to 0.98 in the newest print could be noise or the start of consolidation, but with two consecutive short-term windows confirming positive momentum and the level clearly off the 12-month low, I lean toward continued steepening rather than reversal. The 10s30s slope (0.74) is context only and not driving this call.
Would change its mind if: A drop back below 0.93-0.95 sustained over multiple prints, or negative 21d/63d changes reappearing, would signal the steepening has stalled or reversed.

Distance from zero remains firmly negative (-0.37), so financial conditions are still looser than historical average, with no policy language suggesting a shift — the Fed's accommodative-conditions language is unchanged. Given the fresh reversal and negative 4-week momentum, I lean toward continued mild easing or flatness rather than renewed tightening. Breakeven remains 0.27pp below the 2% objective, leaving room for further convergence before hitting an anchoring ceiling, which historically has been a headwind as expectations approach target. Policy language is stale/unchanged, describing inflation compensation as "low," which lags the actual move in market pricing and doesn't provide new momentum. Given the string of higher prints and no evidence of reversal, the near-term path likely continues modestly upward, but the flattening in the last two observations (1.73→1.73) suggests the sharp acceleration phase may be pausing. I lean toward continued firmness but with less conviction than before given the deceleration signal, expecting drift up or sideways rather than another sharp leg higher.

Headline mom came in at 0.23%, close to the 3m average of 0.23% and above the outgoing 0.12%, so momentum is holding rather than stalling. Headline y/y is now at a 12m high and sits at the top of its range (yoy_range_position = 1.00), reflecting a full year of steady climbing off the 0.44% trough. Core PCE y/y is essentially flat this month (1.70→1.69) and its 3m annualized pace (1.83%) has decelerated versus the y/y level, suggesting core persistence is leveling off even as headline continues higher — likely energy/base effects doing more work in headline. Since headline is the horizon target here, and its 3m annualized (2.76%) exceeds the y/y print, momentum still points upward for the next release. Policy language describing inflation "running below" target is stale relative to the data trend, but that's context not a current signal. I'll maintain an up call, though smaller acceleration in core moderates conviction slightly versus last time.
Would change its mind if: A headline mom print near/below 0.10% or y/y print flat/below 1.6% would indicate this acceleration has stalled.

The range position of 0.33 shows the rate sits in the lower-middle of its recent band, not stretched toward the top. Level-wise, at 4.90% the market is running about 0.9pp below a 4% natural-rate anchor context is a red herring for me, but on pure level-vs-history the series has been oscillating in a tight band with no directional persistence. Policy language shifted to describe the labor market as having "continued to strengthen" with "little changed" unemployment, consistent with a range-bound, non-deteriorating labor market. user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.25
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last 13 weekly readings of total assets show no directional trend, just noisy oscillation around ~4.45-4.47 trillion. The most recent value (4,452,838) is essentially in line with the 13-week average (4,456,402) and close to where it started 13 weeks ago. The 4-week change is a negligible +1,387M (essentially flat), while the 13-week change is -1,488M (also negligible, effectively zero in the context of a multi-trillion balance sheet). The 52-week change of -29,511M represents a very slow drift lower over a full year, less than 1% annualized, far below any meaningful runoff pace. Critically, the policy language is unchanged: the Committee describes reinvesting principal payments and rolling over maturing Treasuries at auction — this is the standard reinvestment/maintenance language, not an active runoff-cap regime or QE. My prior flat call was correct: assets did not show a clear consecutive-week trend, and the 4-week reading actually ticked up. There is no new statement language indicating any change in caps or reinvestment policy. Given the tiny magnitude of both 4-week and 13-week changes, and static policy language, the balance sheet looks essentially flat, with no informative pace signal in either direction.
Would change its mind if: A clear run of 3+ consecutive weekly declines or gains, or explicit statement language announcing new runoff caps, reinvestment suspension, or QE resumption.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_pct_change_13w, assets_13w_avg

=== curve_slope ===
Call: down, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 2s10s spread has been essentially flat to slightly softer over the last 10 prints (1.19→1.22→1.28→1.25→1.26→1.30→1.30→1.27→1.27→1.26), having plateaued after a steepening run. My prior "down" call was directionally correct but overstated: the spread didn't fall meaningfully, it just stalled and drifted 0.04pp off its recent 1.30 peak. slope_change_21d is essentially zero (-0.01pp), confirming near-term stagnation, while slope_change_63d (+0.28) and slope_change_252d (+0.09) still show the broader steepening trend intact. The spread sits at 0.86 of its 12-month range, near the high end, having steepened well off its 0.76 low. The Fed's [date] hike (25bp) raises the front end directly, which mechanically pressures 2s10s flatter unless long yields rise in tandem — with dgs2 and dgs10 both up substantially recently, some of that hike may already be priced. With the front end now higher post-hike and no fresh steepening catalyst in the language, I lean toward continued flattening pressure, but the magnitude looks modest given the plateau in the last several prints rather than a sharp reversal.
Would change its mind if: A clear break back above 1.30 with positive 21d change would signal steepening resumed rather than the hike-driven flattening I expect.
Leaned on: slope_2s10s, slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d, slope_range_position, slope_12m_high

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: down, conviction 0.50
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The NFCI series has extended its steady decline, now at -0.49, a fresh 52-week low, down from -0.48 the prior reading. The trend remains smooth and monotonic across all 13 observations with no reversals, confirming persistent easing momentum. The 4-week change (-0.02) and 13-week change (-0.12) both confirm continued, if modestly decelerating, easing. Conditions remain well below the zero average (distance -0.49) and sit at the very bottom of the 52-week range (range_position 0.00), indicating financial conditions are historically loose and still loosening, not stabilizing. My prior call (down, 0.55) was correct: the index continued to fall and did not approach my falsifier level of -0.41. The pace of decline per period is small (~-0.01/week average) but consistent, suggesting continued gradual easing rather than a turning point. Policy language is unchanged, reiterating accommodative intent, consistent with continued loose conditions. There is no evidence of any base-building or upward inflection. I maintain a down call for next month-end, though conviction is trimmed slightly given the deceleration in the pace of decline (from -0.02 4-week change, smaller than earlier periods), suggesting the easing trend, while intact, may be flattening somewhat.
Would change its mind if: A rise back above -0.41 (13w avg) sustained for two consecutive weeks would signal renewed tightening and reverse this easing call.
Leaned on: nfci, nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_distance_from_zero, nfci_range_position, nfci_52w_low

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  My prior "flat" call was wrong: breakeven_10y continued to climb through the recent window, peaking at 2.08 before the last two readings ticked down to 2.07 and 2.05. The 21d/63d/252d changes are all solidly positive (+0.11, +0.33, +0.63 pp), and the series sits near the top of its 12-month range (range_position 0.97, only 0.05pp from the 12m high). Policy language also confirms the move, upgrading "moved up" to "moved up considerably" — a hawkish-leaning acknowledgment of rising market-based inflation compensation, even as it retains "still are low." This is directly consistent with the multi-month uptrend. However, the last two prints (2.07, 2.05) represent a modest pullback of 3bp from the 2.08 plateau, which could either be noise or the start of consolidation after a strong run. Given the strength of the trailing trend and the confirming policy language, but tempered by the recent two-reading softening near the range top, I lean toward continued firmness rather than a fresh leg up, with meaningful two-sided risk. The distance-from-target is now small (+0.05pp), reducing the room for further catch-up appreciation.
Would change its mind if: A drop below 2.00 sustained over two+ readings, or language reverting to just "moved up" without "considerably," would signal renewed downside.
Leaned on: breakeven_10y, be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_change_252d, be_range_position, be_distance_from_target

=== inflation ===
Call: up, conviction 0.45
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 16 days ago.
Report:
  The prior "up" call was correct: headline CPI y/y jumped from 2.05 to 2.51, a large +0.46pp move, driven by a strong 0.40% m/m print (the highest in the 13-month window) versus an outgoing mom of -0.13%, so the base-effect comparison alone doesn't explain it — actual momentum accelerated. Headline 3m annualized (3.14%) is running well above the 12-month trend and above core, consistent with energy/food giving a temporary lift, but the m/m print itself was firm. Core PCE y/y, my preferred gauge for persistence, ticked down slightly to 1.77 from 1.72... actually rose from 1.72 to 1.77, still up modestly on the 3-month view (+0.08pp) but core 3m annualized is only 1.16%, decelerating and well below target-consistent readings. So underlying persistent inflation looks stable-to-soft, while headline is being pushed up by more volatile components and a hot recent month. Given headline is now at the top of its 12-month range (yoy_range_position=1.00) and monthly momentum remains firm (0.40% last month, 0.26% 3m avg), I expect headline y/y to tick up further or hold near this elevated level next month rather than reverse, even though core suggests the underlying trend is more muted. Policy language also confirms inflation "has increased" with expectations firming somewhat.
Would change its mind if: A headline mom print below 0.15% next release combined with core PCE y/y flat or lower would confirm the recent acceleration was a one-off spike, not sustained.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, headline_mom, core_pce_yoy, headline_3m_annualized, core_3m_annualized, yoy_range_position, mom_gap_vs_outgoing

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.45
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 23 days ago.
Report:
  The most recent print held at 4.70%, matching the prior two months, so my prior "down" call did not materialize this round — the rate is flat, not still falling. Looking at the full path, unemployment has been range-bound between 4.70-5.10% over the trailing year, with the last three readings sitting at the 12-month low of 4.70% and zero Sahm gap (3m avg equals 12m low). The 3m change of -0.20pp reflects the earlier drop from 4.90% to 4.70%, but the last three months show no further movement. Policy language reinforces this: the newer FOMC statement explicitly notes "the unemployment rate has declined" and labor market "continued to strengthen," consistent with the improvement baked into the 3-6-12m changes, but current level data shows stabilization rather than continued loosening or tightening. With the rate parked exactly at its 12-month low and distance from natural rate (0.70pp) implying a still-tight labor market, the most likely near-term outcome is continuation at this level rather than a fresh directional move. There's no evidence of acceleration in either direction — just a plateau after a prior tightening trend.
Would change its mind if: A print moving away from 4.70% in either direction — above 4.80% signaling loosening, or a fresh low below 4.70% signaling renewed tightening.
Leaned on: sahm_gap, unrate_change_3m, unrate_range_position

=== term_premium ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.25
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior call (down, 0.35) was roughly right in direction but modest: the 10y last printed 2.45, down from a recent 2.53 peak, and 21d change is now -0.04pp, confirming the near-term pullback rather than a resumption of the uptrend. That said, the broader picture is still one of an elevated long end: 63d change is +0.60pp and 252d change is +0.45pp, and the yield sits at 0.88 of its 12-month range — near the top of the year's range, well above the 12m low. The 10s30s slope at +0.60pp is steep, consistent with continued term-premium pressure from duration supply/issuance rather than a durable rally. Policy language shows market-based inflation compensation has moved up "considerably" (upgraded from "moved up"), which typically coincides with steeper curves and higher term premium, working against a sustained decline. Given the tension between the very recent small pullback (-0.04pp over 21d) and the strong medium-term uptrend plus still-high range position and steep 30s10s slope, I lean toward a shallow continuation of the recent dip rather than a fresh rally, but conviction is limited since the recent momentum has genuinely flattened.
Would change its mind if: A rebound back above ~2.55% with renewed positive 21d momentum would signal the pullback was noise and the uptrend has resumed.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_change_252d, dgs10_range_position, slope_30s10s

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: up, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last observation shows fed_assets jumping to 4,468,702 from 4,454,676, a one-week rise of ~$14bn, and the 4-week change is now clearly positive at +15,864M versus a still-modest 13w change of only +2,920M. The 13-week average sits near 4,456,857M, well below the latest print, meaning the recent move is an acceleration relative to trend rather than noise within it. My prior "flat" call was reasonable given the choppy, near-zero 13w change, but the newest data point breaks that pattern — assets are now up meaningfully in the most recent weeks and sitting only ~31,027M below the 52-week high, having recovered noticeably from the 52w change of -21,094M. Policy language is unchanged: it describes reinvestment of principal and Treasury rollover at auction, consistent with maintaining sizable holdings, not with an announced runoff cap or new caps — so nothing in the "why" explains a directional shift, but the language doesn't preclude modest reserve-driven fluctuations either. Given the clear recent uptick in the last 1-2 prints after months of flat/choppy readings, I lean toward calling the trend up rather than flat, though conviction is limited since this could just be a temporary technical bump (e.g., bill settlement effects) rather than a genuine policy-driven expansion.
Would change its mind if: A subsequent weekly print reversing back toward or below the 13w average (~4,456,857M) would confirm this was noise, not a new expansion trend.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_13w_avg, assets_below_52w_high

=== curve_slope ===
Call: down, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "down" call was correct: slope_2s10s continued falling from 1.21 toward 1.14, the most recent print, confirming the flattening trend I flagged. The 21d (-0.13pp) and 63d (-0.10pp) changes are both negative and the most recent three readings (1.19, 1.16, 1.14) show accelerating flattening rather than stabilization. The 252d change is still positive (+0.15pp), so the curve is not inverted and remains well off its 12m low (0.38pp above it, range position 0.66), but the trend within the last month is clearly down, not sideways. The FOMC statement language is steady/hawkish-leaning (continued labor strengthening, gradual rate increases), consistent with continued front-end pressure that compresses the 2s10s spread as short rates rise faster than long rates. There's no sign of the steepening reversal I said would change my mind (no break back above 1.30, no positive 21d change) — instead the opposite continues. Given the persistent negative short and medium-term momentum and no offsetting signal, I expect the flattening to continue into month-end, though the pace could moderate given the spread is still mid-range historically rather than at an extreme.
Would change its mind if: A reversal with 21d change turning positive and slope_2s10s climbing back above 1.20 would indicate flattening has stalled or reversed.

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: down, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The NFCI series continues its slow grind lower, now at -0.50, a fresh 52-week low, from -0.44 thirteen weeks ago. The 4-week change (-0.01) shows momentum has decelerated sharply versus the 13-week change (-0.07), with the last several readings essentially flat-lining at -0.49/-0.50. This confirms my prior "down" call was directionally correct: conditions eased further and did not rebound above the 13w average, so the falsifier condition was not triggered. However, the pace of easing has clearly stalled — the last three observations are nearly identical, suggesting the index is consolidating at this low level rather than continuing to fall briskly. Distance from zero remains substantial (-0.50), so conditions are still much looser than historical average, and policy language continues to reinforce an accommodative stance with no hint of tightening. Given the flattening momentum but still-negative trajectory and no reversal signal, I lean toward continued mild easing or flat conditions next month, but with reduced conviction versus before since the trend is losing steam and could plateau at this floor.
Would change its mind if: A rise back above -0.45 sustained for two consecutive weeks would signal momentum has reversed toward tightening.

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.

My prior "flat" call holds up reasonably: breakevens have oscillated in a tight 2.00-2.06 band for the last several readings without a sustained break either way. The 21-day change is slightly negative (-0.06pp) while 63-day and 252-day changes remain positive, indicating the broader uptrend from a year ago has stalled and is now consolidating rather than extending. Range position at 0.92 shows breakevens are still near the top of their 12-month range, but the momentum indicators (21d) show the impulse has faded.
  
  The policy language shift is notable: the removal of "moved up considerably" and reversion to simply "remain low" suggests the Committee no longer sees material upward momentum in market-based inflation compensation — a dovish-on-momentum signal that corroborates the flattening in the data. This is a real change in characterization, not just data noise, and it argues against expecting a fresh leg higher near-term.
  
  Combining the plateauing price action with the softer characterization in the statement, I lean toward flat with a slight downside tilt, since the language downgrade could presage further consolidation or mild pullback rather than continuation of the uptrend.

The latest monthly print (0.16%) decelerated sharply from the prior 0.40%, and is below both the 3m average (0.27%) and the outgoing print — this is exactly the kind of moderation my falsifier flagged, though core PCE did not stay flat; it ticked up to 1.86% from 1.77%. Core PCE momentum (3m annualized 1.72%) remains subdued and below headline's 3m annualized pace (3.31%), consistent with base effects from energy/import prices driving the recent headline acceleration rather than a broadening of persistent price pressure. With core still 0.14pp below target and its trend gently upward but not accelerating, I read this as: the y/y print will likely keep drifting up next month due to base effects already in the pipeline (12m low of 0.87% rolling out), but the underlying monthly momentum is cooling. Net: modest continued upward drift in headline y/y is still the more probable outcome, but conviction is lower given the deceleration in the freshest monthly data.

This is exactly the scenario I flagged as a trigger for revising off "flat": a fresh low below 4.70% signaling renewed tightening. My prior flat call was too cautious given the data now shows continued momentum lower rather than stabilization. The 3m change (-0.10pp) and 6m/12m changes (-0.30pp) confirm a steady, if modest, downward trend in unemployment, not a plateau. Sahm gap remains negligible (+0.07pp), so there's no recessionary acceleration signal — this is gradual tightening, not a sharp move. Distance from natural rate (0.60pp below 4%... wait, actually the level is at 4.60%, still 0.6pp above the 4% natural rate anchor per the data) suggests some slack remains, but the direction is clearly toward tighter conditions. Policy language reinforces this: the updated FOMC statement notes the unemployment rate "stayed near its recent low," consistent with continued labor market strengthening rather than a reversal. Given one month of a fresh low after three flat months, I'd lean toward continued flatness or a further modest decline, but I don't have enough evidence yet of accelerating momentum to call a strong "down" with high conviction — three months of a repeated 4.70% plateau before this print suggests some flattening is still plausible.
Would change its mind if: A print back above 4.70% would reverse the fresh-low signal and support a flat or loosening call instead.

Net: recent momentum is modestly down, but the level remains high in its range and last 63 days show no net movement, arguing against a confident directional call. I lean slightly toward continued softness/flat given the negative 21d momentum, but without stronger evidence I won't push conviction higher.
