user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: up, conviction 0.40
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last print (4,469,618) fell from the prior week's 4,478,236 but remains well above the 13w average (~4,460,615) and above levels from a month ago. My prior "up" call was directionally correct: the 4w change is +11,600M and 13w change is +18,167M, both positive, confirming a modest expansion trend rather than noise. The latest single-week dip is a pullback within that uptrend, not a reversal below the 13w average as I said would falsify it — it's still ~9,000M above that average. The 52w change is still negative (-13,222M) and assets sit ~30,111M below the 52w high, so this is a mild, uneven grind higher off a lower base, not aggressive QE. Policy language is unchanged — reinvestment/rollover language dating to a runoff-transition era, giving no explicit signal of a new QE program. Given the choppy weekly prints (up big, then down, then up again) but a clearly positive multi-week trend, I maintain a modest "up" call, slightly more confident than last time since the 4w/13w trend held up against the reversal test I set.
Would change its mind if: A subsequent weekly print falling back to or below the 13w average (~4,460,615M) would confirm this uptick was noise, not sustained expansion.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_pct_change_13w, assets_13w_avg, assets_change_52w, assets_below_52w_high

=== curve_slope ===
Call: down, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "down" call is playing out modestly: slope_2s10s ticked from 1.14 to 1.13 over the last print, and the 21d (-0.04pp) and 63d (-0.14pp) changes both still show net flattening, though the pace has clearly slowed versus the sharper 63d drift. The series has been choppy in a narrow band (1.11-1.17) for the last ten readings with no clean trend — the latest three prints (1.13, 1.14, 1.13) look like consolidation rather than a fresh leg down. The spread sits at 1.13pp, well within its 12-month range (position 0.64, i.e. above the midpoint, 0.37pp above the 12m low of 0.76 and 0.21pp below the 12m high of 1.34), so this is not a curve near inversion — it's a moderately positive, historically unremarkable slope that has drifted down slightly from a steeper past year. The Fed statement shows a hike delivered with continued "gradual" tightening language and a dissent for holding rates — a hawkish-leaning front-end move that typically compresses 2s10s modestly, consistent with the small realized flattening. No signal of aggressive acceleration in hikes that would drive a sharp inversion move by month-end. I lean toward continued mild flattening but with low conviction given the choppiness and slowing 21d pace.
Would change its mind if: 21d change turning clearly positive and slope_2s10s breaking back above 1.20 would signal flattening has stalled or reversed to steepening.
Leaned on: slope_2s10s, slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d, slope_change_252d, slope_range_position, slope_12m_low, slope_12m_high

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.25
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The NFCI series has been essentially flat and deeply negative, sitting near -0.48 to -0.50 for the past 13 weeks, well below the zero average (loose conditions). My prior "down" call was wrong in direction: the index bottomed around -0.50 and has ticked up slightly over the last four observations (-0.49, -0.49, -0.49, -0.49, -0.48), giving a +0.01 four-week change, though the 13-week change is still slightly negative (-0.02). The range position (0.07 of 52-week range) shows conditions are still near the loose end of the historical band, but the most recent print (-0.48) is the least negative in the series, suggesting a small easing-off-the-bottom / mild tightening tick rather than continued loosening. This is a very small move within a tight band (range width just 0.15), and policy language remains unchanged, reinforcing continued accommodative conditions with no new tightening signal. Given the tiny magnitude of the recent uptick and the overall flatness, I don't see strong momentum either way — the last few weeks show conditions stabilizing near cycle lows with a marginal tick toward tighter, but not enough to call a clear reversal.
Would change its mind if: A sustained move above -0.45 for two consecutive weeks, or a clear multi-week uptrend, would confirm a genuine tightening reversal rather than noise.
Leaned on: nfci, nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_range_position, nfci_52w_low

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last ten readings hover in a tight 1.95–2.00% band, ending at 1.97 after a small bounce from 1.95. The 21-day change is -0.04pp (mild softening), but 63-day is +0.03pp and 252-day is +0.32pp, showing the broader trend over the past year has been upward even as the most recent month has stalled. Breakeven sits just 0.03pp below the 2% objective and 0.85 of the way up its 12-month range, consistent with expectations that are anchored close to target rather than drifting away in either direction. My prior flat call was essentially correct: the series has stayed range-bound (1.95-1.98) since then, with no sustained break above 2.06 that would have signaled fresh upside momentum. Policy language reinforces this: "market-based measures of inflation compensation remain low" is unchanged, and the survey-based language dropped the softening qualifier "most," suggesting slightly firmer anchoring of longer-run expectations rather than a directional shift. With inflation compensation still characterized as "low" by the Committee and month-to-month moves under 0.02-0.03pp, there's no clear catalyst for a directional break over the next month. I maintain flat, though with the mild recent tick down giving a very slight tilt to acknowledge in conviction sizing.
Would change its mind if: A clear break above 2.02 or below 1.93 sustained for two+ readings, or policy language re-flagging a directional move in inflation compensation.
Leaned on: breakeven_10y, be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_change_252d, be_distance_from_target, be_range_position

=== inflation ===
Call: down, conviction 0.35
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 16 days ago.
Report:
  The previous "up" call was directionally correct through most of the run (headline yoy rose from ~2.05 to 2.81) but the newest print reverses that: headline_cpi_yoy fell from 2.81 to 2.44, and the newest mom reading is -0.05%, the weakest/only negative print in the whole series, following a modest +0.16%. That's two consecutive soft mom prints (0.16 then -0.05), pulling the 3m annualized headline pace down to 2.09% versus the outgoing single-month pace of 0.38%. Core PCE yoy, my preferred gauge for persistence, is still inching up (1.86 → 1.87), but the increment has shrunk to +0.01pp versus prior +0.09pp gains — momentum in core is clearly flattening, not accelerating. Core sits 0.13pp below target, still-below-2% per policy language, consistent with "little changed" characterization of core. Headline's drop is likely energy/food driven given the mom collapse, but the magnitude (2.81→2.44, -0.37pp) is too large to ignore for the next print unless there's a rebound. Given the newest mom is negative and the recent mom trend is decelerating (mom_gap_vs_outgoing -0.21pp), I expect headline yoy to be flat-to-down at the next release rather than resuming its climb, though core's persistence keeps me from a strong down call.
Would change its mind if: A rebound in headline mom back above 0.25% alongside continued core PCE yoy gains would signal renewed acceleration and reverse this call.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, headline_mom, headline_3m_annualized, core_pce_yoy, mom_gap_vs_outgoing, core_yoy_change_3m

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: down, conviction 0.40
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 23 days ago.
Report:
  The unemployment rate printed 4.40%, a fresh 12-month low, down from 4.60% and continuing a steady downtrend (-0.30pp over 3m, -0.60pp over 6m and 12m). My prior call for a further decline was correct: the series has moved from 4.70% to 4.60% to 4.40% over the last three prints, showing accelerating tightening rather than stabilization. The unrate_range_position of 0.00 confirms we are sitting at the low of the 12-month range, and the Sahm-gap remains modest and positive (0.17pp), indicating no recessionary loosening signal. Distance from the assumed natural rate (4.0%) is now only 0.40pp, meaning the market is quite tight and has limited room to fall further before hitting typical full-employment levels. Policy language reinforces this: the Fed characterizes the labor market as having "continued to strengthen" with job gains "solid" and unemployment "near its recent low," consistent with continued tightening rather than reversal. Given the persistent downward momentum, lack of any stabilization signal, and no evidence of a base-forming pattern, I lean toward another slight decline or at worst a flat print, but the room for further declines is narrowing given proximity to the natural rate, tempering conviction on more downside.
Would change its mind if: A print back above 4.60% would break the downtrend and suggest stabilization or loosening instead of continued tightening.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_6m, unrate_12m_low, unrate_range_position, unrate_distance_from_natural, sahm_gap

=== term_premium ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.25
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 10y sits at 2.40, essentially unchanged from last release and consistent with the prior "flat" call, which held. The last 10 prints show a tight, rangebound drift (2.47→2.40) with 21d and 63d changes both -0.09pp, indicating stalled momentum rather than a fresh trend. The 252d change (+0.57pp) shows the level is still well above where it was a year ago, and range position (0.82) confirms yields remain in the upper part of the 12-month band, but the recent flattening of momentum suggests that upward push has paused rather than reversed. Slope_30s10s (+0.62) is unremarkable and doesn't signal a fresh long-end selloff or bid. Policy language changes are trivial wording only, not a substantive shift in balance-sheet stance—reinvestment/rollover language is unchanged, so no new issuance/runoff impulse to lift the long end. With no clear catalyst either direction and yields oscillating in a narrow ~4bp-per-day band, I see no strong case for a directional move by month-end. I'll maintain a flat call, slightly lower conviction than before is not warranted since nothing has changed to sharpen the picture — the range-bound pattern continues to look like consolidation near cycle highs rather than the start of a new leg.
Would change its mind if: A break above ~2.50% with renewed positive 21d momentum, or a drop below ~2.30% with negative momentum, would signal the range has resolved.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_range_position, slope_30s10s

arbitrated.notes: Direct read comes from the curve_slope analyst itself: 2s10s has drifted from ~1.17 to 1.13 over 63d, with the most recent prints (1.13,1.14,1.13) looking like consolidation but still net flatter, and the Fed's hawkish "gradual" tightening language with a dissent for holding rates supports continued modest front-end-led compression. Inflation is soft (headline yoy reversing lower, core PCE flattening) which argues against front-end pressure from an inflation-scare angle, and actually could let the long end ease too — but since a plain deceleration in inflation with a still-tightening labor market is a mixed signal, the front end (policy-sensitive) is more anchored to the labor/Fed-language signal than the long end is to the inflation read. Term premium reads flat/rangebound with no catalyst, and financial conditions are flat/marginally tighter — neither offers a strong long-end differential move, so I don't override the long end lower or higher independently. Balance sheet's modest "up" (mild reserve/asset growth) is a slow-moving, back-end-neutral input; not enough by itself to argue for long-end richening (which would steepen) against the front-end tightening pull. Netting these, I retain the curve_slope analyst's own flattening call, reinforced by tightening labor market dynamics feeding hawkish front-end pressure, with the long end anchored by flat term premium — a modest flattening bias, tempered by the choppy/rangebound nature of the recent prints.

why.term_premium: Flat, rangebound 10y with no momentum or catalyst; no independent long-end pull either way.
why.balance_sheet: Modest 'up' expansion trend per that analyst; slow-moving, not a strong long-end differential driver here.

user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.25
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The latest print (4,470,142) fell back sharply from the recent peak (4,484,492), dropping below the prior week and landing right at the 13w average (~4,467,486) — the exact falsifier condition I set previously. This confirms the prior uptick was noisy week-to-week volatility around reinvestment-driven maintenance of holdings, not a sustained expansion trend. The policy language is unchanged: the Committee is reinvesting principal payments and rolling over maturing Treasuries to keep holdings at "sizable levels" — this is a balance-sheet-maintenance stance (neither active QE purchases nor an announced runoff cap), so week-to-week wobbles of a few $B are expected noise from auction settlement timing and MBS prepayment reinvestment lags, not directional signal.
  
  Looking at the medium-term measures: assets_change_13w is only +17,304M (+0.39%) and assets_change_52w is actually negative (-4,523M), meaning holdings are essentially flat to very slightly down over the past year despite this recent wiggle. The 4w change is small and positive but the latest single observation reversed hard. My prior call of "up" was wrong — the bounce was not sustained. Given flat policy language and a year-over-year figure near zero, I now see the balance sheet as flat/directionless, with a slight downward tilt given the negative 52w change and this week's pullback.
Would change its mind if: A sustained multi-week rise back above the 52w high (4,484,492) with 13w change accelerating would revive an expansion call; further drift below the 13w average confirms mild runoff.
Leaned on: fed_assets, assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_change_52w, assets_13w_avg

=== curve_slope ===
Call: down, conviction 0.45
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last print in the series (1.01) confirms the flattening call from last time: the previous view (down, 0.35) was directionally correct, as the spread fell from a recent local peak of 1.06 down to 1.01, the lowest reading in the 10-point window. The 21d (-0.12) and 63d (-0.26) changes are both clearly negative, consistent and not offsetting, while the 252d change is only mildly negative (-0.07), suggesting the flattening has been concentrated in the recent quarter rather than a multi-year grind. The spread sits at 0.43 of its 12-month range — mid-range, not near the low (0.76) or high (1.34) — so there's room to flatten further before hitting range extremes, and it's not yet signaling exhaustion of the move. The Fed statement shows a hike (to 3/4-1%) with continued "gradual increases" guidance, which is the kind of front-end-anchoring action that typically compresses 2s10s if long-end yields don't move proportionally — consistent with continued flattening pressure. The 10s30s slope at 0.67 remains a healthy positive, but that's context, not my driver. Given falling 21d/63d momentum and a hike delivered, I lean toward continued modest flattening, though the level (1.01) is not yet distant from its recent range midpoint, tempering conviction.
Would change its mind if: 21d change turning clearly positive and slope_2s10s breaking back above 1.10-1.15 would signal flattening has stalled.
Leaned on: slope_2s10s, slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d, slope_change_252d, slope_range_position, slope_12m_high, slope_12m_low

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.20
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The NFCI series remains pinned in an extremely narrow band, oscillating between -0.48 and -0.50 for the last 13 readings with no discernible trend. The most recent print (-0.50) sits at the 52-week low and matches the tightest end of the observed range, confirming conditions are at their loosest relative to the recent window, not tightening. The 4-week change (-0.02) and 13-week change (-0.01) both point marginally toward easing, not tightening, and the range_position of 0.00 confirms we're sitting right at the low end of the 52-week range. My prior flat call was correct in that no reversal materialized — the falsifier (sustained move above -0.45) was not met; if anything the index ticked slightly lower. Distance from zero (-0.50) shows conditions remain substantially looser than historical average, consistent with accommodative policy language reiterated unchanged. Given the tiny magnitude of movement (hundredths of an index point) relative to typical noise in this series, this is best characterized as flat with a very slight easing lean. No momentum or breakout signal exists to suggest a shift in either direction over the next month.
Would change its mind if: A sustained move above -0.45 for two consecutive weeks, or a clear multi-week uptrend, would confirm a genuine tightening reversal rather than noise.
Leaned on: nfci, nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_distance_from_zero, nfci_range_position, nfci_52w_low

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: up, conviction 0.45
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last 10 readings show breakeven_10y rising from 1.88 to 1.92, with the most recent prints (1.87→1.91→1.90→1.91→1.92) forming a clear uptrend after a dip to 1.84. My prior "flat" call was too cautious in hindsight: the 21d and 63d changes are still negative (-0.06, -0.16 pp), reflecting the earlier decline, but the very recent trajectory (last 4-5 observations) is steadily higher, and be_change_252d is solidly positive (+0.25 pp), confirming the medium-term direction is up. The series is now at be_range_position 0.78, near the top of its 12-month range, and above its 12m low by 0.58 pp — a meaningful recovery. Distance from the 2% target remains modest at -0.08 pp, so there's little slack before expectations approach the Committee's objective, but the trend is clearly toward closing that gap rather than widening it. Policy language is essentially unchanged in substance (both versions say market-based measures "remain low" and survey measures "little changed"), so this isn't a case of a hawkish or dovish repricing driven by rhetoric — it's a data-driven grind higher in market pricing. Given the persistent short-term upward momentum and closing distance to target, I lean toward a continued modest rise rather than flat.
Would change its mind if: A reversal back below 1.88 sustained for two+ readings, or policy language flagging a renewed decline in inflation compensation.
Leaned on: be_change_252d, be_range_position, be_above_12m_low, be_distance_from_target

=== inflation ===
Call: down, conviction 0.40
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 15 days ago.
Report:
  The prior "down" call was correct: headline CPI y/y fell from 2.44 to 2.18, a -0.26pp drop, driven by outgoing_mom of just 0.12% versus the prior 0.40% and -0.05%. That confirms the deceleration I flagged. Momentum indicators now reinforce this: headline_3m_annualized is just 0.95%, well below the current y/y rate, and headline_mom_3m_avg is only 0.08%, meaning the recent print run-rate is far softer than what's rolling out of the y/y window a year ago. This strongly suggests further y/y softening is more likely than a rebound, since base effects from the rapid early prints (0.38, 0.24, 0.28) are still working out while incoming prints stay soft. Core PCE also ticked down to 1.68 from 1.87, a real deceleration and still comfortably below the 2% objective (core_distance_from_target -0.32pp), reducing concern about underlying persistence. Policy language shift acknowledging inflation "moving close to" 2% reflects the earlier acceleration phase that has since faded per the newest print. Weighting core as the persistence signal, and noting headline's own momentum has stalled, I lean toward continued softening in next month's headline y/y, though the range position (0.67) shows we're not near the cycle low, tempering conviction.
Would change its mind if: A headline mom print above 0.30% combined with core PCE yoy rebounding above 1.85% would signal renewed acceleration and reverse this call.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, headline_mom, headline_3m_annualized, headline_mom_3m_avg, core_pce_yoy, core_yoy_change_3m, core_distance_from_target

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.55
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 22 days ago.
Report:
  The unemployment rate held at 4.40% for a second consecutive month, following a decline from 4.60% the month before. My prior call for further declines (down) did not materialize this month — the rate was flat rather than continuing its fall, though it did not reverse either. The 3m change (-0.30pp), 6m change (-0.50pp) and 12m change (-0.70pp) all confirm a clear tightening trend over the past year, but the trend has now plateaued at the 12-month low with sahm_gap near zero (+0.07pp) and unrate_range_position at 0 (sitting at the low end of the 12-month range). Distance from the natural rate (~4.0%) is only 0.40pp, indicating the labor market is already quite tight by historical standards, leaving less room for further compression. Policy language corroborates this: the Fed shifted language from "unemployment rate stayed near its recent low" to "little changed in recent months," explicitly signaling stabilization rather than continued strengthening. Given the flattening in the data and the explicit "little changed" language, I now see the most likely outcome as a flat print, with only a modest chance of a slight new low given the tight labor market conditions and limited further room to tighten.
Would change its mind if: A print above 4.50% would suggest the plateau is breaking into actual loosening; a fresh new low below 4.40% would revive the tightening trend.

=== term_premium ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.20
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "flat" call was roughly right but the resolution I flagged (break below ~2.30%) has now happened - the yield dropped to 2.18-2.24% before bouncing modestly to 2.29 in the last two prints. Over 21d and 63d the level is down 10-20bp, while the 252d change still shows +35bp, so the longer trend remains upward but the recent momentum has clearly turned negative/consolidating. Range position at 0.74 still sits in the upper half of the 12-month band (1.37-2.62%), so this is not a breakout to new lows, just a pullback within an elevated range. The last three prints (2.28, 2.35, 2.32, 2.30, 2.29) show a mild reversal higher off the recent low of 2.18, suggesting stabilization rather than a fresh leg down. Slope_30s10s at 0.67pp is unremarkable and doesn't signal a strong term-premium impulse either way. Balance-sheet reinvestment policy is unchanged, so no fresh issuance/runoff pressure from that angle. Given the mixed signal - short-term momentum down but bouncing, longer-term trend still up, no breakout - I lean toward continued rangebound behavior with a slight downward bias given the recent lower prints, but conviction is low.
Would change its mind if: A sustained move above 2.40% with positive 21d momentum, or a break below 2.20% with negative momentum, would resolve the current consolidation.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_change_252d, dgs10_range_position, slope_30s10s

=== What the panel says it was never handed ===
  term_premium lacks financial_conditions — Broader financial conditions tightening/easing could explain flight-to-quality bid or issuance pressure driving the recent pullback.
  term_premium lacks balance_sheet — Any change in pace of Treasury runoff/reinvestment would clarify supply pressure on the long end.

user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last 13 weeks show fed_assets oscillating in a tight band (4,467,108 to 4,484,492) with no clear trend. The most recent print (4,470,852) is up modestly from the prior week (4,467,108), giving a positive 4w change (+710M), and the 13w change is also slightly positive (+2150M, +0.05%). This is essentially noise relative to the roughly $4.47T base — far smaller than a typical announced monthly runoff cap would produce if QT were active at pace. My prior "flat" call was correct: the series has continued to chop sideways around the 13w average without sustained directional movement in either direction. The policy language is unchanged and describes a reinvestment/rollover policy consistent with holdings being kept "at sizable levels" — language that doesn't describe an aggressive runoff program, and the data confirms holdings have been stable to very mildly higher over the past quarter, still below the 52w high by about $13.6B. There's no acceleration in either direction. Given the tiny magnitude of changes relative to the base, I read this as flat, with a very slight tilt toward up given the last two data points ticked higher, but not enough to call sustained expansion.
Would change its mind if: A sustained multi-week move beyond +/-0.3% in 13w change, or a clear break above the 52w high or below the 13w average trend, would revise this view.
Leaned on: fed_assets, assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_pct_change_13w, assets_13w_avg, assets_below_52w_high

=== curve_slope ===
Call: down, conviction 0.40
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "down" call played out: slope_2s10s fell further from 0.95 to 0.93 over the last two observations, and the 21d/63d changes are both negative (-0.12pp, -0.24pp), confirming the flattening trend continued rather than stalling. My falsifier (21d turning positive, spread breaking above 1.10-1.15) was not met — the opposite occurred. The spread sits at 0.93pp, only 0.17pp above its 12-month low and in the bottom 29% of its 12-month range (range_position 0.29), indicating flattening bias remains intact. The 252d change is nearly flat (-0.03pp), suggesting the multi-month flattening is a relatively recent/recent-quarter phenomenon rather than a multi-year grind, but the last two prints (0.93, 0.93) show the trend has plateaued slightly rather than accelerating further. The Fed's continued gradual-hike stance (raising the funds rate in the changed language, with further gradual increases anticipated) supports front-end pressure that typically flattens 2s10s as short rates rise faster than long rates, consistent with dgs2 rising more than dgs10 in percentage terms. Given the plateau in the last two readings alongside a still-negative 21d/63d trend, I lean toward continued modest flattening or flat, not a reversal.
Would change its mind if: 21d change turning clearly positive and slope_2s10s breaking back above 1.05-1.10 would signal flattening has reversed.
Leaned on: slope_2s10s, slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d, slope_range_position, slope_above_12m_low

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: down, conviction 0.45
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The NFCI series has continued to grind lower, from -0.49 to -0.54 over the last 13 observations, with the most recent four weeks showing a clear accelerating decline (-0.04 over 4 weeks) and the index now sitting at its 52-week low with range position at 0.00. My prior "flat" call was too cautious in hindsight — the drift lower that was present at the last release has persisted and even modestly accelerated rather than stabilizing. There is no sign of the reversal condition I specified (a sustained move above -0.45); instead conditions have moved further away from that threshold, deeper into easy territory. Policy language remains unchanged and consistent with maintaining accommodative conditions, offering no contradicting signal. The index remains well below zero (-0.54 vs. long-run average of 0), indicating financial conditions are looser than average and the trend is still toward easing, not tightening. Momentum is modest in absolute magnitude (a few hundredths per month) but consistent in direction over both the 4-week and 13-week windows, with the most recent three prints (-0.52, -0.53, -0.54) showing steady incremental easing. I'll revise to a down (easing further) call for next month-end, with moderate conviction given the small but consistent magnitude of the moves.
Would change its mind if: A flattening or reversal in weekly prints back toward -0.50 or higher over two consecutive weeks would signal the easing trend has stalled or reversed.
Leaned on: nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_52w_low, nfci_range_position, nfci_distance_from_zero

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.25
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The previous call (up, 0.45) has been contradicted by the data: breakeven_10y has stabilized at 1.81 for the last three readings after peaking at 1.83, and the 21d/63d changes are both negative (-0.11pp, -0.22pp). That's a clear reversal from the momentum that justified the prior up-call, so I was wrong to expect continued increases. The longer 252d change (+0.23pp) shows the broader trend off the 12m low is still up, and range_position at 0.64 confirms breakevens sit well above the 12m low (1.34) and in the upper-middle of the 12m range, not at a low extreme. But the very recent trend is flat-to-slightly-down, with the series pinned at 1.81 for three consecutive prints — no fresh momentum in either direction. Policy language is unchanged and explicitly describes market-based inflation compensation as "remaining low," which is consistent with sideways stabilization rather than a fresh leg down. Distance from the 2% target remains meaningfully negative (-0.19pp), but that gap has been roughly stable, not widening. Given the flattening pattern and lack of new catalysts, I see no strong directional edge for the coming month — the recent pullback appears to have stalled rather than accelerated.
Would change its mind if: A break below 1.78 or above 1.85 sustained for two+ readings would confirm a new direction; policy language shift on inflation compensation would also change this view.
Leaned on: be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_change_252d, be_distance_from_target, be_range_position

=== inflation ===
Call: down, conviction 0.50
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 16 days ago.
Report:
  The previous "down" call was correct: headline CPI yoy fell from 2.18 to 1.86 (-0.32pp), driven by a weak -0.08% mom print, well below the falsifier threshold of 0.30%. Momentum remains soft — headline 3m annualized is essentially 0%, and headline yoy has now declined 0.95pp over three months, moving off its cycle high of 2.81 toward the middle of the 12-month range. Core PCE also decelerated, falling from 1.87 to 1.68 over the last two prints, with 3m annualized core running at 1.37% — well under 2% and consistent with the policy language's description of "little changed" and "somewhat below 2 percent" core inflation. Core sits 0.36pp below the 2% target and is losing momentum (core_yoy_change_3m -0.23pp), while core-minus-headline is slightly negative, meaning headline's recent strength was partly food/energy driven and is now fading. Both measures point the same direction: deceleration, off a modest post-trough peak. The mom sequence (0.40, 0.16, -0.05, 0.12, -0.08) shows no signs of reacceleration. I weight core PCE for persistence, but headline's sharp yoy drop reinforces rather than contradicts this. I expect the next headline print to continue drifting down or hold near current lows.
Would change its mind if: A headline mom print above 0.30% combined with core PCE yoy rebounding above 1.85% would signal renewed acceleration and reverse this call.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, core_pce_yoy, headline_mom, headline_3m_annualized, core_3m_annualized, headline_yoy_change_3m, core_yoy_change_3m, core_distance_from_target

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.55
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 23 days ago.
Report:
  The latest print held at 4.40%, matching the trailing 12-month low, so the previous "flat" call was correct: the unemployment rate has plateaued at its cycle low for three consecutive readings (4.40/4.40/4.40) after a steady descent from 5.00% six months prior. The 3-month and 6-month changes (-0.20pp, -0.30pp) reflect that earlier decline but the most recent trend is flat, not falling further. Sahm gap sits at 0.00, confirming no acceleration in loosening. The level remains modestly below the assumed natural rate proxy (unrate_distance_from_natural +0.40pp, i.e., 4.40% vs ~4.0% natural), indicating a still-tight labor market, but with no further tightening momentum — the rate isn't printing new lows anymore. Policy language reinforces this: the Fed characterizes the labor market as having "continued to strengthen" with job gains "solid" and unemployment "declined" in the most recent statement, a shift in tone from "little changed" previously, suggesting officials view conditions as still tightening at the margin, though the hard data show a flat plateau rather than continued decline. Absent a fresh low or an uptick, I expect the rate to hold near 4.40% next month. I do not see evidence of an inflection toward loosening.
Would change its mind if: A print above 4.50% would indicate the plateau is breaking into loosening; a new low below 4.40% would revive the tightening trend.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_12m_low, sahm_gap, unrate_range_position, unrate_distance_from_natural

=== term_premium ===
Call: down, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 10Y sits at 2.21%, essentially flat over the last few prints (2.22→2.21) after a modest pullback from a 2.29 local peak. The prior "flat" call held: yields stayed in a tight consolidation band and did not break decisively above 2.40% or below 2.20%. Momentum signs are now mildly negative (21d -0.12pp, 63d -0.25pp) even though the 252d change remains positive (+0.38pp), indicating the multi-month uptrend has stalled and modestly reversed. Range position is 0.67, still in the upper half of the 12-month range, so this is a pause/retracement rather than a breakdown. The 30s10s slope (+0.66pp) is unremarkable and doesn't signal a fresh long-end selloff or flight-to-quality bid. Balance-sheet reinvestment policy language is unchanged, offering a steady, mildly supportive backdrop for longer-term securities (capping upside) rather than a new catalyst. With yield sitting just above the 2.20% low-momentum threshold I set previously, and no clear trigger evident, I lean toward continued near-term softness but without strong conviction — the recent negative short-term momentum modestly outweighs the still-elevated range position.
Would change its mind if: A break above 2.30% with positive 21d momentum, or a break below 2.15% with accelerating negative momentum, would resolve this consolidation and change my call.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_change_252d, dgs10_range_position, slope_30s10s

user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  Total assets have been essentially flat to modestly choppy: the last print (4,463,347) sits close to the 13w average (4,471,341) and within a narrow band of the past year's range, with 13w change of only -6,271 (-0.14%) and 52w change of -3,135 — both trivial relative to a ~$4.47T balance sheet. The 4w change is actually positive (+3,433), so there is no active runoff underway yet; reinvestment of maturing securities is still full, per the still-active "rolling over maturing Treasury securities... reinvesting principal payments" language. My prior flat call was correct — the series continues to oscillate in a tight band with no sustained directional break beyond my stated thresholds. The key new development is qualitative, not yet in the data: the Committee has newly signaled it expects to begin a balance-sheet normalization (runoff) program "this year," contingent on the economy evolving as anticipated. This is forward guidance, not an active policy change yet — the current reinvestment policy is still in place, so no shrinkage has begun. For the one-month-ahead horizon, I expect assets to remain essentially flat, since the announced program has not yet started and no cap or start date is confirmed. Conviction remains low.
Would change its mind if: A confirmed start date/cap for balance-sheet runoff, or a sustained break of 13w change beyond +/-0.3%, would flip this to a down call.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_pct_change_13w, assets_13w_avg, assets_change_52w

=== curve_slope ===
Call: up, conviction 0.40
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "down" call was wrong: the last two prints show the curve steepening, not flattening. Slope_2s10s rose from 0.88 to 0.93, extending a clear uptrend in the most recent observations (0.78→0.83→0.88→0.93). The 63-day change is still negative (-0.20pp), reflecting the flattening move earlier in the window, but the 21-day change has flattened to 0.00 and the most recent three prints show consistent steepening momentum that dominates the very short-term picture. The spread sits at 0.93pp, in the lower-middle of its 12-month range (range position 0.29, between 0.76 low and 1.34 high), so there's room to steepen further before hitting resistance. The Fed just hiked to 1-1/4% while maintaining reinvestment policy to keep long-end holdings "at sizable levels" — a front-end move that, mechanically, tends to compress 2s10s if long rates don't rise commensurately, yet the data show the spread widening, suggesting 10-year yields are outpacing 2-year moves for now. Given the recent three-observation steepening streak is the freshest signal, and the level remains well within historical range (not near extreme flatness), I'll shift toward a modest "up" call for month-end, though the 63-day trend was flattening, tempering conviction.
Would change its mind if: A reversal back below 0.85 with a negative 21d change would signal the steepening stalled and flattening resumed.
Leaned on: slope_change_63d, slope_change_21d, slope_range_position, slope_12m_low, slope_12m_high

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: down, conviction 0.50
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The NFCI series has continued its steady, gradual easing trend: index moved from -0.56 to -0.57 over the last two prints, extending the broader slide from -0.49 thirteen weeks ago to -0.57 now. The 4-week change (-0.02) and 13-week change (-0.09) both confirm sustained, if modest, further loosening rather than any stall or reversal. The index sits at its 52-week low with range_position at 0.00, meaning conditions are the loosest they've been in the observed window, with no sign of bouncing back toward -0.50. My prior call (down, 0.45) was correct — there was no flattening or reversal toward -0.50 as I flagged would falsify the view; instead the trend continued in the same direction, actually gaining a touch of consistency. Policy language reinforces this: the Fed's reference to keeping longer-term securities holdings sizable "to help maintain accommodative financial conditions" is consistent with, and supportive of, the easing bias observed in the index. Momentum is modest but directionally unambiguous, and distance from zero (-0.57) is deepening, not narrowing. I see no evidence of deceleration in the pace of easing sufficient to call flat.
Would change its mind if: Two consecutive weekly prints flattening or reversing back toward -0.50 or higher would signal the easing trend has stalled or reversed.
Leaned on: nfci, nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_distance_from_zero, nfci_range_position, nfci_52w_low

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.25
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last 10 breakeven prints (1.67→1.73) show a modest uptrend over the recent window, with the last few readings clustering 1.71-1.73 after rising from 1.66. The 21d and 63d changes are negative (-0.08, -0.24 pp), indicating a pullback from a local peak, but the 252d change is solidly positive (+0.36 pp), and the series sits at 0.49 of its 12-month range - squarely in the middle, not at an extreme. The very latest tick (1.72→1.73) suggests the short-term dip has stabilized and ticked back up. Breakevens remain well below the 2% objective (-0.27 pp distance), consistent with policy language noting market-based inflation compensation "remains low." My prior "flat" call was reasonable given the sideways action around 1.71-1.73, and the newest print confirms that range-bound behavior rather than a decisive breakout in either direction. There's no policy language shift specifically on inflation compensation to signal a change. Given the mixed short-term (down) vs medium-term (up) signals and the tight recent range (1.71-1.73 for last 4 readings), I still see this as fundamentally flat with a slight upward lean given the latest uptick and the longer-term trend.
Would change its mind if: A sustained break below 1.68 or above 1.78 for two+ readings, or explicit policy language shift on inflation compensation trend, would change this view.
Leaned on: breakeven_10y, be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_change_252d, be_range_position, be_distance_from_target

=== inflation ===
Call: down, conviction 0.55
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 15 days ago.
Report:
  The prior "down" call was correct: headline CPI yoy fell from 1.86 to 1.64, continuing the downtrend, and the trigger conditions I set for reversal (mom>0.30% and core PCE>1.85%) were not met — in fact the opposite occurred. Headline mom printed just 0.07% after a -0.08% reading, and core PCE yoy dropped further to 1.55%, its lowest in the window. Momentum measures confirm this: headline_3m_annualized is only 0.45%, core_3m_annualized just 0.98%, both well below the yoy levels, implying further near-term disinflationary pull-through. The 3m yoy change is -0.80pp for headline and -0.32pp for core, a sizable deceleration. Policy language corroborates this, newly acknowledging inflation "has declined recently" and is "running somewhat below 2 percent," with near-term expectations for it to stay below target before stabilizing later — a downgrade from the prior statement's more sanguine "close to objective" language. With core sitting 0.45pp below target and decelerating, and headline momentum near zero, the weight of evidence favors continued softness in the next print. I do not see the sharp core PCE rebound or high headline mom that would flip this call.
Would change its mind if: A headline mom print above 0.30% combined with core PCE yoy rebounding above 1.80% would signal renewed acceleration and reverse this call.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, core_pce_yoy, headline_3m_annualized, core_3m_annualized, headline_yoy_change_3m, core_yoy_change_3m, outgoing_mom, core_distance_from_target

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: down, conviction 0.45
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 22 days ago.
Report:
  The unemployment rate printed 4.30%, a new 12-month low, below the prior plateau of 4.40% that had held for three months. My previous "flat" call anticipated a plateau continuation, but the new low (down 0.10pp from the prior three readings) indicates the tightening trend actually resumed rather than stalling — I should have weighted the possibility of a fresh low more given the steady 3m/6m/12m downtrend (-0.10/-0.40/-0.60pp) was still intact even during the flat stretch. The rate is now 0.30pp below the ~4% natural-rate anchor's upper bound, sahm_gap is a negligible +0.07pp (no recession-signal loosening), and range_position sits at 0.00 (at the 12-month low). Policy language corroborates this: the updated FOMC statement explicitly states the unemployment rate "declined" (versus "little changed" previously), confirming the labor market continued to strengthen. With no evidence of loosening — no rise off lows, no Sahm-rule trigger, and confirmatory language — the market looks tight and still tightening modestly. I expect the rate to hold near or tick to a marginal new low rather than reverse upward, though the pace of prior declines has been gradual, so I don't expect a large move.
Would change its mind if: A print back above 4.40% would signal the tightening trend has stalled or reversed into loosening; a further drop below 4.30% would confirm continued tightening.
Leaned on: unrate_12m_low, unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_6m, unrate_change_12m, sahm_gap, unrate_range_position

=== term_premium ===
Call: up, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior call (down, 0.30) was wrong: yields have risen through my own stated falsifier, breaking above 2.30% (now 2.31) with clearly positive 21d momentum (+0.10pp), even though the 63d change was still negative. The last four prints (2.21, 2.22, 2.27, 2.31) show accelerating upward momentum, not consolidation. The 12m range position at 0.75 shows the yield sitting well into the upper quartile of its range, and the 252d change (+0.85pp) confirms a strong underlying uptrend rather than mean-reversion. Slope_30s10s (+0.53pp) and slope_10s2s (+0.93pp) are both positive and not flagged as flattening sharply, consistent with continued term-premium building rather than curve inversion pressure. On the policy side, the language shift removes the emphasis on "maintaining accommodative financial conditions" via reinvestment and instead introduces a program description for gradually reducing securities holdings (tapering reinvestment) — this is a hawkish/balance-sheet-shrinking signal that historically pushes term premium and long-end yields higher via reduced duration demand from the Fed. Combining the fresh technical breakout, upper-range positioning, and the shift toward balance-sheet normalization language, I now lean toward continued upward pressure on the 10-year yield into month-end.
Would change its mind if: A reversal back below 2.25% with renewed negative 21d momentum, or a sharp flattening of slope_30s10s, would undercut this upward call.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_252d, dgs10_range_position, dgs10_above_12m_low, slope_30s10s, slope_10s2s

  term_premium lacks balance_sheet — Explicit pace/timing of reinvestment taper would clarify magnitude of supply pressure on long end.
  term_premium lacks financial_conditions — Broader financial conditions tightening could corroborate or offset rising term premium.

The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.40
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last observation (4,465,284) sits roughly mid-range within the recent 13-week band and is essentially flat versus the prior print (4,476,903), continuing the choppy, directionless pattern seen for months. The 4w change is modestly positive (+1,937M), the 13w change modestly negative (-4,858M, -0.11%), and 52w change is near zero (+786M). None of these show a sustained trend in either direction — this looks like noise around reinvestment-driven full replacement, consistent with the standing policy of rolling over maturing Treasuries and reinvesting agency MBS principal. My prior flat call was correct: the balance sheet has not begun contracting.
  
  The policy language has shifted meaningfully in tone: from "this year" to "relatively soon" for beginning balance-sheet normalization, signaling QT is drawing closer but has not yet started — reinvestment is still explicitly being maintained "for the time being." This is forward guidance about a future change, not evidence of current runoff. Since actual measured assets remain flat and reinvestment policy is unchanged in practice, the balance sheet should stay flat through the next month-end, though the "relatively soon" language raises the probability of a start in the following months and modestly tilts risk toward eventual decline.
Would change its mind if: An announced start date/monthly cap for runoff, or a sustained break of 13w change beyond -0.3%, would flip this to a down call.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_pct_change_13w, assets_change_52w, fed_assets

=== curve_slope ===
Call: up, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 2s10s spread has stabilized at 0.96pp for the last three readings after rising from 0.88-0.90 earlier in the window. My prior "up" call was correct: the steepening continued and has now plateaued rather than reversed. The 21d change (+0.07) is positive but decelerating, and the 63d change is still negative (-0.09), showing the move is recent and not yet a strong trend. The 252d change (+0.17) confirms a modest steepening bias over the year. Slope sits at 0.34 of its 12-month range, still closer to the low end (0.76-1.34 range), meaning there's room to steepen further before hitting resistance, but the flat-lining of the last three prints (0.96, 0.96, 0.96) suggests momentum has paused. The policy language shift — from a hike to holding rates steady — removes near-term front-end upward pressure, which would typically support further steepening (or at least prevent flattening) since a pause reduces the case for 2yr yields rising faster than 10yr. This is a mild positive for continued steepening, but the plateau in recent prints tempers conviction. I lean toward continued modest steepening but acknowledge the momentum has stalled, so this is a lower-conviction call than before.
Would change its mind if: A drop back below 0.90 with negative 21d change would signal renewed flattening and invalidate this steepening lean.
Leaned on: slope_2s10s, slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d, slope_change_252d, slope_range_position

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The NFCI series continues its slow easing grind, now printing -0.58, at its 52-week low and range_position of 0.00. However, momentum has clearly decelerated: the 4-week change is just -0.01, far smaller than the -0.08 seen over 13 weeks, and the last three prints are flat at -0.58, -0.58, -0.58. This is a deceleration/stall pattern, not a reversal, but it does mean the easing trend that justified my prior "down" call has lost most of its momentum. Conditions remain well below the zero average (distance -0.58), so the level itself is unambiguously loose/easy, but the question here is direction over the next month, not level. With three consecutive unchanged readings, the most defensible call is that conditions are flattening out near this floor rather than continuing to loosen at the prior pace. There's no policy language input to suggest a fresh impulse either way. Labor tightness remains flat-to-firm (unemployment ticking up only marginally off cycle lows, still tight), which argues against a front-end selloff steepener and is consistent with continued flattening pressure from a still-tight labor backdrop keeping front-end yields anchored higher relative to a softening long end (soft core PCE, soft breakevens). Balance sheet is flat with only a language-hardening tilt — not yet actionable, and even if it matters it's a long-end supply variable that would argue for steepening (less absorption pressure), a minor counterweight but not enough to overturn the strong, fresh, direct momentum signal. I side with continued flattening.

discounted.why: No monthly cap/start date announced; still noise-dominated, too weak to move slope call.

arbitrated.falsifier: Spread rebounding above 0.85pp with positive 21d change, or a clear DGS10 breakout above 2.25% with steepening 10s30s, would flip this to steepening.

risks.text: Long-end drift is small and mid-range; a reversal in DGS10 could quickly stall the flattening thesis.
An explicit balance-sheet runoff announcement would add long-end supply pressure and could steepen the curve unexpectedly.
Flattening momentum is recent (21d/63d) while 252d change is still positive, so this could be a short-lived counter-trend move.
Crowded flattener positioning into month-end could amplify a sharp reversal if data surprises hawkish or dovish abruptly.

trade.rationale: Flattener: long front-end yield exposure, short long-end yield exposure, sized equal-opposite to isolate slope compression per curve_slope's fresh, accelerating flattening signal.

why.curve_slope: Direct spread measurement at 12-month low with confirmed accelerating negative momentum; primary signal, fresh and unambiguous.
why.term_premium: Long end (DGS10) drifting down modestly; supports bull-flattening mechanism but magnitude is small, mid-range.
why.labor_tightness: Labor market still tight (only marginal uptick off cycle low); keeps front-end yields anchored, reinforcing flattening bias.
why.balance_sheet: Flat, no actionable runoff trigger yet; language hardening is a minor steepening risk but not yet in the data.

Over the last 13 weeks the index has hovered tightly between -0.58 and -0.56, with the latest print at -0.57. The 4-week change is -0.01 and the 13-week change is +0.00, both negligible. Range position (0.06) confirms the index sits near the bottom of its 52-week range, far from the 52-week high of -0.35, meaning conditions are loose relative to the past year and there's no sign of tightening pressure building. Given the persistent, tiny oscillations within a 0.02-wide band, I see no basis to call a direction with confidence. The driver is going nowhere meaningful at this horizon — the level is stable, low, and directionless. I'll maintain a flat call, perhaps with slightly higher conviction since the pattern of near-constancy has now persisted for over three months.

Given the flat short-term price action and the absence of a policy-language shift, I maintain a flat call, though I tilt slightly toward modest upside given the positive longer-horizon momentum and position in the range.

=== inflation ===
Call: up, conviction 0.45
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 15 days ago.
Report:
  Last release's actual print confirmed reacceleration: headline m/m rose to 0.51% (from 0.38% prior), well above my 0.30% trigger, pushing headline yoy to 2.18% from 1.93% - a 0.25pp jump in a single month. My prior "flat" call was wrong; the two most recent m/m prints (0.38, 0.51) are the strongest in the 13-month window and headline_3m_annualized has climbed to 3.77%, well above target and above the 12-month range midpoint (yoy_range_position 0.46 but rising fast). However, core PCE continues to soften, now 1.43% yoy, a fresh cycle low, with core_yoy_change_3m at -0.11pp and core_3m_annualized just 1.27% - decelerating, not accelerating. This is a genuine headline/core divergence: headline is being pushed up by volatile components (energy/food likely, given the gap of -0.75pp core_minus_headline), while underlying persistent inflation (core) keeps drifting further below target. Policy language also now describes both overall and core inflation as "below 2 percent," a downgrade from prior language, consistent with the core softening. For next month, I weight the strong, accelerating momentum in headline (two consecutive strong m/m prints, rising 3m annualized) over the soft core trend, since the question is about headline_cpi_yoy specifically. Momentum favors another up move, though core divergence tempers conviction.
Would change its mind if: A headline m/m print below ~0.10% or core PCE yoy stabilizing/rebounding would signal the recent headline pop was transitory rather than a new uptrend.
Leaned on: headline_mom, headline_cpi_yoy, headline_3m_annualized, core_pce_yoy, core_3m_annualized, core_yoy_change_3m, core_minus_headline

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.45
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 22 days ago.
Report:
  The unemployment rate printed 4.30% in the latest observation, matching the 12-month low and reversing the prior tick up to 4.40%. My prior "flat" call was essentially correct: the 4.40% reading proved a one-month blip rather than a reversal, and the series has settled back at its low. Over 3 months the rate is unchanged (+0.00pp), over 6 months it's down modestly (-0.10pp), and over 12 months down solidly (-0.70pp). The Sahm-gap is negligible (+0.03pp), well below any recession-signal threshold, and range position is at 0 (sitting at the 12-month low). Distance from a 4% natural rate is only 0.30pp, indicating a labor market that is tight but not tightening further at pace — a plateau near cycle lows rather than active tightening or loosening. Policy language reinforces this: the FOMC statement shifted from "unemployment rate has declined" (past tense, describing prior improvement) to language describing continued strengthening without highlighting a fresh decline, consistent with a market that has stabilized rather than one still tightening. With no momentum in either direction and the rate sitting exactly at its low, I expect the next print to be flat, likely in the 4.30-4.40% band.
Would change its mind if: A print above 4.50% would confirm a genuine loosening reversal; a new low below 4.30% would confirm renewed tightening.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_12m_low, sahm_gap, unrate_range_position, unrate_distance_from_natural

The yield is now at 2.33%, sitting at 0.73 of its 12-month range (1.56-2.62%), a fairly elevated position rather than near the low end. The 63-day change is flat (+0.02pp), but the 21-day change shows clear recent upward momentum, and the 252-day change (+0.77pp) confirms a sustained uptrend over the past year. The 30s10s slope is +0.53pp, modestly positive but not showing dramatic steepening. Policy language confirms continued balance-sheet runoff (rolling over maturing Treasuries, reinvesting MBS paydowns into MBS) with no change toward more accommodation — this passive supply pressure is consistent with upward pressure on long-end yields. Inflation expectations are described as low and stable, which doesn't argue for a big term-premium repricing either way, but doesn't offset the recent upward momentum. Given the clear reversal in the last 21 days and the level sitting well above the 12-month low, I now lean toward continued modest upward drift in the term premium.
Would change its mind if: A pullback below ~2.28% with negative 21d momentum, or a flattening/inverting 30s10s slope, would flip this back toward flat/down.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_252d, dgs10_range_position, slope_30s10s

Given the announcement is forward-looking (start "in [date] " or equivalent), and the most recent data still show flat/rising assets, I lean toward a modest decline beginning next month, but with only moderate confidence since actual runoff paces and initial caps are small.

The yield sits at 0.71 of its 12-month range (2.62 high, 1.79 low), well into the upper half, indicating the long end remains closer to its highs than its lows despite the last two-observation pullback from 2.46 to 2.38. Policy language shows reinvestment/rollover of Treasury and MBS holdings continuing unchanged — a steady-state balance sheet stance, not a fresh tightening impulse that would obviously push yields higher. On balance, the range position and 252d trend still favor a mild upward bias, but the recent stall keeps conviction moderate rather than strong.

arbitrated.notes:
Direct read on curve_slope: the 2s10s spread itself is reported falling for two straight prints (0.83→0.79→0.78), below its own 0.82 threshold, with negative momentum across 21d/63d/252d and range position near the 12m low. That is a fairly clean, multi-horizon-consistent flattening signal from the analyst whose whole mandate is this spread, so it gets primary weight.

Cross-checking against front-end vs long-end drivers: term_premium (long end proxy, DGS10) shows a mild-but-real upward bias (252d +0.53pp, range position 0.71) though it just stalled over the last two prints. labor_tightness shows unemployment at a 12-month low and still grinding down (tighter labor market), which is historically a front-end-hawkish, curve-flattening force since it supports near-term policy firmness even as the statement language ("stayed low") suggests the committee sees stabilization rather than urgency for cuts. Balance-sheet normalization is only just beginning with small initial caps — a modest, still-uncertain long-end supply/duration effect that shouldn't be read as a strong steepening catalyst this month; it's more noise than signal for slope specifically. Inflation is flat/cooling (core decelerating), which argues against a front-end selloff and is broadly consistent with continued flattening rather than a steepening reversal. Inflation expectations ticking up modestly but plateauing don't move the front-end/long-end balance much.

Putting these together: the direct slope measurement (flattening, two-print confirmed break of its own threshold) is corroborated by a still-tightening labor market keeping front-end yields supported, while the long end shows only a stalled, moderate upward bias. No driver here argues strongly for steepening. I'll lean flattening with moderate conviction, tempered by the deceleration in the pace of the spread's own decline and the early-stage, ambiguous balance-sheet impulse.

why.curve_slope: Direct 2s10s spread measurement: two straight declines, below own threshold, negative multi-horizon momentum confirms flattening.
why.term_premium: DGS10 shows mild upward bias (252d +0.53pp) but recent two-print stall tempers it; modest long-end lift, not a steepening driver.
why.labor_tightness: Unemployment at 12m low, still declining — tighter labor market supports front-end yields, reinforcing flattening bias.

Core_3m_annualized (1.68%) remains below core yoy (1.55% trend), consistent with soft underlying momentum. Headline_3m_annualized at 2.24% is close to spot yoy, suggesting no strong near-term thrust either way. Policy language confirms both measures "have declined this year and are running below 2 percent," with expectation of near-term softness stabilizing toward target over medium term — no hawkish inflation alarm. Given decelerating mom momentum after the hurricane bump, softening core underlying trend, and policy language reaffirming sub-2% readings, I lean toward flat-to-slightly-down for next headline print rather than further acceleration.
Would change its mind if: A headline mom print reaccelerating above 0.3% or core PCE yoy jumping above 1.7% would signal renewed broad-based pressure, reversing this flat call.

The series shows a clean, steady downtrend from 4.70% to 4.10% over 13 months, with 3m and 6m changes both -0.20pp and 12m change -0.60pp. Sahm-gap is negligible at +0.07pp, meaning no rapid-rise loosening signal is present. The rate is now only 0.10pp above my 4.0% natural-rate anchor, sitting at its 12-month low with zero range position, indicating a labor market that continues to tighten steadily rather than plateau. Policy language reinforces this: the Fed explicitly notes the unemployment rate "declined further" despite hurricane-related payroll disruptions, suggesting underlying tightness persisted through a transitory shock. There's no evidence of deceleration in the pace of decline — the trend has been remarkably smooth without reversals. Given the level is already close to what I consider full employment, some flattening is plausible soon, but momentum and the absence of any uptick argue for continuation next month rather than a turn. I lean toward another modest decline or at worst flat, with more weight on further tightening given the consistency of the trend.
Would change its mind if: A print above 4.20% would signal a loosening reversal; continued prints at or below 4.10% confirm tightening persists.

=== balance_sheet ===

This is a much slower pace of decline than a steady QT cap would produce over 13 weeks if consistently applied; the noisy up-down pattern (down, up, down, up, down) suggests reinvestment/settlement noise or a cap that is only loosely binding, not a clear accelerating runoff. My prior flat call was reasonable and remains supported: the latest print did not confirm any acceleration, in fact it ticked up slightly. Absent statement language (none given here) confirming pace or pause, I read this as balance sheet essentially flat-to-very-slightly-declining, with no strong signal of renewed QT acceleration or QE.   That said, the spread remains deeply compressed: slope_range_position of 0.10 shows the curve is still near the bottom decile of its 12-month range, and slope_distance_from_flat at just 0.58pp confirms the curve remains historically flat with little cushion before inversion. The 21d bounce is modest and could easily be noise within a still-flattening regime, especially as the longer-horizon trends (63d, 252d) remain firmly negative. The 10s30s slope at 0.23 also suggests limited long-end steepening pressure.
  
  Given the conflicting signals — a genuine but small near-term uptick against a dominant multi-quarter flattening trend and a curve sitting near cycle lows — I don't have conviction for a continued strong down move, but the balance of evidence doesn't support calling steepening either. I'll set this closer to flat, with a slight lean toward down given the still-negative longer-term momentum.
Would change its mind if: A clear break above 0.60-0.65 sustained over multiple prints would confirm steepening; a drop back below 0.55 would confirm renewed flattening.

My prior flat call at 0.35 conviction was essentially correct — the small further drift to -0.62 was within the bounds of "sustained below -0.61," which is the threshold I set for reconsidering, but the magnitude of that drift is trivial and momentum has not accelerated. There's no policy language driving this. Given the extremely low weekly changes and the index having spent multiple readings clustered tightly at -0.61/-0.62, I see this as continued near-stagnation with a very mild easing bias rather than a fresh directional move. The series is at its 12-month high (be_range_position 1.00), sitting +0.45pp above its 12-month low, with no signs of stalling in the last few prints (each of the last six readings is flat-to-up, no reversals). Policy language corroborates the market data: the statement was upgraded from "remain low" to explicitly acknowledge that "market-based measures of inflation compensation have increased in recent months," a direct confirmation of the price action. Distance from the 2% target is now +0.11pp, a modest overshoot but not extreme. My prior call (up, 0.30) was correct — breakeven rose from ~2.09 area to 2.11 — and the evidence set has if anything strengthened this month versus last (accelerating short-term momentum, confirming policy language). I am raising conviction given the corroboration between price and language, though the move remains modest in absolute terms and the level is not yet stretched far from target.

Policy language confirms both measures "have declined this year and are running below 2 percent" but does not describe a fresh downtrend — it's consistent with the recent stabilizing/turning pattern. Given headline momentum has clearly broken above my stated threshold and core is also drifting up off its low, I now see modest reacceleration risk in headline for the next release, driven partly by energy pass-through and partly by a genuine core uptick.
Would change its mind if: A headline m/m print reverting back below ~0.15% and core PCE yoy stalling or falling back toward 1.4% would undercut this reacceleration read.

There is no evidence of deceleration in the pace of improvement — each recent print has either held or ticked down, with no reversals in the last several months. Momentum, level, and policy language all point the same direction. Absent a plateau or uptick signal, the most defensible call is continued modest tightening, though a market already at cycle lows carries some mean-reversion risk that tempers conviction slightly below very high.

Taken alone this argues for "flat, slight steepening bias" rather than renewed flattening — I largely accept that read since it's the freshest, most direct read on the actual spread.

Cross-checking against the front-end and long-end drivers that were actually reported: labor_tightness is flat-with-slight-tightening-bias (unemployment ticked up to 4.10% but underlying trend still tight) — this doesn't push the front end sharply in either direction, consistent with no strong slope pressure from the short end. financial_conditions tightening (NFCI rising off lows) is a headwind that typically compresses front-end easing expectations somewhat, a mild flattening input, but it's a broad-conditions measure, not cleanly front-end.

The long end has more directional signal: term_premium is plateauing near 12m highs (2.87, off the 2.94 high, momentum stalling) — no fresh long-end breakout. Balance_sheet is confirmed continuing runoff (down, -29,657 4w), which reduces Fed's footprint in longer-duration Treasuries and is typically a modest long-end steepening pressure (less price support = higher long yields relative to front, since front end is dominated by policy rate expectations). It's also corroborated on the long end by term_premium: DGS10 has fallen sharply (2.89→2.74, -0.13pp over 21d) even as inflation and financial conditions data don't argue for a long-end selloff. Front end rising on hike expectations while long end is actively falling is a textbook flattening combination — both curve_slope's raw print and the two component drivers agree on direction, which gives me confidence this is not just a stale echo.

Financial_conditions tightening (NFCI grinding up) is directionally consistent with a hiking cycle continuing to squeeze the front end, reinforcing rather than contradicting the flattening call, though its slope implication is indirect. Inflation and inflation_expectations are genuinely mixed (headline reaccelerating on base effects but momentum stalling, breakevens stepping down) and don't have a clean, differential front-vs-back read, so I give them no direct weight on slope beyond what's already captured in term_premium's own framing. Balance_sheet's continued runoff is a duration/term-premium-adjacent story but doesn't obviously bend front vs. back differently, so I set it aside for slope purposes despite the panel flagging it as a missing input to term_premium.

Net: flattening bias, moderate-high conviction, trade sized as a front-end-up/long-end-down flattener.

discounted.why: Runoff pace is a level/term-premium story, not clearly differential front-vs-back for slope.

arbitrated.falsifier: A 2s10s print back above 0.55-0.58 alongside a DGS10 bounce above 2.85 would flip this to a stabilization/steepening read.

risks.text: A dovish Fed pivot could stall front-end rise, removing the main flattening driver.
A broad risk-off rally could pull both DGS2 and DGS10 down together, muddying the slope read.
Curve is already at a 12-month low, raising odds of a technical bounce/short-covering steepening.

trade.rationale: Flattener: short DGS2/long DGS10 exposure equally weighted so a parallel shift nets to zero, isolating the flattening slope view.

why.curve_slope: Own 2s10s print at 12m low, falling in 6 of 9 recent obs; core evidence, kept as reported.
why.term_premium: DGS10 falling sharply confirms long-end pull lower; supports flattening independent of curve_slope series.
why.labor_tightness: Tighter labor (falling unemployment) supports continued front-end hikes pushing DGS2 up, reinforcing flattening.

With both core and headline accelerating together and at multi-month highs, I see continued upward momentum into the next release, though a single large jump warrants some caution about payback/volatility before declaring a firm new trend.
