user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: down, conviction 0.60
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The series continues its steady decline: latest print 4,305,491 vs prior 4,315,896, continuing an unbroken string of week-over-week decreases across most of the 13-week window (with only minor upticks at obs 2 and 5). The 4-week change (-22,028) and 13-week change (-86,707, -1.97%) confirm runoff is proceeding at a fairly consistent pace, roughly in line with or slightly above the recent trend rate. Assets remain well below the 52-week high (-171,412), with no sign of stabilization. My prior call of "down" was correct — the trajectory has not reversed or plateaued. There is no statement language this period to signal any change in caps, pace, or reinvestment policy, so I have to rely purely on the measurement trend, which is unambiguous: this is continued, steady QT. Absent any policy signal suggesting a slowdown, pause, or acceleration, the most reasonable extrapolation for the next month is a continuation of the same roughly -20k to -25k per 4-week pace. I maintain a confident down call, with conviction slightly higher than before given the consistency of the multi-week decline and absence of any contradicting evidence.
Would change its mind if: A multi-week reversal (rising fed_assets over 3+ consecutive weeks) or statement language announcing a pause/end to runoff or a cap change.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_pct_change_13w

=== curve_slope ===
Call: down, conviction 0.45
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 2s10s spread has ground lower again this period: 0.36→0.33 over the ten prints, with the latest tick actually up slightly (0.32→0.33) but still near the bottom of the recent range. My prior "flat" call was too sanguine — the 21d (-0.10), 63d (-0.15) and especially 252d (-0.55) changes all point firmly toward continued flattening, and the spread is now sitting just 0.02pp above its 12-month low (range position 0.03 of 1), meaning virtually the entire trailing year's range has been from here upward. The curve is at its most compressed level in the window, only 0.33pp from outright inversion. The Fed's statement language shifted to an actual hike (to 1.75-2.00%) plus continued "further gradual increases" guidance — this is a hiking committee pressing the front end while the long end has been comparatively anchored, consistent with the mechanical flattening pattern seen in dgs2 outpacing any long-end move. There's no evidence of steepening impulse; the one-tick uptick (0.32→0.33) is noise, not a trend reversal. I'm shifting from flat to a modest "down" lean, though conviction is capped because the last single data point ticked up and the pace of flattening (21d) has decelerated versus the 63d/252d figures.
Would change its mind if: A sustained move back above 0.40pp over coming weeks, or several consecutive prints reversing the recent low, would confirm steepening rather than continued flattening.
Leaned on: slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d, slope_change_252d, slope_range_position, slope_above_12m_low, slope_distance_from_flat

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: up, conviction 0.45
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior call (continued easing, i.e. NFCI drifting down) was wrong: after bottoming near -0.57, NFCI has risen for the last three readings (-0.56, -0.55, -0.54), a +0.02 move over 4 weeks. This is a small but consistent uptick off the 52-week low, and the level is now back above my -0.53 invalidation threshold's neighborhood, though not yet decisively through it. Conditions remain well below the zero average (-0.54), so financial conditions are still loose in absolute terms, but the momentum has clearly shifted from easing to modest tightening over the past month. The 13-week change is still slightly negative (-0.04), reflecting the earlier easing trend, but the most recent trajectory (last three points) dominates for a one-month-ahead call. With range_position at 0.64 (above midpoint of the 52-week range) and no policy language suggesting a reversal driver, I read this as a modest continuation of the recent uptick rather than a sharp reversal. I'm revising to a tightening lean, but conviction is moderate given the move is small and could still be noise within a still-easy regime.
Would change its mind if: A drop back below -0.56 sustained for two+ weeks would signal the tightening uptick has failed and easing has resumed.
Leaned on: nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_range_position, nfci_above_52w_low

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.25
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "down" call was wrong: breakeven_10y has been essentially flat-to-slightly-up over the last 10 readings (2.12→2.11), with the 21-day change now +0.04pp and 63-day +0.06pp, both positive — the falsifier condition (positive 21-day change) I set has been triggered. The longer 252-day change of +0.38pp shows a substantial rise over the past year, and range_position of 0.85 indicates breakevens sit near the top of their 12-month band (2.18 high), well above the 12-month low. Distance from the 2% target is +0.11pp, still modest but persistent. The recent day-to-day series is noisy (bouncing between 2.10-2.12) without a clear trend, and policy language changed from "remain low" to a more neutral "little changed on balance," suggesting the Committee no longer sees expectations as depressed — a mildly hawkish/neutral shift in framing rather than a dovish one. Given the flat recent trajectory but positive medium-term drift and high range position, I lean toward flat-to-slightly-up rather than continuing to call down. The evidence doesn't support a strong directional call given the tight 2.10-2.12 chop, but the balance of evidence (positive 21d/63d changes, high range position, softened policy language) tilts modestly upward.
Would change its mind if: A drop back to 2.10 or below sustained for two+ observations, or negative 21-day change, would flip this back to flat/down.
Leaned on: be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_range_position, be_distance_from_target

=== inflation ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.35
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 15 days ago.
Report:
  Last release: headline CPI y/y rose to 2.81% from 2.47%, continuing the uptrend I called. However, the underlying momentum signal is now mixed. The outgoing m/m print was just 0.09%, well below the 3m average of 0.19% and below my own stated falsifier threshold (~0.10%) for flagging deceleration risk. Headline 3m-annualized (2.33%) and core 3m-annualized (2.32%) have actually converged back toward the y/y level rather than accelerating further, suggesting the sharp y/y climb was partly base-effect driven (comparing against a soft month a year ago) rather than fresh momentum. Core PCE did not reverse below 1.7% — it printed 1.98%, its highest in the window — which keeps the core trend constructive and near target. But with the freshest monthly print decelerating sharply and the 3-month annualized pace now roughly matching (not exceeding) the y/y rate, the acceleration impulse looks like it's fading. Policy language shifted to describing inflation as "near" the symmetric objective with expectations "little changed," consistent with stabilization rather than further acceleration. I weight core PCE for persistence, which still looks firm, but the collapse in the latest headline m/m print argues for the y/y line flattening rather than extending its recent pace. Net: modest deceleration risk after strong recent readings.
Would change its mind if: A rebound in m/m prints back above ~0.20-0.25% for 2+ months, or core PCE pushing convincingly above 2.0%, would revive the acceleration case.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, outgoing_mom, headline_mom_3m_avg, mom_gap_vs_outgoing, headline_3m_annualized, core_3m_annualized, core_pce_yoy, headline_yoy_change_3m

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.25
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 22 days ago.
Report:
  The latest print rose to 4.00% from a 12-month low of 3.80%, exactly matching the threshold I set for questioning further tightening. My prior call of "down" (continued tightening) was wrong this round — the rate rose 0.20pp off the low rather than continuing to fall. That said, the broader trend remains benign: 12m change is -0.30pp, 6m change is -0.10pp, 3m change is flat, and the 3m average (3.93%) still sits below the latest print, suggesting some noise around a still-low plateau rather than a clear reversal. The Sahm-gap of +0.13pp is well below the 0.5pp threshold that would signal recession-onset loosening. The level itself (4.00%) is right at the natural-rate estimate, not signaling slack. Policy language is essentially unchanged, still describing labor market as continuing to strengthen with unemployment "staying low" — no acknowledgment of loosening. Given one-month bounces of this magnitude (3.80→4.00) have happened before in this series without producing a sustained trend, I read this as more noise than trend-break, but the flat 3m trend and uncertainty warrant a smaller, hedged conviction than a firm directional call.
Would change its mind if: A print at or above 4.20% would confirm a genuine loosening trend; a return to 3.80-3.90% would confirm the bounce was noise and tightening resumed.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_6m, unrate_change_12m, unrate_3m_avg, unrate_12m_low, sahm_gap, unrate_distance_from_natural

=== term_premium ===
Call: down, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 10-year has drifted sideways-to-slightly-down over the last few sessions (2.85 latest vs 2.92 ten readings ago), consistent with the prior "down" call playing out modestly. My falsifier (sustained move above 2.95 or push to 3.03+) was not triggered, so the prior call was directionally correct, though the recent path is mostly flat/choppy (21d change only +0.02pp) rather than a strong decline. Over the medium term the yield is still up 0.63pp over the past year and sits at 0.75 of its 12-month range, meaning it remains historically elevated despite the recent pullback. The 30s10s slope is modest (+0.13pp) and 10s2s slope (+0.33pp) doesn't suggest aggressive long-end selling pressure right now. With inflation-expectations language slightly simplified but not signaling a hawkish shift, and no fresh evidence of heavy issuance or balance-sheet-driven pressure in front of me, I see the long end as range-bound with a mild easing bias continuing near-term, but the very small 21d move tempers confidence in a strong down call. I lean toward continued modest softness or flatness rather than a reversal higher.
Would change its mind if: Sustained move back above 2.95 for several sessions, or push toward 3.03+ highs, would flip this back toward up.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_change_252d, dgs10_range_position, slope_30s10s, slope_10s2s

=== balance_sheet ===

The 4-week change (-36,739), 13-week change (-108,605), and 52-week change (-233,446) all confirm sustained, ongoing QT at a fairly steady pace — roughly $27-30bn/month recently, consistent with prior months. Assets remain well below the 52-week high (-252,260), reinforcing the runoff trajectory. My prior call of "down" was correct: there was no multi-week reversal, and no statement language signaled a pause or cap change (indeed, the latest statement is silent on this driver, which itself does not alter the mechanical, pre-announced pace of runoff). With no new communication suggesting a change to the caps or a pause, the mechanical process should continue to drain assets at a similar pace into next month-end. I see no evidence of deceleration or reversal in the recent weekly prints — the last few weeks show continued declines. This supports maintaining a "down" call with continued solid conviction, similar to the prior period's reasoning.
Would change its mind if: A multi-week reversal (rising fed_assets over 3+ consecutive weeks) or statement language announcing a pause/end to runoff or a cap change.

Still, the broader picture remains flattening-biased: slope_change_63d (-0.18) and slope_change_252d (-0.58) are both firmly negative, and the spread sits at just 0.09 in its 12-month range (0.18-0.86), only 0.06pp above the 12-month low. So this is a small bounce within a much larger flattening trend, not a reversal of it. The 21d change is only -0.08, meaning the longer-lag negative is decaying, consistent with the recent uptick. Policy language shifted from a hike to "maintain" the target range — a pause in front-end tightening — which mechanically tends to support the front end less aggressively rising, potentially allowing a bit of curve steepening at the margin. Given the tug between a fresh 3-print bounce and a still-deeply negative 63d/252d trend, I lean toward the modest recent steepening continuing marginally, but conviction is limited since the spread remains near cycle lows and one soft CPI or hawkish speaker could reverse it quickly.
Would change its mind if: A drop back below 0.20pp over the next few prints, or slope_change_21d turning negative again, would confirm the flattening trend has resumed.

Core PCE tells a different, firmer story: it ticked up to 2.00% y/y, right at target, with core_yoy_change_3m at +0.11pp — a slow, persistent grind higher. But headline's recent softness (weak monthly prints, falling y/y) and a much lower base effect suggest headline y/y is more likely to tick down or stay flat next month rather than reaccelerate, since the 3m annualized pace is well under the current y/y print. arbitrated.notes:
The direct curve_slope read (0.24, essentially flat, conviction 0.25) is the most on-point input, but it's a synthesis and its own long-term momentum (252d -0.60pp) points toward flattening pressure that hasn't fully played out — it's a stall after a longer flattening trend, not a stall after steepening. term_premium (up-lean, 0.30 conviction) is long-end-specific: DGS10 near 12-month highs with positive 63d/252d momentum, consolidating rather than reversing — this is a genuine long-end pull that argues for some steepening impulse if it resumes. labor_tightness (flat-to-down unemployment, i.e., a still-tightening labor market) is front-end relevant: a labor market pinned at 12-month lows keeps the Fed's hawkish anchor on the front end, which the curve_slope analyst explicitly cites as the reason 2s10s stays compressed. balance_sheet's steady QT (down, 0.70 conviction) is long-end supply pressure — continued runoff should, if anything, add modest upward pressure on long-end term premium/yields relative to the front end, a mild steepening force, though it's a monthly-cadence input with no direct slope linkage reported.

Netting these: front end stays anchored by a still-tight labor market and no dovish pivot signal; long end has modest continued upward pressure from term premium momentum and QT-driven supply, though both are decelerating/consolidating rather than accelerating. This tilts marginally toward stabilization-to-modest-steepening rather than further flattening, but conviction is low given the curve_slope analyst's own low conviction and clustering near 12-month lows. I don't fully override the curve_slope flat call, but I lean slightly steeper given the long-end-specific inputs (term premium, balance sheet) both point the same modest direction against a front end that's merely holding, not tightening further.

discounted.why: Not slope-relevant on its own; flat NFCI has no clear front-vs-long differential.
Level/policy-path input already embedded in labor_tightness and term_premium reads; avoids double-counting.
Breakeven plateau is a level signal, not clearly differential across curve segments.

arbitrated.falsifier: A clear break in slope_change_21d/63d back toward flattening (below 0.18pp) or an unemployment print reversing above 3.80% would flip this back to flat/flatten.

risks.text: curve_slope analyst's own conviction is low (0.25) and could reassert flattening if 252d trend dominates.
A dovish Fed pivot on labor softness would flatten the curve further, contradicting this lean.
QT pace could accelerate or pause unexpectedly, reversing the modest steepening pressure assumed here.
Low conviction across most inputs means positioning could be thin and reverse quickly on a single data surprise.

trade.rationale: Modest steepener: long-end term premium and QT supply pressure lean slightly steeper against a tight-labor-anchored front end, but low conviction given weak, stalling slope signal.

Term_premium reports 10Y yields grinding higher (3.08→3.15), pressing toward 12-month highs, with a positive and stable 30s10s slope (+0.24pp) — that's long-end-specific upward pressure, not a broad level move, since it's confirmed by steepening in the adjacent long-end curve segment (30s10s), which wouldn't move with a parallel shift alone. Labor_tightness is flat/plateaued (unemployment stuck 3.70-3.80%), so front-end hike pressure is not intensifying beyond what's priced — no fresh flattening impulse from the front. Balance_sheet's continued QT runoff is a duration-supply story that plausibly reinforces the term-premium build at the long end (more Treasury supply absorption needed) but curve_slope's own read still shows no realized steepening yet. Weighing this: the front end has no new tightening catalyst (labor plateaued, inflation flat), while the long end has a live, gathering term-premium bid with corroborating 30s10s steepening. This tips the reconciled call from curve_slope's pure "flat" toward a modest steepening lean — the term premium driver is the one with genuine new information this month, and it specifically implicates the long end. I don't have strong enough conviction to call this a large move given curve_slope's own low realized volatility, so I keep the position modest.

discounted.why: QT pace unchanged/mechanical; directionally supportive of long-end premium but not itself a slope signal.
Not a slope-differentiating input; mild tightening doesn't clearly split front vs long end.
Flat headline/core reading offers no differential front-vs-long signal beyond what's in labor/term premium.
Breakevens plateaued flat; no incremental slope information beyond term premium's own read.

arbitrated.falsifier: A pullback in 10Y below 3.05 with 30s10s flattening, or 2s10s printing below 0.22pp, would flip this back to flat/flattening.

risks.text: Term premium move could reverse quickly if long-end yields retrace from cycle highs.
A surprise dovish pivot could flatten term premium and curve simultaneously, invalidating the steepening lean.
Position carries negative roll if curve stays pinned near current flat levels for the full horizon.
Steepening trades are crowded; a positioning unwind could cause a sharp reversal unrelated to fundamentals.

trade.rationale: Modest steepener: long-end term premium building (10Y near highs, 30s10s positive) while front end has no fresh tightening catalyst; curve_slope's own flat read caps size.

Call: down, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "flat" call at 0.30 conviction was directionally too sanguine: breakeven has since drifted lower and the "below 2.03" downside trigger I set was in fact already met and has continued. Looking at the last 10 prints, the series fell from 2.02 to a low of 1.91, then bounced modestly to 1.98/1.97. The 21-day and 63-day changes are both negative (-0.08pp and -0.15pp), showing the recent trend is down, even though the 252-day change is still positive (+0.13pp), reflecting an earlier run-up that has since partly reversed. Current level (1.97) sits below the 2% objective by -0.03pp, and range position (0.38 of the 12-month range) confirms it's in the lower-middle of the past year's band, not near highs. The last two prints (1.94→1.98→1.97) show stabilization/mild bounce off the 1.91 trough, which tempers a strong down call — this looks more like consolidation after a decline than continued freefall. Policy language remains boilerplate ("little changed, on balance"), offering no new signal. Given the mixed near-term bounce against the clearly negative medium-term trend and sub-target level, I lean modestly down rather than flat, but without high conviction given the stabilization in the newest two readings.
Would change its mind if: A rise back above 2.02-2.03 over the next month would confirm reversal upward rather than continued softness; a fresh push below 1.90 confirms downward momentum.
Leaned on: be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_distance_from_target, be_range_position

=== inflation ===
Call: down, conviction 0.55
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 15 days ago.
Report:
  The prior "flat" call was largely vindicated: headline CPI yoy fell from 2.49 to 2.15, and the newest monthly print was -0.07% m/m, well below the ~0.30% threshold I flagged for acceleration. This is a clear deceleration signal, not just noise. Headline yoy has dropped 0.50pp over three months and now sits at the bottom of its 12-month range (yoy_range_position 0.02, essentially at the 12m low). The 3m-annualized headline pace (+1.49%) is running well below the trailing yoy rate, indicating the deceleration has real near-term momentum, not just base effects. Core PCE tells a similar but softer story: yoy eased to 1.87 from 1.96, and 3m-annualized core (+1.42%) is also running below target and below its own yoy rate. Core remains closer to 2% than headline (core_distance_from_target only -0.13pp) and is the better read on persistence, but even core's momentum has turned down modestly (core_yoy_change_3m -0.12pp). Policy language is unchanged on inflation being "near 2 percent" with stable expectations, offering no signal of a turn. Given the negative outgoing monthly print and soft 3-month annualized rates on both measures, I expect next month's headline yoy to tick down further or stay historically low, absent a rebound in monthly prints.
Would change its mind if: A monthly CPI print rebounding above ~0.25% m/m, or headline/core yoy ticking back up, would signal the deceleration has stalled or reversed.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, headline_mom, core_pce_yoy, headline_3m_annualized, core_3m_annualized, headline_yoy_change_3m, yoy_range_position, outgoing_mom

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.55
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 22 days ago.
Report:
  The unemployment rate held at 3.80% for the third consecutive reading, matching my prior flat call and confirming it was correct — no print above 3.90% or below 3.70% materialized, so neither loosening nor renewed tightening momentum is evident. The 3m and 6m changes are both exactly 0.00pp, indicating a genuine plateau. The 12m change of -0.40pp still reflects tightening over the past year, but that move has fully stalled in recent months. The Sahm-rule style gap (current vs 12m low) is a trivial +0.07pp, far below any threshold suggesting acceleration in labor market slack. Range position at 0.25 shows the rate sits closer to its 12-month low than high, consistent with a still-tight labor market that is simply no longer tightening further. The level itself, at 0.20pp below my assumed natural rate of ~4%, points to a tight market, but the flat recent trend dominates my forward call since the mandate weighs both level and momentum. Policy language reinforces this: the Fed's updated statement now says unemployment "has declined" rather than "stayed low," but the printed data show stabilization, not further declines. I expect the plateau to persist into the next report.
Would change its mind if: A print above 3.90% would signal loosening momentum; a print at or below 3.70% would confirm renewed tightening beyond the recent plateau.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_6m, unrate_12m_low, sahm_gap, unrate_range_position, unrate_distance_from_natural

Call: down, conviction 0.35

Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:

My prior "up" call was wrong for this most recent leg: the 21-day change is now -0.11pp, reversing the 63-day (+0.12pp) and 252-day (+0.67pp) uptrends. Range position at 0.75 still sits in the upper part of the 12-month band, so the longer-term uptrend context is intact, but the immediate momentum has turned lower. The 30s10s slope remains positive at 0.29pp and has not flattened or inverted, so there's no acute steepening/curve signal of fresh long-end premium building. My stated falsifier condition (drop below 3.05 with range position under 0.6) is partially met — yield is below 3.05 — but range position at 0.75 is still elevated, so the picture is mixed rather than a clean reversal. Given the recent two-observation decline and lack of steepening pressure, I lean toward continued softness or flatness near current levels rather than a resumption of the climb, but the elevated range position and still-positive slope argue against a strong down call.
Would change its mind if: A rebound back above 3.06 with range position climbing back toward 0.8+ and 30s10s steepening further would revive the up call.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_range_position, slope_30s10s

Would change its mind if: A print back at or below 3.80% would confirm the plateau/tightening view; a print at or above 4.00% would confirm a clearer loosening trend.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_12m_low, unrate_above_12m_low, sahm_gap, unrate_distance_from_natural

=== term_premium ===
Call: down, conviction 0.40
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 10yr has continued to grind lower, printing 2.69 last, down from 2.86 ten observations back. Both the 21d and 63d changes are -0.37pp, confirming the decline is not just a one-day blip but a sustained move over the past one-to-three months. The prior "down" call was validated by this data. Range position at 0.35 shows the yield sitting in the lower third of its 12-month band (2.40-3.24), consistent with continued downward pressure rather than a bottoming/reversal. The 252d change is still positive (+0.22pp), so the longer-run trend remains mildly higher, but the recent momentum clearly dominates and is what matters for a one-month-ahead call. Slope_30s10s at 0.33pp is not showing aggressive steepening that would signal fresh long-end selling pressure or duration supply concerns; if anything, a moderate slope with falling yields suggests demand for duration/flight-to-quality dynamics rather than issuance-driven term premium rise. Policy language is neutral/unchanged, offering no fresh catalyst either way. Given the persistent negative short and medium-term momentum, no evidence of reversal, and yield still well below its 12-month high, I expect the drift lower to continue modestly into month-end, though conviction is tempered since the move could stall given how far it's already come from the recent local peak.
Would change its mind if: A rebound back above ~3.00 with range position climbing back toward 0.7+ and 30s10s steepening further would flip this to an up call.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_range_position, slope_30s10s

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: down, conviction 0.75
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior call (down, 0.75) was correct: total assets fell again in the newest observation (4,050,044 → 4,047,052), extending a steady decline. Over the last 4 weeks assets are down $28,584M, over 13 weeks down $126,018M (-3.02%), and over 52 weeks down $394,265M, with assets now $387,811M below the 52-week high. The trajectory is smooth and monotonic in the recent weeks (no up-weeks in the tail of the series), consistent with ongoing balance-sheet runoff proceeding at a steady pace under the existing caps. There is no statement language today (silence), so I have no new information about a pause, taper of the cap, or reinvestment change — the absence of commentary means I default to continuation of the current mechanical runoff path rather than inferring any shift. Nothing in the measurements suggests deceleration; the 4-week pace (~$28.6B) is roughly in line with the 13-week average pace, suggesting no acceleration or deceleration signal either. Given the consistency and lack of any contradicting policy language, I expect assets to be lower again at next month-end.
Would change its mind if: 3+ consecutive weeks of rising fed_assets, or statement language announcing a pause/end to runoff or a cap change.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_change_52w, assets_pct_change_13w

=== curve_slope ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 2s10s spread is currently 0.18pp, near the middle-low of recent oscillation (0.15-0.19 over the last ten readings) and sitting at just the 10th percentile of its 12-month range (0.11-0.78pp), confirming the curve remains historically quite flat/compressed. My prior flat call was directionally fine: the last two prints held at 0.18, neither breaking below 0.15 nor pushing above 0.25 as I had specified. However, the momentum readings still show mild flattening pressure - slope_change_21d (-0.03) and slope_change_63d (-0.09) are both negative, and the 252-day change (-0.35) shows the broader trend over the past year has been a significant flattening from higher levels. The policy language shift toward "patient" stance and pausing hikes (vs. prior gradual-hike language) removes some front-end upward pressure, which could allow modest re-steepening if long yields hold, but this is a front-end consideration outside my slope-only lens. Given the spread has stabilized in a narrow 0.15-0.19 band for the last six readings with no clear breakout, and short-term momentum is only mildly negative, I see this as consolidation rather than a fresh directional move.
Would change its mind if: A break below 0.15pp or renewed negative slope_change_21d/63d would confirm resumed flattening; a clear push above 0.22-0.25pp would confirm steepening.
Leaned on: slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d, slope_range_position, slope_12m_low, slope_12m_high

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: down, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last three prints reverse the tightening I flagged previously: -0.42 → -0.44 → -0.46 → -0.48, i.e. the index has fallen for three straight readings after bottoming at -0.42. nfci_change_4w is now -0.06, back to easing, and the trough at -0.42 sits right at the 52w high, meaning conditions never actually breached zero or got tight in absolute terms — they stayed deep in loose territory (nfci_distance_from_zero -0.48) and are now loosening further. My prior "up" call is falsified by exactly the trigger I specified: the drop back below -0.48-ish levels with the 4w change flattening/reversing toward more negative. The 13w change is still slightly positive (+0.08), showing the medium-term trend was tightening, but the most recent momentum has clearly turned. Policy language shifted to "patient," consistent with an environment where the Committee is not signaling further tightening urgency, which fits the recent easing in the index. Given the index is near its 52w low and moving further from zero, I read this as a genuine easing move, not just noise, though the magnitude (three weeks of small -0.02 steps) is modest.
Would change its mind if: A rebound above -0.44 sustained two+ weeks, or nfci_change_4w turning positive, would signal renewed tightening.
Leaned on: nfci_change_4w, nfci_range_position, nfci_distance_from_zero, nfci_change_13w

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: up, conviction 0.40
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The previous 'down' call was wrong on the last data point: breakeven_10y rebounded to 1.84 and 1.85, back into the 1.80-1.85 range I flagged as my reversal trigger. The 21-day change is now +0.14pp, reversing much of the prior 63-day (-0.21pp) and 252-day (-0.24pp) declines. Level is still below the 2% objective (distance -0.15pp... actually -0.15 suggests target near 1.99-2.0, so still meaningfully under target) and range_position of 0.34 shows breakevens sit in the lower third of the 12-month range, but the immediate trend has clearly turned up over the last two prints. Policy language confirms market-based measures had moved lower in recent months, which is backward-looking and consistent with the 63d/252d declines, but the newest prints diverge from that already-acknowledged softness. Given the sharp two-observation rebound (1.78→1.84→1.85), the near-term momentum favors continued firming or at least stabilization at the higher end of the recent range, rather than resumption of decline. I don't have enough confirmation to call this a strong reversal, but the trigger condition I set has been met, so I'm shifting off 'down' toward a modest 'up' lean.
Would change its mind if: A drop back below 1.78 over the next month would indicate the rebound was noise and downward momentum has resumed.
Leaned on: breakeven_10y, be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_change_252d, be_range_position, be_distance_from_target

=== inflation ===
Call: down, conviction 0.45
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 16 days ago.
Report:
  The prior "down" call was correct and if anything understated: headline CPI yoy fell sharply from 2.00 to 1.49, a full percentage point drop over 3 months, driven by a -0.08% mom print (the second negative mom reading in three months). Headline 3m annualized is now -0.33%, deeply below target pace, and yoy sits at its 12-month low with zero cushion above that low. This is unambiguous headline deceleration, almost certainly energy/food driven given the sharp divergence from core. Core PCE, my preferred gauge of persistence, tells a very different story: it ticked up again to 2.04%, essentially at target, with core 3m annualized at +2.09% — stable, not decelerating. The core-minus-headline gap has widened to 0.55pp, confirming the headline move is a base/energy effect rather than broad disinflation. For the next CPI print specifically, headline momentum has been weak-to-negative for three straight months (mom_gap_vs_outgoing -0.30pp), and there's no sign of a rebound in the trend. Given the persistent negative-to-flat mom prints and no evidence of core reaccelerating enough to pull headline up, I lean toward continued flat-to-down headline yoy next month, though the streak of consecutive weak prints raises some risk of mean-reversion/base-effect payback.
Would change its mind if: A headline mom print above ~0.25%, or core PCE yoy turning down alongside headline, would signal the deceleration/divergence pattern is breaking.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, headline_mom, headline_3m_annualized, headline_yoy_change_3m, core_pce_yoy, core_3m_annualized, core_minus_headline, yoy_above_12m_low

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: up, conviction 0.40
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 23 days ago.
Report:
  The latest print rose to 4.00% from 3.90%, extending a climb from the 12-month low of 3.70% reached a few months back. That's a 0.20pp increase over 3 months (unrate_change_3m and sahm_gap both +0.20pp), and the rate now sits at the top of its 12-month range (range_position 0.75, at the 12m high of 4.10% tied). My prior call of "up" at 0.35 conviction was correct: the print did move to 4.00%, confirming the loosening lean I flagged. The 12m change is still flat at 0.00pp, so this isn't yet a Sahm-rule-triggering acceleration, but the trajectory since the 3.70% trough is a clear, steady uptrend across the last several readings (3.70→3.80→3.80→3.90→4.00). The policy language, though describing an earlier period of a strengthening labor market, is stale relative to the more recent unemployment prints showing gradual drift upward. At the natural-rate benchmark of ~4%, distance_from_natural is now 0, meaning the market is right at equilibrium tightness but arrived there via a rising-rate path, which carries more signal than the level alone. Given the consistent grind higher off the trough and now sitting at the range ceiling, I lean toward continued mild loosening rather than a reversal back down.
Would change its mind if: A print back at or below 3.80% would signal reversal to tightening; a print above 4.10% would confirm accelerating loosening and raise conviction further.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, sahm_gap, unrate_range_position, unrate_12m_low, unrate_distance_from_natural

=== term_premium ===
Call: down, conviction 0.40
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The previous "down" call was validated: the 10yr fell from 2.70 to 2.63 over the latest print, continuing a clear downtrend (63d change -0.45pp, 21d change -0.06pp). The yield now sits near the bottom of its 12-month range (range_position 0.10, only 0.07pp above the 12m low of 2.56%), confirming persistent downward pressure on the long end rather than any stabilization. The 30s10s slope at +0.36pp is modestly elevated but not steepening aggressively, and there's no sign of the rebound-and-steepening pattern that would have flipped this call. Policy language now explicitly notes market-based inflation compensation has moved lower in recent months — consistent with a compressing term premium and disinflationary pull on long yields, reinforcing rather than contradicting the price action. With the yield pinned near 12-month lows and no measurement showing reversal, momentum favors continued softness or at best stabilization near these depressed levels. I do not see reason to expect a bounce absent a fresh catalyst, so I maintain a down lean, though the pace of decline has decelerated (21d change is small), so conviction is moderate rather than high.
Would change its mind if: A rebound back above ~3.00 with range position climbing toward 0.7+ and 30s10s steepening further would flip this to an up call.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_range_position, dgs10_above_12m_low, slope_30s10s

=== balance_sheet ===

The latest weekly print (3,981,420) shows a sharp drop of roughly -47,011 from the prior week (4,028,431), the largest single-week decline in this series. The 4-week change is -65,632, and 13-week change is -124,778 (-3.04%), both consistent with continued QT rather than any deceleration. My prior call (down, 0.75) was correct — there was no 3-week rising streak, and no statement language signaled a pause; indeed no statement language on this driver was given at all, meaning no policy shift to reinvestment or cap changes has been communicated. With reserves continuing to drain and no announced change to the runoff cap, the mechanical trajectory points to further balance sheet contraction into month-end. The absence of statement commentary is itself informative — it implies no deviation from the existing runoff program. I maintain a confident down call, with conviction slightly higher than before given the acceleration in the most recent weekly decline, though I temper it slightly since a single volatile week (possibly reflecting technical/settlement effects) could partially reverse.
Would change its mind if: 3+ consecutive weeks of rising fed_assets, or statement language announcing a pause/end to runoff or a cap change.

Report:
  The slope has ticked up from a tight 0.14-0.17 range to 0.19 and now 0.21pp, the highest print in the 10-observation window. My prior "flat" call is now looking stale: slope_change_21d is +0.05pp (positive) and the last two observations broke clearly above the 0.14-0.17 band that had held for eight readings, approaching the 0.22-0.25 steepening threshold I set as my own falsifier. That said, longer lookbacks still show flattening pressure (slope_change_63d -0.02pp, slope_change_252d -0.42pp), and the spread remains deeply compressed in its 12-month range (slope_range_position 0.19, only 0.10pp above the 12-month low of 0.11pp), so this is a modest bounce off very flat levels, not a resumption of a steep curve. Given headline's strong recent momentum (two straight 0.38% prints, well above the 3m average of 0.35%), the mechanical carry into next month's y/y print favors another modest headline uptick unless energy reverses. I weight the momentum evidence over the core trend for this specific one-month headline call, since the question is about headline_cpi_yoy specifically. However, a rate already at 3.70% has limited room to fall further before mean-reverting, and the range has bounced within 3.70-4.00% for a year, so I don't expect a sharp move. The 4-week change (-24,627) and 13-week change (-128,800, -3.26%) both confirm a consistent, moderate pace of contraction, roughly in line with recent history — no signs of deceleration or reversal. With the latest statement silent on this driver (no new guidance on caps or reinvestment), the base case is simple continuation of the announced runoff pace. I maintain a confident down call for month-end, with conviction slightly below the prior reading only because the statement provided zero new confirming language this period, leaving the call resting purely on the measurement trend, which remains unambiguous.

The direct slope analyst gives a modest, hedged flattening call (0.35 conviction): after a multi-month steepening impulse (0.20→0.30), the last three prints have pulled back to 0.25, with medium-term momentum (21d/63d) still positive but short-term momentum turning down. Cross-checking against the front-end and long-end drivers: term_premium (down, 0.45) says the 10y is pinned at its 12-month low with negative momentum across all horizons — a genuine long-end pull that would mechanically flatten 2s10s if the front end doesn't fall as much. But term_premium itself flags it's missing balance_sheet and financial_conditions inputs. Balance sheet runoff (down/contracting, 0.75, fresh) is disinflationary for term premium via reduced private float pressure but also reflects continued QT — historically associated with some upward pressure on term premium/long end over time, though the current print shows no such effect yet. Labor tightness is flat/plateauing (0.35) — a stable, tight labor market that keeps the front end anchored by Fed policy expectations, not pushing cuts, which limits front-end downside and caps how much steepening could come from Fed-cut repricing. Inflation (down, 0.55) and inflation_expectations (down, 0.45) both point to continued disinflation, which supports lower long-end yields (via lower term premium) but also historically supports Fed easing expectations, which would pull the front end down too — an ambiguous net slope effect, so I don't let these override the slope analyst's own read.

Given the direct slope measurement shows recent flattening momentum, corroborated by term_premium's long-end grind lower against a stable/anchored front end (labor tightness flat), I lean modestly toward flattening, but keep conviction restrained given the medium-term steepening trend hasn't fully reversed and the flattening signal is only 3 prints old.

However, the near-term 21-day change is +0.07pp, showing some stabilization/mild bounce off the lows, and the last two prints (1.78→1.76) show renewed softness after that bounce. The policy language shift from "have declined" to "remain low" is notable — it signals the committee now sees the decline as having leveled off into a persistently low plateau rather than an ongoing slide, which tempers the case for further near-term downside. Distance from target (-0.24pp) confirms breakevens are still running below the 2% objective. Range position (0.27) shows levels are still in the lower third of the 12-month range. Given the mixed short-term signal (mild bounce, then renewed dip) against a "leveled off" narrative, I lean toward flat-to-slightly-down but with reduced conviction versus last time.

arbitrated.notes:
The direct slope read (curve_slope) is unambiguous and fresh: 2s10s has compressed sharply to 0.13pp, an extreme low-range reading, with negative deltas across 21d/63d/252d — broad-based, momentum-confirmed flattening. This is the primary signal for my seat. The question is whether the two drivers that hit front and long end differently corroborate or contradict.

Term_premium (long end) shows the 10y pinned near 12-month lows with decelerating downward momentum (21d flat) — the long end is low but not falling further at pace. Balance_sheet shows continued runoff (long-end supply pressure easing as QT nears its early conclusion), which is modestly bullish for long-end yields (lower), reinforcing anchored long-end levels rather than a steepening impulse from rising term premium. Labor_tightness ticked up slightly (unemployment 3.60→3.70) — a modest loosening signal that would normally support front-end richening (lower front-end yields, i.e., steepening pressure), but the move is within noise (Sahm gap only +0.10pp) and conviction is low. Financial conditions are flat/marginally easier, not a strong differentiator.

Weighing this: the long end has genuine anchoring forces (falling term premium, decelerating balance-sheet runoff removing supply pressure) while the front end lacks a clear easing catalyst strong enough to offset — labor tightness data is too noisy to lean on for a steepening front-end call, and financial conditions give no slope signal. This supports the curve_slope analyst's direct read: flattening is intact and likely continues, driven by the long end grinding lower/anchored while front-end policy expectations hold. I take a moderate-conviction flattening view, not extreme, since momentum in the spread itself already looks stretched (0.13pp, near flat) and could stabilize rather than press further into inversion.

discounted.why: Move within noise band (Sahm gap +0.10pp), too weak to drive a front-end steepening override.
Not a slope-relevant driver; flat/marginal easing gives no front-vs-long differentiation.
CPI momentum stabilizing is a level/duration signal, not clearly differentiating front vs long end.
Breakeven drift is a duration/inflation-compensation signal already reflected in term_premium's long-end read.

arbitrated.falsifier: A rebound in slope_2s10s above 0.20 with positive slope_change_21d, or a clear break above 3.80% unemployment confirming front-end easing, would flip this to steepening.

risks.text: Spread at 0.13pp is already near extreme; further compression may be limited, risking stabilization not flattening.
Early QT conclusion could eventually steepen via long-end supply relief once markets price the end-date, not yet in data.
Crowded flattening trades could unwind sharply on any dovish surprise that richens the front end faster than the long end.

trade.rationale: Flattener: long DGS2/short DGS10 yield exposure equal-opposite, capturing continued 2s10s compression while netting out parallel shift risk.

why.curve_slope: Direct fresh read: spread compressed to 0.13pp, broad-based negative deltas confirm flattening momentum intact.
why.term_premium: 10y yield anchored near 12-month lows; long end not rising, reinforcing flattening rather than steepening.
why.balance_sheet: Continued runoff this month reduces long-end supply pressure, keeping long yields anchored, consistent with flattening.
why.labor_tightness: Slight uptick in unemployment (loosening) could support front-end richening/steepening, but move is noise-level, low weight.

However, headline is the metric being asked about for the next release, and the level has now reached the top of its 12-month range with two consecutive 0.28% monthly prints — that momentum could carry one more month before mean-reverting. I'll call a modest continuation up, but with tempered conviction given core's contradicting signal and the historically choppy MoM series (note -0.07, -0.08 prints not far back).

FOMC language is essentially unchanged, still describing the labor market as "strong" with unemployment "remained low" — no shift in characterization that would signal an inflection. With three consecutive months at 3.60% after a brief dip to 3.50%, the series shows stability, not a trend in either direction. Since headline_cpi_yoy is the specific series I'm forecasting, and its momentum (3 consecutive ~0.28-0.29% prints, 3m avg 0.29% vs outgoing -0.08%) is unusually strong and consistent, I lean toward another modest increase next month, but temper conviction given core's softness suggests headline strength may not be durable and could be a base-effect or volatile-component driven pop.

Range position of 0.20 (out of 1) confirms the rate is still hugging the low end of its 13-month range. There's no evidence here of acceleration or deterioration — this is a labor market oscillating in a narrow 3.50-3.70% band with no clear directional momentum. Absent a fresh shock, the most defensible expectation for the next print is another reading close to 3.60%, possibly a tick in either direction, but not a meaningful move.

arbitrated.notes:
The direct curve_slope read is the strongest, freshest, and most directly relevant input: 2s10s spread at a new 12-month high (0.34pp), top of range, positive momentum across 21d/63d/252d windows, corroborated by 10s30s also widening (+0.47pp) — a broad long-end steepening rather than a front-end-specific artifact. That gives high confidence the slope call itself (steepening) is intact rather than exhausted.

Cross-checking against the other drivers on a front-vs-long basis: term_premium (long end) shows modest but persistent upward drift in DGS10 (range position 0.34, +0.14/+0.23pp over 21d/63d) — consistent with, and supportive of, the long end leading the steepening rather than the front end selling off. Labor_tightness is flat/range-bound (unemployment oscillating 3.50-3.70%, no clear directional momentum), implying no fresh front-end repricing pressure from labor data — the front end is anchored, consistent with a Fed "on hold" narrative rather than easing or hiking, which supports the steepening thesis coming from the long end drifting up while front end holds. Balance_sheet expansion (reserves up, five straight increases, accelerating) is a long-end-adjacent driver: continued QE-like expansion typically caps/anchors front-end funding costs while adding duration supply pressure that firms up further out — again consistent with steepening bias, not against it. Inflation and inflation_expectations both leaning up add some long-end upward pressure via term premium, reinforcing the same direction.

No driver here argues for flattening. The disagreement all resolves in one direction: front end anchored (Fed on hold, ample liquidity), long end drifting up on term premium and mild inflation firming. I maintain and slightly reinforce the steepening call from curve_slope, without inflating conviction too far given some mean-reversion risk after touching a range high.

discounted.why: Flat call, no momentum — used only as a consistency check on front-end anchoring, not a slope driver.

arbitrated.falsifier: 2s10s spread drops back below 0.20pp with negative 21d momentum reasserting, or DGS10 range position falls back under 0.25 — would flip to flattening.

risks.text: Spread is at a 12-month high; mean reversion could stall or reverse the steepening quickly.
A Fed surprise (renewed cutting or hawkish pivot) could re-anchor front end differently than assumed.
Balance sheet expansion could reflect emergency liquidity rather than deliberate QE, altering long-end supply dynamics.
Steepener trades may be crowded after strong recent momentum, raising unwind risk.

trade.rationale: Equal-and-opposite steepener: short-end anchored by on-hold Fed and ample liquidity, long end drifting up on term premium and mild inflation firming — captures slope only, net zero on parallel shift.

why.curve_slope: Direct, fresh measurement: 2s10s at 12mo high, momentum positive across all windows, corroborated by 10s30s widening.
why.term_premium: DGS10 mild persistent uptick supports long-end-led steepening; low absolute conviction on level but useful for slope direction.
why.balance_sheet: Own measurement (total assets) clearly rising, consistent, accelerating — used only to infer anchored front-end/long-end supply dynamic, not a level call.
why.labor_tightness: Flat/range-bound reading offers no directional edge itself, but confirms front-end anchoring assumption via absence of shock.

That's a strong, fresh, multi-horizon signal and I weight it heavily. term_premium's report shows dgs10 collapsing to a fresh 12m low, falling sharply over 21d/63d/252d — that's a long-end yield decline, which on its own says nothing about slope unless we know what the front end did. But curve_slope's own spread data already nets that out: since the spread widened even as the 10y fell, the front end (2y, driven by labor/policy expectations) must have fallen even faster. Labor_tightness confirms the front-end anchor: unemployment pinned near 12-month lows with no loosening signal, consistent with front-end yields staying low/anchored on continued easing expectations, reinforcing why front end can outpace long-end declines. The policy language confirms this is intentional large-scale QE: the Committee explicitly commits to increasing Treasury holdings by at least $500B and agency MBS by at least $200B "over coming months," plus full reinvestment of principal payments into agency MBS — a full reversal from any caps-based runoff regime to open-ended expansion. Given the explicit forward guidance for continued increases in holdings and the reinvestment directive, the balance sheet should keep expanding into next month-end, likely at a rapid pace, before any moderation. arbitrated.notes:
The rate-axis picture is dominated by two forces pulling the curve in opposite directions from a slope perspective. At the long end, term_premium reports 10y yield flat-to-pinned near 12-month lows (0.70, 0.70), with its own analyst explicitly flagging the fresh QE commitment ($500B UST + $200B MBS, plus reinvestment) as a suppressive force keeping the long end anchored. Crucially, term_premium's own report notes it was never handed the balance_sheet data directly — but balance_sheet's read (assets exploding +1.10T in 4 weeks, forward guidance for continued purchases, zero room below 52w high) directly corroborates and reinforces the term_premium analyst's suppression thesis. This is the informed cross-read: balance sheet expansion caps the long end more than the front end, which is a flattening-to-bull-steepening-ambiguous force on its own, but combined with an anchored front end (Fed at zero lower bound per curve_slope's own note), the QE-driven long-end suppression should compress the 2s10s spread — flattening bias.

Meanwhile curve_slope itself reports stalled momentum, three flat prints at 0.47pp, well off the 12m low, with no compression pressure evident yet — its own house view is genuinely flat, not directional. But curve_slope was formed without weighing the accelerating balance_sheet QE signal (a same-meeting driver, not stale), which specifically targets long-end suppression via explicit purchase commitments. That tips the balance modestly toward flattening: front end pinned near zero by policy, long end being actively pushed down further by open-ended QE purchases targeting duration. Labor_tightness (loosening labor market) and disinflation trends reinforce an accommodative-for-longer Fed, keeping the front end anchored while QE does the flattening work at the long end. I lean flat-to-modest-flattener rather than full conviction reversal, since curve_slope's own momentum read shows no confirmed break yet.

discounted.why: Front-end driver, directionally supportive but secondary to the QE/long-end suppression story dominating this call.

arbitrated.falsifier: A break in DGS10 back above 1.00% with rising range position, or a 2s10s print above 0.55pp with confirming momentum, would flip this back to flat/steepening.

risks.text: If QE purchases taper faster than guided, long-end suppression reverses and flattening thesis fails.
Curve_slope's own three-print flat reading could simply persist, making the flattening call premature.
Balance sheet expansion could instead widen term premium if perceived as inflationary or debt-driven rather than duration-absorbing.

trade.rationale: Modest flattener: long end capped by explicit open-ended QE purchase commitments while front end stays anchored near zero by policy; slope momentum itself is only tentatively confirming.

why.balance_sheet: Fresh, decisive QE acceleration with explicit forward purchase commitments; own measurement (assets) rising sharply, unambiguous.
why.curve_slope: Own house view is flat/stalled, but nudged toward mild flattening given uncorroborated QE long-end suppression it didn't weigh.
why.term_premium: 10y yield pinned near lows, flat trend, but QE commitment implies further downside risk to long-end premium ahead.
why.labor_tightness: Unemployment spiked 0.90pp, unchanged pass-through from analyst; supports front-end anchoring via dovish policy expectations.

user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: up, conviction 0.85
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior call (up, 0.85) was correct: total assets rose again to 6,573,136 from 6,367,887, a 4-week gain of ~$1.32tn and 13-week gain of ~$2.43tn, with the series making a new 52-week high (assets_below_52w_high = 0). This is the sharpest pace of expansion in the series, consistent with the open-ended, uncapped purchase language now in force ("purchase...in the amounts needed to support smooth market functioning"), which replaced the earlier fixed $500B/$200B commitment. The removal of fixed caps in favor of open-ended, needs-based purchases signals the Fed retains full flexibility to keep expanding aggressively, and the massive recent print shows that flexibility being used at full force. There is no language here about tapering, tapering schedules, or reducing purchase pace — the newer language is, if anything, more open-ended than the prior one. Given the trend, the magnitude of recent increases, and policy language still committing to active, unconstrained purchases, the balance sheet is very likely to keep expanding into next month-end, though the pace of the most recent 4-week jump was extraordinary and may moderate somewhat from this peak rate.
Would change its mind if: A statement announcing tapering/reduction of purchase pace, or a print showing assets flat or declining despite open-ended purchase language, would reverse this call.
Leaned on: fed_assets, assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_52w_high, assets_below_52w_high

=== curve_slope ===
Call: up, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  Prior call was "flat" at 0.45 conviction; the last two prints (0.43→0.44) confirm this was reasonable near-term, though the broader trend is more constructive for steepening than "flat" captures. The 10-observation series shows a slight uptrend from a 0.38 trough back to 0.43-0.44, and the recent path (0.43, 0.42, 0.43, 0.44) is essentially range-bound with a mild positive drift. 21-day change is slightly negative (-0.03pp), but 63-day (+0.28pp) and 252-day (+0.20pp) changes are solidly positive, showing the dominant multi-month trend is steepening. Slope sits at 0.67 of its 12-month range (low -0.04, high 0.68), well above the midpoint, meaning the curve has already un-inverted substantially and is not near either extreme trigger level I set previously (0.55 high, 0.35 low). With no fresh break through either threshold and the last two prints ticking up, I lean toward continued modest steepening rather than flat, but the very small 21-day move keeps conviction limited. The Fed holding rates near zero with asset purchases continuing is consistent with anchored front-end yields, which mechanically supports further curve steepening if long yields drift up.
Would change its mind if: A drop back below 0.40pp with confirming multi-day momentum, or a push above 0.55pp, would clarify direction and raise conviction.

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: down, conviction 0.45
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "up" call was correct through the peak (0.31) but the latest print shows the series has now turned down for two consecutive weeks (0.31 → 0.29 → 0.24), and nfci_change_4w has decayed to just +0.06 from a much larger prior pace, consistent with the stall/reversal condition I set as my falsifier. This looks like the tightening impulse cresting: the index went from deeply negative (-0.62) to a 52-week high of +0.31 over 13 weeks, an enormous move, but the last two observations show conditions easing off that peak. The policy language shows the Fed deploying massive credit-support tools (expanded Treasury/MBS purchases, discount window, swap lines) explicitly aimed at easing financial conditions and restoring market functioning — this is exactly the kind of intervention that would arrest and reverse a tightening spiral. Given the index is still far above zero (+0.24, in the 92nd percentile of its range), conditions remain historically tight, but the momentum has clearly shifted from tightening to at least stabilizing/easing. I now lean toward continued mild easing or flat-to-down over the next month as the policy response works through, though the extreme starting level means conditions stay tight in absolute terms even as direction reverses.
Would change its mind if: A resumption of upward weekly prints back toward 0.31 or nfci_change_4w turning meaningfully positive again would signal renewed tightening.
Leaned on: nfci_change_4w, nfci_distance_from_zero, nfci_range_position

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: down, conviction 0.45
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The previous "down" call is now contradicted by the latest print: breakeven_10y rose from 1.14 to 1.07... wait, actually the last observation (1.07) is a decline from the prior 1.14. Looking at the full series: 1.01, 0.94, 0.96, 1.06, 1.05, 1.11, 1.17, 1.14, 1.14, 1.07 — there was a clear rally from the 0.94 trough through 1.17, then a pullback over the last two readings to 1.07. The 21-day change is still positive (+0.20pp), reflecting the earlier rebound, but the most recent print breaks that uptrend, and the 63-day and 252-day changes remain solidly negative (-0.57pp, -0.89pp), confirming the medium/longer-term trend is still down. Breakevens sit well below the 2% objective (-0.93pp) and in the lower-middle of the 12-month range (0.39 position), well off the highs. Policy language explicitly notes market-based inflation compensation "has declined," reinforcing the disinflationary market read. My prior down call looks directionally correct on the multi-month trend, though the intra-period bounce was real; the latest datapoint's reversal lower supports resuming the down thesis rather than a durable rebound.
Would change its mind if: A sustained move back above 1.15-1.20 over two consecutive readings would indicate the recent pullback was noise, not renewed downward momentum.
Leaned on: be_change_63d, be_change_252d, be_distance_from_target, be_range_position

=== inflation ===
Call: down, conviction 0.55
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 15 days ago.
Report:
  The previous "down" call was directionally correct and then some: headline CPI y/y collapsed from 1.49% to 0.31%, driven by two consecutive sharply negative mom prints (-0.45%, -0.79%) that look like a demand/energy shock rather than gradual disinflation. Given the falsifier I set (mom rebound above +0.20% with core PCE holding above 1.65%) was clearly not met — mom is deeply negative and core PCE actually dropped to 1.53%, its distance from target widening to -0.47pp — this looks like a genuine, severe deceleration event, not noise. However, the magnitude of the last two mom prints (-0.79% in particular) is extreme and likely reflects a one-off shock (collapsing energy prices) that mechanically depresses y/y for a few more months but is unlikely to repeat at the same intensity. The 3m annualized headline of -4.69% is not sustainable; base effects from this collapse will keep y/y low next month, but the mom drops are too large to compound identically. Core PCE momentum (3m ann +1.19%) is comparatively stable, suggesting underlying persistent inflation hasn't fully collapsed — the headline move is being amplified by a component shock. Policy language shift toward "weathered recent events" corroborates a disruptive, not ordinary, disinflation episode. Net: another modest y/y decline is likely as the shock continues to work through, though the pace of decline should moderate.
Would change its mind if: A headline mom print back above 0.00% combined with core PCE stabilizing at/above 1.55% would signal the shock is fading rather than deepening further.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, headline_mom, headline_3m_annualized, core_pce_yoy, core_3m_annualized, core_yoy_change_3m, headline_yoy_change_3m

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: up, conviction 0.35
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 22 days ago.
Report:
  The prior call (up, 0.45) badly understated what happened: unemployment exploded from 3.50% to 4.40% and then to 14.80% in the latest print — an unprecedented, sudden collapse in labor market tightness. This is not a gradual Sahm-rule-style deterioration; it is a shock discontinuity (12m change +11.1pp, sahm_gap +4.07pp, range_position at 1.0, distance from natural rate +10.8pp). Such a violent, non-linear jump strongly suggests a mass layoff/shutdown-type event rather than typical business-cycle loosening. For the next monthly report, the key question is whether this level persists, extends further, or partially reverses. Historically, shock-driven unemployment spikes of this magnitude tend to keep rising for at least one more reading before stabilizing or reversing, as labor market disruptions typically take multiple months to fully register and any recovery is gradual. Given the sheer velocity and magnitude of this move, and no evidence yet of stabilization, I lean toward further deterioration or at best a plateau at this elevated level. The extreme jump makes further increase somewhat more likely than an immediate reversal, though the magnitude of continued increase is highly uncertain.
Would change its mind if: A next print back toward 8-10% or lower would signal rapid labor market recovery; a print above 15-16% would confirm continued severe deterioration.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_12m, sahm_gap, unrate_range_position, unrate_distance_from_natural, unrate_12m_low

=== term_premium ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.50
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 10y yield remains pinned near the bottom of its 12-month range (0.64% latest, range_position 0.05, only 10bp above the 12m low of 0.54%). The previous flat call was roughly correct: yields have oscillated in a narrow 0.58-0.67% band over the last 10 readings, with no directional breakout. Longer-horizon momentum (63d -0.93pp, 252d -1.90pp) still reflects the earlier collapse, but the 21d change is essentially flat at -0.06pp, showing the move has stalled rather than continuing. Policy language shifted from a specific, capped purchase commitment ($500bn Treasuries/$200bn MBS "over coming months") to open-ended, uncapped purchases "in the amounts needed to support smooth market functioning" — this is arguably a more aggressive, flexible commitment to suppress long-end yields, reinforcing the low-yield equilibrium rather than signaling withdrawal. The 30s10s slope at 0.64pp is not unusually steep, suggesting no acute long-end-specific pressure from duration extension or issuance fear. With no evidence of balance-sheet runoff or flight-to-quality reversal, and purchases still open-ended, I see no catalyst to push the term premium higher into month-end. The signal remains one of stabilization at low levels rather than any renewed upward pressure.
Would change its mind if: A move back above ~1.00% with range_position rising above 0.20 would indicate the suppressive impulse has reversed and term premium is climbing again.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_range_position, dgs10_above_12m_low, slope_30s10s

=== What the panel says it was never handed ===
  labor_tightness lacks financial_conditions — Whether credit/financial stress is intensifying would indicate if the labor shock is deepening or stabilizing.
  labor_tightness lacks balance_sheet — Scale of policy support could affect how quickly firms rehire or continue layoffs.
  term_premium lacks balance_sheet — Actual pace/composition of Treasury purchases would clarify how much suppression is ongoing vs. planned taper.
  term_premium lacks financial_conditions — Broader market stress or easing would show if flight-to-quality demand for long bonds is changing.

arbitrated.notes: My seat is 2s10s slope only. Four relevant inputs: curve_slope itself (direct measurement, up-drift, conviction 0.35), term_premium (flat, pinned near 12m lows, no long-end breakout), balance_sheet (sharply expanding, uncapped purchases), and labor_tightness (shock deterioration, front-end anchoring via Fed reaction function). The curve_slope analyst's own read is the most direct evidence: despite calling "flat" last time, the 63d/252d trend is solidly steepening and the level (0.67 of range) is well off inversion extremes, with two straight upticks. That's a genuine, if modest, steepening signal. Term_premium's own view is "flat" for the 10y outright, but flat long-end yields combined with a front end pinned near zero by policy (open-ended purchases, extreme labor shock keeping Fed on hold indefinitely) is itself consistent with a curve that steepens on net — the front end has essentially no room to fall further while long-end term premium has more optionality to drift up over time as the massive balance-sheet expansion eventually raises reflation/duration-supply concerns, even if not yet visible in the 21d term-premium print. The labor shock (unemployment spike to 14.8%) is unambiguously a front-end anchor: it locks in "lower for longer" policy, pinning DGS2 near the floor, which mechanically supports steepening even if long yields merely stay flat rather than rising. Balance-sheet expansion is a two-sided input for term premium (suppresses long yields via purchases but also signals eventual reflation risk) — I weight it as mildly steepening on net given the "uncapped, needs-based" language which historically has preceded eventual curve steepening once stress ebbs. Financial conditions/inflation don't map cleanly onto slope and are already reflected via term_premium and labor readings, so I don't double count them. Net: modest steepening bias, moderate conviction given the front-end anchor is very high conviction while the long-end catalyst is weak/flat.

A flight-to-quality shock from renewed labor/financial stress could pull long yields down as much as short, flattening the curve.
Term premium's own flat call is only mildly supportive; steepening thesis is thinner than front-end anchor alone suggests.
Labor shock could partially reverse quickly, removing the front-end anchor and allowing 2y yields to drift up, flattening the curve.

trade.rationale: Modest steepener: long DGS10/short DGS2 exposure via equal-opposite weights, expressing front-end anchored by policy/labor shock against a slowly steepening long end, net of level.

why.curve_slope: Direct measurement: multi-month uptrend (63d/252d positive), two consecutive upticks, no reversal signal despite near-term flatness.
why.term_premium: 10y pinned at range lows, essentially flat 21d; slight lean toward eventual mild upward drift given open-ended purchase risk, but no confirmed breakout.

why.labor_tightness: Deferring to analyst's own reading: unemployment shock (own measurement) still rising/plateauing; used here only for its front-end policy-anchoring implication.

user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: up, conviction 0.85
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The previous call (up, 0.85) was correct: fed_assets rose again to 7,097,316 from 7,037,258, continuing an uninterrupted expansion. The 4-week change is still strongly positive (+441,387) and the 13-week change is enormous (+2,938,679, +70.66%), reflecting the initial massive QE ramp. The level sits exactly at the 52-week high with zero drawdown, confirming no pause in accumulation. Policy language has shifted from the earlier fixed-minimum commitment ($500B Treasuries/$200B MBS plus reinvestment) to an open-ended "purchase in the amounts needed to support smooth market functioning" formulation — this is not a tapering signal; it's an unlimited/open-ended QE commitment, arguably even more accommodative in framing since it removes a fixed ceiling. There is no language suggesting any reduction, cap, or reinvestment-only mode. Given the magnitude of recent weekly increases and the reaffirmed (indeed expanded) open-ended purchase commitment, the balance sheet should continue to expand into next month-end, though the pace of the most recent 4-week gain, while still large, is somewhat below the peak ramp implied by the 13-week average. I maintain a strong up call, slightly tempered by the possibility of the growth rate itself decelerating from the initial surge.
Would change its mind if: A statement announcing tapering/reduction of purchase pace, or a print showing assets flat or declining despite open-ended purchase language.
Leaned on: fed_assets, assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_52w_high, assets_below_52w_high

=== curve_slope ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.25
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last print of 0.49pp came after a small pullback from a recent 0.53, continuing the choppy, range-bound pattern this series has shown all along (0.48→0.55→...→0.53→0.49). My prior "up" call was not well supported by this outcome — the spread finished at the same 0.49 level it had already touched twice before, essentially flat over the last several observations rather than resuming a steepening trend. That said, the broader medium-term trend remains modestly steepening: slope_change_63d (+0.22pp) and slope_change_252d (+0.35pp) are both solidly positive, and the spread sits at 0.74 of its 12-month range (well above the 12-month low of -0.04pp), historically an elevated position. The 21-day change is only +0.06pp, indicating recent momentum has stalled near-term even as the multi-month uptrend persists. Policy language shows the Fed holding at the zero lower bound and continuing QE purchases, which structurally anchors the front end and typically supports gradual steepening from curve-flattening/inversion regimes, but nothing here signals a fresh near-term catalyst for the next month. Given the flat last few prints within a 0.49-0.55 band and no clear breakout in either direction, I'll shade toward a mild steepening lean but with reduced conviction versus last time, since the immediate action has not confirmed the call.
Would change its mind if: A break below 0.45pp with two consecutive days confirming, or a push clearly above 0.55pp, would clarify direction.
Leaned on: slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d, slope_change_252d, slope_range_position, slope_12m_low

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: down, conviction 0.60
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The NFCI series has now fallen for six straight weeks, from a peak of 0.31 down to -0.21, confirming the "down" call made last time. The 4-week change of -0.36 shows strong easing momentum, and the index has now crossed below zero into looser-than-average territory (distance_from_zero -0.21). The 13-week change is still positive (+0.23) reflecting the earlier spike, but that is stale relative to the recent sharp reversal. Policy language has shifted decisively toward extraordinary support: large-scale asset purchases (at least $500bn Treasuries, $200bn MBS), discount window/intraday credit measures, and swap-line coordination. This is the kind of forceful, multi-pronged liquidity injection that historically drives NFCI sharply lower over the following weeks, as it directly targets market functioning and credit flow stress that had been pushing the index up. Given the acceleration of easing (each of the last six prints has declined, with the pace not yet decelerating) and the newly announced aggressive facilities whose effects are still being absorbed into the index, I expect continued downward movement into month-end, though the magnitude of further decline may moderate as conditions normalize.
Would change its mind if: A reversal in weekly prints back toward positive territory, or nfci_change_4w turning positive, would signal renewed tightening and change this view.
Leaned on: nfci_change_4w, nfci_distance_from_zero, nfci_52w_high, nfci_range_position

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last 10 breakevens are 1.09, 1.17, 1.15, 1.16, 1.14, 1.12, 1.13, 1.14, 1.18, 1.15 — the most recent print (1.15) sits mid-range and the last few readings (1.13, 1.14, 1.18, 1.15) show stabilization/mild bounce rather than continued decline. My prior "down" call was directionally consistent with the 63d and 252d trends (-0.26pp, -0.58pp), but the very recent 21d change is now +0.01pp, essentially flat, and the last two prints (1.18, 1.15) sit at or above the 1.15-1.20 band I flagged as my falsifier for the down thesis. That suggests the sharp pullback has lost momentum and is consolidating rather than accelerating further down. The level remains well below the 2% objective (-0.85pp), and policy language confirms market-based inflation compensation has declined versus survey measures unchanged — consistent with the medium-term downtrend being real, not just noise. But for the one-month-ahead call, the near-term signal (21d flat, recent uptick to 1.18 before settling to 1.15) argues against further near-term declines. I'm shifting off "down" toward "flat," acknowledging the longer trend remains soft but the immediate momentum has stalled.

Given the depth of the shock, one more month of low or slightly lower headline y/y seems more likely than a snapback, though the deceleration in the pace of decline tempers conviction of further big drops.
