However, the direction this month is down, not up — a meaningful decline from the peak. Given the extreme volatility just witnessed (job losses of this magnitude are unprecedented in this series), and with policy language still describing an unfolding crisis with "considerable risks," it's plausible the rate could still move either direction sharply. But the immediate print-over-print momentum has turned downward from the 14.8 peak, and reopening-type dynamics after an acute shock often produce further partial reversals in the following month. I'll lean toward continued modest improvement (down) rather than renewed deterioration, though with low-moderate conviction given the extreme uncertainty and possibility of a second wave of job losses.
Would change its mind if: A print back above 14-15% would confirm renewed severe deterioration; a print near 12% or lower would confirm continued recovery.

arbitrated.notes:
This is a curve-slope call, so I need to net out front-end vs long-end pressures rather than take the level. The dedicated curve_slope analyst (2s10s itself) reports the spread flat at 0.49pp, having stalled after touching this level twice before, with only a mild medium-term steepening bias (63d/252d positive) but zero near-term momentum (21d +0.06pp) — conviction reduced to 0.25 from prior. That's the most direct read and I weight it heavily.

Cross-checking with front-end and long-end drivers: term_premium (long end, DGS10) is pinned near cycle lows (0.65%, range_position 0.06) with negligible 21d move and explicitly says no catalyst for a move either direction — this argues for continued long-end anchoring, not a steepening breakout. Balance_sheet is expanding aggressively and open-endedly, which is a QE/suppression story that keeps the long end pinned (bullish for long-end price, i.e., caps yields there) while the front end is already at zero and can't fall further — mechanically this dynamic is flattening-to-stable, not steepening, since it's compressing the long end toward the anchored front end. Labor_tightness (unemployment falling from extreme peak, still down but off the top) and inflation cooling sharply both argue for continued extraordinary accommodation (front end pinned near zero for a long horizon), reinforcing an anchored front end. None of these give me a clear steepening catalyst — if anything, the open-ended purchase commitment is a fresh long-end suppression signal that argues against the modest steepening lean the slope analyst still holds.

Netting this out: I don't see enough evidence for either a confirmed steepening breakout or a flattening move. The slope series itself is genuinely rangebound, and the long-end suppression story (balance sheet) roughly offsets the medium-term steepening drift. I'll hold a very low-conviction flat/marginal-flattening lean, size the trade small, and treat this largely as a no-strong-view stance.

discounted.why: Front-end policy already at zero bound; labor print doesn't shift near-term slope dynamics meaningfully.

arbitrated.falsifier: A confirmed break of the 2s10s spread above 0.55pp or below 0.45pp for two consecutive prints would establish clear directional slope momentum.

risks.text: Aggressive open-ended QE could eventually suppress long end faster than front end, flattening more than expected.
A shift in Fed purchase composition toward longer maturities would flatten the curve independent of level moves.
Rangebound slope series may be masking a coiled breakout in either direction given historically elevated range_position.

trade.rationale: Small, low-conviction steepener sized to the residual mild steepening drift in curve_slope, offset by term_premium's long-end anchoring; not a strong directional slope call.

why.curve_slope: Spread stalled at 0.49pp after touching it twice; near-term momentum flat, medium-term steepening drift intact but stale, so scaled down conviction.
why.term_premium: DGS10 pinned near lows, no catalyst either way; slight flat-to-lower lean, dampens the steepening case for the long end.
why.balance_sheet: Own measurement (fed_assets) clearly rising, open-ended QE language; taken at face value from analyst, unchanged.

My prior "flat" call at 0.15 conviction was too cautious — the break above the 0.75-0.80 zone I flagged as a trigger has now happened, with the level pushing to a new local high and range_position at 0.25 of the 12-month range (still low in absolute terms given the 12m high of 1.94%, but the direction of travel over the last two prints is clearly higher). The 10s30s slope remains steep at 0.77pp, consistent with continued long-end richness relative to the very front end but also compatible with ongoing upward drift in the 10y itself as term premium normalizes off cycle lows. Policy language still commits to at-least-current-pace Treasury purchases, which caps how much this can run, but the reiterated "inflation moderately above 2%" language signals tolerance for a somewhat higher long-end path without pushback. Given the two-month uptrend and the fresh high in the 10-print window, I lean toward continued modest upward drift into month-end rather than reversion, though the move is not yet extreme and could stall given the still-low range position and ongoing QE purchases anchoring the belly/long end.
Would change its mind if: A reversal back below 0.80 with range_position falling would signal the uptrend has stalled; a push toward 0.55-0.60 would confirm renewed anchoring.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_range_position, slope_30s10s

=== What the panel says it was never handed ===
  term_premium lacks balance_sheet — Actual pace/composition of purchases would clarify how much long-end supply pressure is being offset.
  term_premium lacks financial_conditions — Broader financial conditions gauge would show if long-end rise reflects easing or tightening backdrop.

arbitrated.notes:
The direct read comes from curve_slope itself: 2s10s has broken to a 12-month high (0.74pp), with accelerating momentum across 21d/63d/252d windows and confirmation from a widening 10s30s slope. This is a genuine bear-steepening move — dgs10 up ~0.88% vs dgs2 up only 0.14% — not a parallel shift. term_premium corroborates this from the long end: 10y yields breaking to fresh local highs, up 0.19pp/21d and 0.33pp/63d, which is the mechanical driver of the steepening. Both drivers agree on direction and reinforce rather than conflict, since term_premium is describing the same long-end move that curve_slope's own dgs10 input captures — no double-counting risk to the slope call since they're consistent, not independent confirmations of level.

Front-end anchoring is corroborated indirectly: policy language (average inflation targeting, low-for-long) keeps the 2yr pinned, and labor_tightness (unemployment falling, tightening labor market) would normally pull front-end yields up, but stays fresh and consistent with continued gradual improvement rather than a snap toward hikes — the Fed's explicit patience framework blunts front-end pass-through. balance_sheet's continued QE (up call) also caps how much the long end can steepen but hasn't been enough to arrest the recent breakout, per term_premium's own read.

I take an up (steepening) view on curve_slope with high-moderate conviction, leaning primarily on the curve_slope analyst directly, reinforced by term_premium's long-end confirmation. Labor_tightness and balance_sheet are useful context on why front-end is anchored but don't independently flip the slope call. financial_conditions and inflation_expectations are flat and largely non-slope-relevant here, so I set them aside as no-view. This is now a breakout, not consolidation, and both fresh long-end and front-end reads reinforce it.

discounted.why: Front-end level driver, doesn't independently resolve slope direction beyond anchoring context.
QE pace is a level/supply factor, used only as context on steepening ceiling, not a slope signal itself.

arbitrated.falsifier: A sustained break of slope_2s10s back below ~0.65pp over two sessions, or dgs10 reversing toward 0.55-0.60 while dgs2 holds, would flip this to flat/flattening.

risks.text: A broad risk-off rally could pull dgs10 down sharply, masking as flattening even though front end is anchored by policy.
A sudden Fed shift toward yield-curve-control or purchase reallocation to long end could cap the steepening abruptly.
term_premium's own range_position is still low (0.25 of 12m), so the breakout could stall without confirmation.
Bear-steepening trades are consensus; a crowded positioning unwind could cause a sharp reversal in slope.

trade.rationale: Steepener: short front-end yield exposure, long long-end yield exposure, equal-opposite so a parallel shift nets to zero and only slope P&L remains, per curve_slope and term_premium's bear-steepening breakout.

why.curve_slope: Direct measurement of 2s10s at fresh 12m high, accelerating across all windows; primary read, taken near face value.
why.term_premium: Long-end 10y breakout confirms the mechanical driver of steepening; corroborates rather than duplicates curve_slope.
why.labor_tightness: Unemployment falling = labor tightening, but Fed patience language blunts front-end pass-through, supporting continued anchoring.
why.balance_sheet: Continued QE caps long-end pressure somewhat but hasn't stopped the breakout; context for pace of steepening, not reversal.

However core PCE y/y actually rose (1.48→1.42 is the last two prints... let me be precise: core PCE went 1.39→1.48→1.42, so it dipped slightly in the newest reading after rising). Momentum signals are mixed: headline_mom ticked up to 0.23 from 0.12, and headline_3m_annualized (2.48%) still runs above the y/y rate (1.18%), implying underlying pull upward if sustained. Core_3m_annualized (2.19%) also exceeds core y/y (1.42%), reinforcing that recent monthly prints are firmer than the trailing year-over-year figure, which is still anchored by base effects from the earlier collapse (0.20-0.31% lows). Core_yoy_change_3m is positive (+0.21pp), showing core has been reaccelerating over the past quarter even though headline y/y slipped one-tenth. Policy language softened slightly, attributing weakness to "earlier declines" in oil rather than "significantly lower" oil prices — a subtle disinflationary-drag deceleration, consistent with fading base effects turning less favorable to disinflation, which points toward higher y/y readings ahead. Given the tug between a soft headline y/y print and firmer 3m-annualized momentum in both headline and core, I lean toward a modest reacceleration next month, but with real uncertainty given the ambiguous core reading.
Would change its mind if: A third consecutive headline y/y decline alongside core_3m_annualized falling back below the y/y rate would confirm momentum has genuinely faded, not just paused.

Call: down, conviction 0.45

Report:

The trend remains firmly one of labor-market tightening (falling unemployment) on a 3m (-1.70pp) and 6m (-6.50pp) basis, though the pace of improvement has decelerated markedly compared to earlier months (the initial drops were much larger). The level is still 2.70pp above the ~4% natural rate and the Sahm-gap of +3.63pp remains large, indicating the labor market is still slack in absolute terms even as it heals. Policy language reinforces this: activity and employment "have continued to recover" (upgraded from "picked up"), consistent with steady, ongoing improvement rather than stalling. There's no evidence of reversal — no uptick, no flattening at 6.9% or above. Given the still-strong month-over-month cadence of declines (though decelerating), the most likely near-term outcome is another modest decline in the unemployment rate, continuing the recovery trajectory, but I temper conviction given the deceleration signals the low-hanging-fruit phase of the recovery may be ending.
Would change its mind if: A print flat or above 6.7% would signal the recovery has stalled; any reversal back toward 7.5%+ would indicate renewed deterioration.

The 21d change is now only +0.05pp and 63d +0.10pp, both modest, while the 252d change remains sharply negative (-0.90pp), confirming the dominant multi-month trend is still one of a compressed term premium, sitting only 0.32pp above the 12-month low and at a range_position of 0.23 — firmly in the bottom quartile of the past year's range. The 30s10s and 10s2s slopes are steady and unremarkable, showing no acceleration in long-end cheapening. Fed language reiterating ongoing asset purchases "at least at the current pace" continues to support a compressed long-end premium, capping upside. Given the flattening of recent momentum and the yield's continued proximity to its lows, I no longer see a clear directional edge for the next month; the brief uptick appears to have petered out near 0.84-0.88 without follow-through.
Would change its mind if: A clear break above 0.90 with range_position rising toward 0.30+ would confirm renewed upward pressure; a drop toward 12m low would confirm renewed anchoring.

Momentum remains positive across all horizons shown (+0.18pp over 21d, +0.35pp over 63d, +0.24pp over 252d), and the series has been grinding higher in small steps rather than spiking, suggesting a persistent rather than transitory repricing of inflation expectations. The level is now essentially at the Committee's 2% longer-run objective (distance -0.01pp), consistent with policy language aiming for inflation "moderately above 2 percent for some time" so that expectations remain anchored — market pricing is validating that framework rather than signaling de-anchoring in either direction. With breakeven at the top of its 12-month range and no evidence of stalling (no negative 21d reading, no reversal below prior lows), the trend is intact. The one caution is that being at the top of the range and essentially at target could invite mean-reversion or a pause, so I don't push conviction to the top of the scale, but the weight of evidence still favors continued modest upward drift over the next month rather than reversal.
Would change its mind if: A drop back toward the low-1.9s with negative 21d change, or stalling flat for multiple prints, would signal the uptrend has topped out.

  The prior call (up, 0.35) was correct: headline y/y ticked up from 1.18 to 1.32, continuing the recovery off the 0.20 trough. Momentum remains constructive. The core slope call rests on curve_slope itself, which shows a clean, broad-based steepening: slope_2s10s at new 12m/range highs, positive momentum across 21d/63d/252d, and — critically — the move is decomposed as dgs10 +1.11% vs dgs2 +0.11%, i.e. almost entirely long-end driven. Two independently-sourced analysts converging on "long end doing the work" is the strongest signal on the panel for slope durability, even though term_premium's own conviction (0.45) is moderate and it flags mean-reversion risk after a choppy run.

Front-end drivers reinforce rather than contradict this: labor_tightness continuing to improve (unemployment down) and inflation drifting up together argue for eventual policy normalization pressure, but policy language on both fronts remains anchored at zero with continued purchases — front end stays pinned by design. Balance sheet expansion continues at pace but doesn't obviously map to slope (its effect is more a level/term-premium suppressant); I don't lean on it directly since curve_slope's own long-end decomposition already captures the relevant transmission, and its supply-composition detail is missing anyway (an acknowledged gap). Financial conditions are also missing from curve_slope's view but that data shows easing decelerating to flat, i.e. it's neutral-to-slightly-supportive of continued risk-on/steepening, not a headwind — so it doesn't override the steepening read.

Net: steepener, driven by long end selling off faster than front end, with moderate-high conviction given cross-confirmation but tempered by term_premium's own caveat on choppiness and possible near-term consolidation.

arbitrated.notes:
My seat is 2s10s slope only. The curve_slope analyst itself is the most direct read: the spread is at 1.58pp, essentially a 12-month high, with strong, consistent steepening momentum across 21d/63d/252d windows and confirming context from 10s30s (+0.67pp). That is a genuine, well-evidenced steepening signal, though decelerating near the range ceiling — hence moderate rather than high conviction.

Cross-checking against the other drivers for front-vs-long balance: term_premium shows the 10y at a 12-month high with strong multi-horizon upward momentum (long end pushing higher), which is a steepening force on its own. Labor_tightness continues to improve (unemployment falling), albeit decelerating — a tightening labor market that would normally pressure the front end via rate-hike expectations, which is a flattening force, but the deceleration in the pace of improvement suggests this pressure is easing, not accelerating, reducing front-end upward pressure. Balance_sheet is unambiguously still in full QE expansion (fresh 52-week highs in assets, no taper language), which caps front-end yields hard via continued short-duration liquidity support while doing much less to constrain the long end supply/inflation dynamics driving term premium and breakevens (also freshly at highs). This asymmetry — anchored front end via QE, unanchored long end via rising term premium/breakevens/inflation reacceleration — reinforces rather than contradicts the curve_slope analyst's own steepening read.

I therefore side with the steepening call, leaning primarily on curve_slope's direct measurement, corroborated by term_premium and balance_sheet's implications for front-end anchoring. I discount labor_tightness's flattening implication given its explicitly decelerating pace, and treat financial_conditions/inflation as level-only, no differential slope information.

raw_response:
{"notes": "The curve_slope analyst itself flags a stall: 2s10s at 1.44, down from 1.47, with negative 21d momentum (-0.05pp) even as 63d/252d trends remain positive but decelerating. That's a genuine, fresh, own-driver signal and I weight it heavily. The question is whether front-end or long-end forces resolve the stall into renewed steepening or into flattening.

Front end: labor_tightness shows a clean, accelerating improvement (unemployment 5.80% vs 6.10%, fresh 12m low, 3m/6m changes both negative and accelerating) plus inflation running hot (headline CPI yoy 4.92%, core PCE 3.13%, momentum still building). Together these are the strongest, most recent, highest-conviction signals in the panel and point toward the Fed being pulled toward less accommodation — that pressure should show up first at the front end as the market prices in eventual removal of the extraordinary "lower for longer" language, tightening front-end yields relative to the long end. That's a flattening force.

Long end: term_premium is weak and equivocal (0.25 conviction, last two prints down, near-term momentum negative) even though the medium-term trend is up. Balance_sheet remains expansionary with no taper signal (conviction 0.55, still committed to $120B/month), which caps long-end term premium and keeps duration supply/demand from pushing yields up disproportionately at the long end. === balance_sheet ===
Call: up, conviction 0.65

  The measurement confirms the prior call: total assets rose again, now at a new 52-week high (8,101,945), up ~198bn over 4 weeks, ~382bn over 13 weeks, and ~4.95% over the quarter. This is a clean, uninterrupted expansion trend with no deceleration visible in the last few prints (each of the last several observations is higher than the prior). The policy language is unchanged and explicitly commits to continued purchases of at least $80bn/month Treasuries and $40bn/month MBS "until substantial further progress" — i.e., still active QE with no taper signaled. The pace of the 4-week change (~198bn) is roughly consistent with, if not slightly above, the stated $120bn/month combined pace, supporting continued mechanical expansion. There is no evidence of runoff, caps, or reinvestment-only language. Given the level is already at a fresh high and the statement gives no hint of tapering, the base case for month-end is a continuation of expansion at a similar pace. This was the correct call last time and the evidence still supports it undiminished.
Would change its mind if: A statement shift to tapering/reducing the monthly purchase pace, or a sustained multi-release decline in fed_assets, would overturn this view.

The last three prints (1.26, 1.24, 1.22, 1.20) show a fairly steady downward drift rather than noise around a level, which is a flattening signal. The spread sits at 0.67 of its 12-month range — still comfortably above the 12-month low (0.41 above it) — so this isn't inversion or even close, but the trend over the past one to three months is unambiguously toward compression. The 10s30s slope at 0.61 pp remains a positively-sloped long end, giving no signal of imminent front-driven inversion pressure bleeding into 2s10s. Given the persistent short-and-medium-term flattening momentum against a still-early stage in the range, I lean toward continued modest flattening into month-end rather than a stabilization, reversing my prior flat call.

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The NFCI series has essentially flattened at -0.69 for the last five readings after a gradual decline from -0.65. The 4-week change is exactly 0.00, indicating momentum has stalled, and the 13-week change of -0.05 reflects easing that has already fully played out and stopped rather than an ongoing trend. The index sits near its 52-week low (nfci_above_52w_low = 0.00) and range_position of 0.01, meaning conditions are as loose as they've been in a year, but there's no further easing momentum visible — the series has bottomed out at this level for six consecutive prints. Policy language remains unchanged, reaffirming accommodative conditions but not signaling a new easing impulse. My prior call of "down" was right through the flattening period but the driver has now clearly stopped moving. With zero recent momentum and the index pinned at a floor, the most defensible call is flat: conditions are historically loose but not currently loosening further, and there's no evidence of imminent tightening either. This is a case where the easing trend has exhausted itself at a plateau, and continuation of that plateau is the best single-month expectation absent new catalysts.

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.40

Report:
  Breakeven 10y has stabilized in a narrow 2.30-2.34 band over the last several prints (2.30, 2.34, 2.33, 2.32, 2.32), after an initial 21d decline that has largely leveled off (be_change_63d only -0.03pp vs -0.14pp at 21d, meaning the drop occurred early and has since flattened). My prior "flat" call was directionally correct: the series did not break down further nor push higher — it settled. Current level sits +0.32pp above the 2% target and in the 82nd percentile of its 12-month range, still elevated versus the 12m low (+0.98pp above it) but well off the 12m high. The policy language tweak (past tense "having run" vs "running") is a subtle signal of the Committee viewing the below-target period as more resolved, but this is a small wording shift and doesn't clearly reflect through to breakeven pricing yet. With three consecutive readings clustered tightly around 2.32-2.33, momentum has essentially stalled — neither the down-break through 2.35ish nor a fresh push above 2.45 has materialized. I see no strong catalyst for a directional move in either direction over the next month; the market's inflation expectations look anchored modestly above target but not trending.

The mom_gap_vs_outgoing of +0.20pp confirms the most recent month accelerated versus its own trailing average rather than decelerating. Policy language shift from "below 2 percent" to "risen, largely reflecting transitory factors" corroborates that inflation has clearly broken above target, though the "transitory" framing suggests officials expect this to fade — that is a forecast I cannot yet confirm from the data, which still shows acceleration in both headline and core. Given the 0.85% outgoing print and rising 3m annualized pace, I expect the next headline yoy print to rise again.

Report:
  The previous call (down/tightening) was wrong: unemployment ticked up from 5.80% to 5.90%, a small but genuine reversal after a long steady decline. The broader trend over 12 months remains a massive improvement (-5.10pp), and the 3m/6m changes are still net negative (-0.20pp, -0.80pp), so this is not a Sahm-rule-style rapid deterioration — sahm_gap is only +0.13pp, well below the 0.5pp threshold that would signal recession-onset loosening. However, the uptick from the 12m low (unrate_above_12m_low +0.10pp) combined with the fact that the prior two prints were flat-then-down (6.10, 6.10, 5.80) before this reversal suggests the rapid tightening phase is stalling as the rate approaches its natural-rate proximity (distance_from_natural still 1.90pp, so there's room to keep improving, but the pace has clearly slowed). Policy language turned more optimistic ("indicators of economic activity and employment have strengthened"), which is consistent with a labor market still recovering, not deteriorating — the language doesn't support further loosening. Given one uptick after months of decline, I read this as a stall/pause rather than a trend reversal, but I no longer have confidence in continued rapid improvement. I lean flat-to-slightly-up for the next print, with low-moderate conviction given the single data point and the strong disinflationary labor trend still intact.
Would change its mind if: A print back at or below 5.80% would confirm continued tightening; a print above 6.1% would confirm a genuine loosening trend reversal.

arbitrated.notes:
This is a curve-slope seat, so I focus on the direct 2s10s driver plus cross-reads that change front-vs-long balance rather than level. The curve_slope analyst itself reports a persistent, multi-print grind lower in 2s10s (1.29→1.20 over recent weeks, 21d -0.26, 63d -0.32), with 10s30s still positively sloped so this isn't front-driven inversion pressure — it's genuine long-end-relative compression. That's a fresh, directly-measured read and gets the most weight.

Cross-checking against level drivers: term_premium (10y) reports a pause/mild downward drift in long-end yields (21d -0.17, 63d -0.24) after a longer rise, explicitly acknowledging it lacks balance_sheet and financial_conditions context. balance_sheet reports continued uninterrupted QE expansion at ~$120bn/month pace with no taper signal — this is a fresh, high-conviction call that argues for continued suppression of long-end term premium via ongoing purchases, reinforcing rather than contradicting the flattening (long end anchored, capping any back-up in 10y). Labor_tightness is flat/stalling with low conviction and is a front-end-relevant driver, but a stalled labor improvement is at most a mild argument against front-end hikes being priced more aggressively — if anything a stalling labor market keeps front-end anchored near zero, which is flattening-neutral rather than steepening. Inflation is accelerating sharply (up 0.72), which could pressure long-end nominal yields higher (steepening risk), but term_premium's own direct reading shows the 10y actually declining despite this, suggesting Fed purchases and transitory framing are currently winning against inflation-driven curve steepening pressure.

Net: the direct slope evidence, corroborated by continued QE anchoring the long end, supports continued modest flattening into month-end, tempered by inflation acceleration as a steepening risk.

arbitrated.falsifier: 2s10s printing back above ~1.26-1.28 sustained over 2+ readings, or 21d slope change turning positive, would flip this to steepening.

Accelerating CPI/core PCE could eventually force long-end yields up faster than front end, reversing flattening.

10s30s could begin compressing too, which would change the read from pure 2s10s grind to broader long-end story.

trade.rationale: Flattener: long DGS2 yield exposure, short DGS10 yield exposure equally weighted, expressing continued 2s10s compression while netting out any parallel shift.

why.term_premium: 10y drifting lower recently, consistent with flattening; acknowledges missing balance-sheet/FCI context, so weighted below direct slope read.
why.labor_tightness: Stalling improvement (uptick to 5.90%) keeps front end anchored near zero, mildly flattening-neutral, not a steepening catalyst.

user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: up, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The previous "up" call was correct: fed_assets rose again to 8,332,743 from 8,342,598... actually note the last print (8,332,743) is slightly below the prior observation (8,342,598), a small -9,855 dip that shows up as assets_below_52w_high. But the broader trend remains clearly expansionary: 4-week change is +111,270, 13-week change is +429,202 (+5.43%), and 52-week change is +1,342,325. This is textbook QE, consistent with ongoing $80B Treasury + $40B MBS monthly purchases. The policy language shift is subtle but notable: it moves from an active present-tense commitment ("will continue to increase") to a retrospective framing ("Last [date] the Committee indicated that it would continue..."), which could be an early signal of a shift toward tapering discussion, though no explicit reduction in pace is announced yet. Given purchases are still nominally in force and the multi-week trend is firmly positive, I expect fed_assets to be higher at next month-end, though I trim conviction slightly given the one-period dip and the language hint that taper conversation may be beginning.
Would change its mind if: An explicit taper announcement reducing the $80B/$40B monthly caps, or two consecutive releases showing fed_assets declining, would overturn this view.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_pct_change_13w, assets_change_52w

=== curve_slope ===
Call: up, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last release printed 1.10, extending a run of stabilization/mild steepening after the 63-day flattening episode. Looking at the 10-point series (1.04→1.02→1.03→1.02→1.05→1.12→1.09→1.09→1.09→1.10), the curve dipped to 1.02 mid-series, spiked to 1.12, then settled in a tight 1.09-1.10 band for the last four readings. My prior call (down, 0.25) was wrong in direction: the spread did not resume flattening — it has held near the top of its recent local range and edged up slightly (21d change +0.07pp). The 63d change (-0.36pp) reflects the earlier flattening move that has since stalled and partially reversed, consistent with my stated falsifier being triggered (sustained readings ≥1.10 for 2+ periods). Range position (0.55) is mid-pack of the 12-month range, not extreme in either direction, and 252-day change is still positive (+0.50pp), suggesting the longer trend is steepening, not flattening. With four consecutive prints clustered at 1.09-1.10 and no fresh directional catalyst in the unchanged policy language, the most defensible call is a slight continuation of the modest upward drift, though the flattening in the low-30s spread and the tight clustering argue against a strong move either way.
Would change its mind if: A drop back below ~1.05 sustained over 2+ readings would signal renewed flattening; a break above 1.15 would confirm steepening acceleration.
Leaned on: slope_2s10s, slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d, slope_change_252d, slope_range_position

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: up, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The NFCI index has been drifting up slowly for several readings: -0.69 flat for a stretch, then a gradual crawl to -0.65 over the last four observations, holding steady at -0.65 for the last three prints. My prior call of "up" was directionally correct — the index did rise, confirming the mild tightening drift rather than stalling back to -0.68/-0.69. However, momentum has now visibly flattened: the last three observations are identical at -0.65, and nfci_change_4w has slowed to +0.02 from what was presumably a slightly faster pace earlier. The index remains deeply negative (-0.65, distance from zero -0.65), still firmly in easy/accommodative territory per the unchanged policy language, and sits only in the lower quarter of its 52-week range (range_position 0.23). The trend is a very gentle grind toward less-easy conditions, but the flattening of the last three prints suggests the drift may be pausing near this level rather than accelerating. Given the plateau, I lean toward continued mild tightening but with reduced conviction versus last time, since the immediate momentum has stalled even as the multi-week trend still points modestly upward.
Would change its mind if: A drop back to -0.67/-0.68 or nfci_change_4w turning negative would signal the drift has reversed toward easing.
Leaned on: nfci, nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_distance_from_zero, nfci_range_position

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: down, conviction 0.40
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The previous "up" call has not played out: breakeven_10y peaked at 2.39, then retreated to 2.37 and 2.33 — the newest print. Both the 21d (-0.04pp) and 63d (-0.11pp) changes are now negative, confirming the near-term momentum has turned down, exactly the falsifier condition I specified (break below 2.35 with negative 21d momentum). The longer 252d change (+0.56pp) shows the broader uptrend from a year ago remains intact, and breakevens still sit at 0.78 of their 12-month range and 0.33pp above the 2% target — so this is a pullback within an elevated regime, not a collapse. But the last two prints (2.37→2.33) are a clear short-term reversal, and there's no fresh policy language suggesting a shift in anchoring. Given the recent two-observation decline and negative short/medium momentum, I expect the near-term drift to continue mildly lower or stabilize just below recent highs at month-end, rather than resume the climb. Conviction is modest since the level remains well above the 12m low and the multi-quarter trend still points up.
Would change its mind if: A rebound back above 2.38-2.40 within the next few readings, with 21d momentum turning positive, would revive the uptrend view.
Leaned on: be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_change_252d, be_range_position, be_distance_from_target

=== inflation ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.30
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 16 days ago.
Report:
  My prior flat call was wrong: headline y/y held near cycle highs (5.15%) but did not decisively turn down, and my stated falsifier has not clearly been met. Headline mom did decelerate to 0.28% in the latest month, following 0.46% before that - two consecutive prints below the 0.53% 3m average, which is the softening I said I'd watch for. However, core PCE y/y kept rising (3.86 -> 3.93), not turning down, so the falsifier for a genuine peak is only half-confirmed. Headline y/y itself is roughly flat over the last three prints (5.30, 5.25, 5.15), a marginal downtick, while core continues to accelerate (core_yoy_change_3m +0.80pp) and remains far above target (core_distance_from_target +1.93pp). The 3m annualized rates (headline 6.55%, core 5.70%) are still running well above the y/y rate, indicating underlying momentum has not genuinely broken even though the single latest month cooled. Policy language still frames inflation as transitory, but the underlying core trend argues against that framing. Given core is the better persistence read and it's still rising, I don't see enough evidence for a down call; but the cooling mom print argues against renewed acceleration too. Net: closer to flat-to-slightly-up, with tempered conviction given the mixed mom deceleration versus core acceleration.
Would change its mind if: Two more consecutive headline mom prints below 0.3% combined with core PCE y/y turning down would confirm a genuine, broadening deceleration.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, core_pce_yoy, headline_mom, headline_3m_annualized, core_3m_annualized, core_yoy_change_3m, headline_mom_3m_avg, outgoing_mom

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: down, conviction 0.55
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 23 days ago.
Report:
  The unemployment rate printed 5.10%, down from 5.40% prior — a continuation of the tightening trend I called last time, and it stayed well under my 5.60-5.70% reversal threshold. The series shows a clear, persistent downtrend: -0.70pp over 3 months, -1.10pp over 6 months, -3.30pp over 12 months, with the rate sitting exactly at its 12-month low (unrate_above_12m_low = 0.00pp, range_position = 0). Sahm-gap is modest at +0.37pp, well below levels that would signal a recessionary turn, and momentum remains firmly in the direction of tightening rather than any stalling. The level is now only 1.10pp above the assumed natural rate (unrate_distance_from_natural), meaning the labor market is closing in on full employment territory. Policy language echoes this, noting that indicators of economic activity and employment have "continued to strengthen." Given the consistency and magnitude of recent declines, and no evidence of deceleration in the pace of improvement, I expect the unemployment rate to fall again or at worst hold near this level at the next report. There's no sign in the data of the kind of rate increase that would flag loosening.
Would change its mind if: A print above 5.30-5.40% would signal stalling; above 5.60% would indicate a genuine reversal toward loosening.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_6m, unrate_change_12m, unrate_12m_low, unrate_above_12m_low, unrate_range_position, sahm_gap, unrate_distance_from_natural

=== term_premium ===
Call: up, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 10y last printed 1.30, up slightly from the prior 1.29 and continuing a choppy but modestly higher path over the last few observations (1.25→1.35→1.34→1.31→1.29→1.30). My prior "down" call at 0.25 conviction was wrong in direction, though the miss was small since the level has stayed range-bound. Looking at momentum: the 21d change is +0.10pp (mildly higher), while 63d is -0.29pp (still net lower over that longer window), and 252d is +0.56pp (substantial rise over the year). The yield sits at 0.60 of its 12-month range, above the midpoint but well below the 1.74 high, so no directional extreme. The 30s10s slope at +0.62pp is unremarkable and doesn't signal an acute steepening/flattening impulse at the long end. Policy language shows only a cosmetic rewording of the same asset-purchase pace (still $80bn/$40bn), not a change in purchase amounts, so no fresh runoff/issuance pressure is evident from this alone. Given the mixed signals — short-term uptick but medium-term net decline, mid-range positioning — I lean toward a modest continuation of the recent slight upward drift rather than a reversal, but the evidence is not strong enough for high conviction.
Would change its mind if: A clear break above 1.40 with renewed positive 21d/63d momentum confirms upward pressure; a break below 1.15 would confirm downward continuation instead.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_range_position, slope_30s10s

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: up, conviction 0.60

Total assets rose again to 8,556,181 from 8,564,943 two observations back is roughly flat, but the broader trend across the 13 observations is clearly upward: 4-week change +108,200M, 13-week change +334,708M (+4.07%), and the series sits at essentially the 52-week high (below by only ~8,762M). The last print did tick down slightly from the immediately preceding observation (8,564,943 → 8,556,181), the first such dip in the recent run, but this is a single-observation wobble against a strong multi-week uptrend and is consistent with normal week-to-week variation in purchase settlement timing rather than a change in policy stance. The policy language remains unchanged: the Committee is still committed to purchasing at least $80B Treasuries and $40B agency MBS per month, with no taper announced. Given the cap-driven mechanical nature of QE, absent an explicit reduction in purchase amounts, the balance sheet should continue expanding into next month-end. I maintain the up call, though I trim conviction slightly given the one-observation pause suggesting the pace may be decelerating or nearing a plateau.
Would change its mind if: An explicit taper announcement reducing the $80B/$40B monthly caps, or two consecutive releases showing fed_assets declining, would overturn this view.

Given the stabilization at 1.04-1.07 for three readings and the tapering signal working against further steepening, I lean toward continued flattening or at best a flat/rangebound spread near current lows.

Inflation_expectations, however, cuts the other way at the margin: breakevens have reversed from 2.69 to 2.51 in three prints, a genuine turn rather than noise. Falling breakevens embedded in nominal 10y yields would cap the long end further and could accentuate flattening (or, if it reflects growth pessimism, could also drag policy expectations down later — but near-term the momentum favors flattening now). Balance_sheet's continued expansion (still buying $80B/month, no taper in caps) keeps a bid under the long end via duration absorption, another modest flattening force, though this driver only speaks to level not slope and I've discounted it heavily as a slope input. Term_premium is genuinely ambiguous — 10y yields stalled/pulled back near-term but longer trend still up — and financial_conditions is flat/uninformative for slope. Net: front end firms on hawkish/tight-labor/hot-inflation expectations while the long end is capped by falling breakevens and continued Fed purchases. This is a flattening call, moderate conviction given curve_slope's own conviction is only 0.35 but corroborated by two independent drivers.

discounted.why: Genuinely ambiguous near-term pullback vs longer uptrend; doesn't clearly tilt front-vs-long balance.
NFCI pinned flat for 12 readings; no slope-relevant signal.
Breakeven reversal noted but folded into long-end cap logic rather than a standalone slope override.

arbitrated.falsifier: A sustained rebound of the 2s10s spread back above 1.15 for 2+ readings, or a stalling/reversal in unemployment declines above 4.7%, would flip this to neutral/steepening.

risks.text: An explicit taper timeline announcement could spike long-end yields faster than front end, reversing the flattening.
Falling breakevens could reflect growth fears that eventually pull front-end policy expectations down too, re-steepening.
Curve_slope's own conviction is only moderate (0.35), so the flattening signal itself is not fully robust.

trade.rationale: Flattener: long front-end yield exposure, short long-end yield exposure, betting 2s rise relative to 10s as tapering/tight-labor/hot-inflation pressure the front end while balance-sheet buying caps the long end.

why.curve_slope: Spread broken from 1.16-1.25 to 1.04-1.07, held 3 readings; front end rising faster than long end under tapering signal.
why.labor_tightness: Unemployment at fresh 12m lows, steady momentum; own measurement falling, implying tighter labor market pressuring front end up, flattening.
why.balance_sheet: Confirmed expansion continues under unchanged caps; own measurement rises, sustaining long-end demand and flattening pressure.

Policy language confirms this isn't yet seen as easing: officials shifted from calling imbalances "transitory" to acknowledging inflation "has exceeded 2 percent for some time," and began tapering asset purchases — a clear acknowledgment of sustained overheating. Core is running far above target (+3.21pp) and the range position is at the top of its 12-month band (1.00). While the deceleration in mom from 0.86 to 0.60 is a mild yellow flag worth watching, it's not enough on its own to break the trend given still-elevated 3m annualized rates and rising y/y in both series. I still expect headline CPI y/y to rise next month.

arbitrated.notes:
The direct 2s10s slope measure itself gives the clearest, freshest signal: the spread has broken to a new 12-month low (0.61), with accelerating flattening momentum (-0.18pp/21d, -0.46pp/63d) and hawkish policy language ("soon appropriate to raise the target range") that historically compresses front-end/belly spreads faster than the long end. That guidance-shift mechanism is exactly a front-end story — it pulls DGS2 up disproportionately versus DGS10 — so it reinforces flattening on the slope axis even though it is bullish for front-end yields specifically, not a parallel-shift level call.

Term premium's "up" call (dgs10 climbing, near range highs, taper reducing long-end demand) is a long-end, rate-level observation. Taken alone it could suggest steepening if the long end is the one moving, but the magnitude here (dgs10 +0.27pp/21d) is modest next to the much larger, more decisive flattening print in curve_slope itself, and 10s30s staying positive (+0.32pp) tells us the pressure is concentrated in the front/belly, not a long-end selloff — consistent with the curve_slope analyst's own read. So term_premium's long-end pressure is real but secondary and does not overturn the flattening thesis; if anything it's already embedded in curve_slope's slope_10s30s check.

Labor tightness (flat, stalling) and inflation (up, hot) both feed the front-end hawkish repricing that curve_slope's policy language reflects — labor stalling near natural rate plus accelerating inflation supports imminent tightening, reinforcing front-end-led flattening rather than contradicting it. Balance sheet (still expanding, tapering) is a slow-moving, longer-horizon support factor for long-end yields, but it's decelerating and mixed (latest tick down), so I discount it as a stale, weak counterweight to the much fresher, sharper flattening print.

Net: I side firmly with flattening, driven by front-end policy repricing, with term premium's modest long-end lift not enough to offset it.

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.40
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead

  The latest print (8,962,474) extends the uptrend: assets rose over the last 4 weeks (+34,345) and 13 weeks (+171,979, +1.96%), and the series sits at its 52-week high with zero drawdown from that high. My prior "up" call was correct — the balance sheet kept expanding through this release. However, the policy language has now shifted materially: the "at least $20bn/$10bn per month" purchase language has been struck, and new language explicitly states the Committee "expects to begin reducing its holdings... at a coming meeting." This is forward guidance for imminent runoff, not yet realized in the data. The measurement series still reflects the tail end of QE (purchases still occurring or just concluding), so the backward-looking pace remains positive. But the "why" has flipped decisively toward tightening, meaning the next month is a transition point — purchases likely stop or reverse before month-end. Given the explicit announcement of a coming shift to balance-sheet reduction, I now expect the growth trajectory to stall or turn flat/down within the next month, even though the most recent hard data still shows expansion. This is a case where language leads the measurement.
Would change its mind if: A subsequent statement announcing a specific runoff cap and start date, or a monthly fed_assets decline, would confirm the shift to down.

Policy language shows the Committee has pivoted to active tightening — raising rates and signaling "ongoing increases" with one dissent favoring an even larger hike — which is a classic driver of front-end-led flattening as the market prices a more aggressive hiking path against a stickier long end. This suggests the flattening pressure is more likely to persist or even push into inversion than to reverse. However, having already fallen to the 12-month low with almost no room left before inversion, the rate of further flattening could slow mechanically, and 10s30s at 0.12 remains a still-positive long-end cushion. On balance I lean toward continued flattening but at reduced conviction versus last time given the two flat readings suggest deceleration.
Would change its mind if: Two+ consecutive readings back above 0.15pp, or slope_change_21d turning positive, would signal flattening has stalled.

arbitrated.notes:
The direct curve_slope read (down/flattening, conv 0.45) is corroborated by the front-end and long-end drivers I'm allowed to use for cross-checks. labor_tightness shows the unemployment rate at a 12-month low with policy language explicitly tightening ("strong labor market," reduced purchases) — this is a classic front-end-pinning force: a tight labor market keeps the Fed hiking, anchoring 2s to the policy path. term_premium shows dgs10 still elevated near range highs but with the last two prints ticking down from the cycle high (2.48→2.35→2.32), momentum decelerating even as the level stays high — so the long end is not accelerating upward the way the front end is being pinned by hikes. That asymmetry (front end pressured by tightening policy/tight labor market vs. long end losing upward momentum) is consistent with continued flattening, not a reversal.

The balance_sheet driver is a wildcard: forward guidance toward runoff should, in isolation, cheapen the long end (less official-sector demand), which would argue for steepening pressure at the margin. But this is forward guidance not yet realized — the analyst's own conviction is modest (0.40) and it's explicitly a future transition, not a current-month fact. I discount its steepening implication as premature and let the already-in-train flattening (slope at 4bp from inversion, 12m low, sharply negative 63d/252d momentum) dominate near-term.

Net: I maintain the flattening call but temper conviction slightly given the deceleration in the last two slope prints (0.04→0.04) and the partially offsetting balance-sheet-runoff signal that could add long-end cheapening later in the horizon. This is a slope call, not a level call — duration stays with the Duration pod.

arbitrated.notes:
The direct curve_slope analyst reports 2s10s at 0.19-0.22pp, still near 12-month lows (15th percentile range position), with the dominant 63d/252d trend firmly flattening (-0.44pp/-1.27pp) despite a fading 2-print bounce. That analyst's own conviction is only 0.35 because the very recent tick could be early reversal noise, but the structural evidence (percentile position, multi-horizon momentum, hiking cycle underway) still points down.

Cross-checking against the front-vs-long drivers: term_premium is the most freshly-formed, highest-conviction (0.55) read in the panel, showing the 10y at a fresh 12-month high with accelerating 21d/63d/252d momentum, explicitly driven by newly-telegraphed balance-sheet runoff — a long-end-specific term-premium impulse. This is a genuine long-end-only force, not a level view I'm borrowing incorrectly; it directly supports further flattening only if the front end rises faster, but if term premium is the mechanism lifting the 10y on its own, it could actually work against flattening at the margin. However, the policy language cited by curve_slope (first hike delivered, more hikes "ongoing," dissent for larger move) is a classic front-end-repricing story that historically dominates early-cycle curve dynamics — front end reprices faster than the term-premium-driven long end can keep pace, producing bear flattening. Labor tightness remains flat at a historic-low unemployment rate, reinforcing that the Fed has room and cause to keep hiking the front end, reinforcing flattening pressure rather than reversing it.

Balance sheet is topping out/plateauing (not yet active runoff), so its long-end lift is prospective, not yet fully priced — a modest offset to flattening but not enough to flip the call given the hiking cycle is already live and mechanically front-loaded. Net: lean flattening, moderate conviction, tempered by the genuine 21d bounce and stretched positioning.

discounted.why: Runoff only telegraphed, not active; prospective long-end effect not yet a live slope driver this month.

arbitrated.falsifier: Three consecutive monthly closes above 0.25pp with slope_change_63d turning positive, or a confirmed runoff cap/date that outweighs front-end hiking pressure.

risks.text: Fed could pause hikes sooner than expected, removing the front-end pressure driving flattening.
21d slope bounce could be the start of a genuine steepening reversal, not noise.
10y already stretched at 12m highs; a reversal in term premium could disproportionately hit the long end.

trade.rationale: Bear-flattener: long 2y yield exposure, short 10y yield exposure, betting front end rises faster than long end as hiking cycle dominates over prospective runoff-driven term premium.

why.curve_slope: Direct measurement: 63d/252d trends firmly down, near 12m low percentile; recent bounce fading, so flattening lean retained.
why.term_premium: 10y at fresh 12m high, accelerating momentum, driven by balance-sheet runoff signal — long-end-specific lift, tempers but doesn't reverse flattening.
why.labor_tightness: Flat at cycle-low unemployment; supports continued hiking cycle (front-end pressure) but no independent slope signal of its own.

A shrinking balance sheet is a long-end supply headwind that pushes term premium and long yields up disproportionately — this is a slope-relevant, front-vs-long asymmetric driver, not a level call. On the front end, labor_tightness remains very tight (unemployment at a fresh cycle low, 3.60%), which argues for continued Fed hiking pressure on the short end — a force that would compress, not expand, the curve. However, the labor call is 23 days stale versus the fresher balance_sheet/term_premium prints, and its own magnitude (0.35 conviction, "flat-to-slightly-down") is modest. Weighing the fresh, corroborating long-end supply story (balance sheet runoff + rising term premium) against a stale but still-present front-end tightening pull, I judge the net slope effect leans mildly toward steepening: the long end has a clearer, fresher, multi-driver catalyst (confirmed runoff) while the front-end tightening is well-priced and decelerating in its own momentum signal. The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: up, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The previous "down" call was wrong for the latest stretch: the last four weekly prints rose from 8,914,281 to 8,918,254 to 8,932,420 to 8,934,346, giving a +20,065 4-week change, even though the 13-week change is still slightly negative (-28,128, -0.31%) and assets sit just below the 52w high. Statement language confirms QT is ongoing ("will continue reducing its holdings"), consistent with a policy-driven downward drift over time, but the recent weekly data show a clear near-term bounce that contradicts a simple linear runoff path — this is typical of temporary balance-sheet noise (e.g., bill settlements, repo/reserve operations, or technical factors) rather than a change in the announced caps. Given the mandate to weight recent pace over level, the last month's actual trajectory is up, not down. I need to acknowledge this reversal rather than repeat the prior call. However, the underlying policy stance is unchanged runoff, so I don't have high confidence this bounce persists through month-end; it could easily resume its slow decline. I'll call this month flat-to-up with modest conviction, favoring up given the immediate 4-week momentum, while flagging that the multi-month trend remains modestly down.
Would change its mind if: A renewed multi-week decline in fed_assets back toward or below the 13w average, or statement language accelerating runoff caps, would revert this to a down call.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_pct_change_13w

=== curve_slope ===
Call: down, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior call (up, 0.35) was wrong: the spread has fallen further, from 0.10 to 0.04 then a small bounce to 0.06pp. The 21-day change is -0.26pp, a fairly sharp flattening impulse, even though the 63-day change is flat at +0.02pp, meaning the flattening is concentrated very recently. The spread sits at just 0.08 on a 0-1 range basis over the past 12 months — near the bottom of its range, only 0.11pp above the 12-month low and far below the 1.29pp high. The curve is barely positive (0.06pp from flat), essentially hugging the inversion boundary. The 10s30s slope at +0.16pp is modestly positive, showing the flattening pressure is concentrated in the belly/front end (2s10s) rather than uniform across the curve. Policy language shows continued aggressive hikes (funds rate raised to 1.5-1.75%, with a dissent for even faster 50bp hikes), which historically compresses the front end and flattens 2s10s further as short rates rise faster than long-term yields. This combination — aggressive front-loaded tightening language plus recent sharp flattening and low range position — points to continued flattening pressure, not steepening. My prior steepening call must be abandoned.
Would change its mind if: A rebound back above 0.15pp with slope_change_21d turning positive would signal the flattening pressure has reversed into steepening.
Leaned on: slope_2s10s, slope_change_21d, slope_range_position, slope_above_12m_low, slope_distance_from_flat, slope_10s30s

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: up, conviction 0.65
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior call (up/tightening, conviction 0.55) has been confirmed and extended. NFCI has risen steadily every single week over this window, from -0.37 to -0.19, a +0.09 move in the last 4 weeks and +0.19 over 13 weeks, with zero interruption or reversal — the exact opposite of the falsifier I set (a print below -0.30 or two consecutive declines). The index is now at its 52-week high and at range_position 1.00, meaning conditions have never been tighter relative to the past year, though the level (-0.19) is still below the zero long-run average, so conditions remain looser than historical norm in absolute terms even as they tighten in trend. Momentum is consistent and monotonic across all 13 observations, which is a strong, unambiguous signal of a persistent easing-to-tightening trajectory (i.e., financial conditions steadily tightening off very loose levels). Given the total absence of any stall or reversal, and the acceleration is even modest but consistent, I expect this trend to continue into month-end. I have no policy language contradicting this. The main risk is mean-reversion after such a persistent one-directional run, but the data give no basis to expect it yet.
Would change its mind if: A weekly print below -0.23 (reversing the recent trend) or two consecutive weekly declines would signal the tightening trend has stalled.
Leaned on: nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_range_position, nfci_above_52w_low

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: down, conviction 0.62
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior call (up, 0.30) was wrong: breakeven_10y has fallen sharply since, from 2.55 to 2.48 to 2.36 to 2.33 — a clean break well below the 2.58 level I set as my falsifier, sustained for three consecutive observations. The trend is now unambiguously down: -0.31pp over 21 days and -0.53pp over 63 days, both accelerating into the most recent print. The series is sitting near its 12-month low (only 0.11pp above it) and in the bottom 14% of its 12-month range (range_position 0.14), having round-tripped from a 3.02% high. The 252-day change is flat, meaning this is a fresh reversal rather than a continuation of a longer downtrend — but the recent momentum is the dominant signal for a one-month horizon. Distance from the 2% target remains positive (+0.33pp), so expectations are still anchored above objective, but the trajectory is clearly toward the target rather than away from it, arguing against any near-term bounce. Policy language is boilerplate and offers no offsetting signal. Given three consecutive declines and no sign of stabilization, I expect breakeven_10y to be lower again at month end.
Would change its mind if: A rebound above 2.45 sustained for 2+ observations would signal stabilization and negate the current downtrend.
Leaned on: be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_range_position, be_above_12m_low

=== inflation ===
Call: up, conviction 0.55
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 15 days ago.
Report:
  My prior "up" call is confirmed by the newest print: headline CPI y/y rose again to 8.98% from 8.54%, and the MoM print jumped to 1.26%, well above the falsifier threshold of 0.4% and above the 3-month average of 0.84%. Headline 3m-annualized is a very hot 10.52%, and yoy_range_position sits at the top of its 12-month range, confirming headline momentum remains strong, not turning. Core PCE y/y, however, continues to soften — 5.18% vs 5.60% two months prior, a -0.42pp 3-month change, with core 3m-annualized at 4.47%, well below headline's pace. This divergence (core_minus_headline at -3.80pp) is mostly energy/food-driven per the still-unchanged policy language citing "higher energy prices" and the invasion's upward pressure on inflation. Since headline is what I'm asked to forecast next, and its momentum (accelerating MoM, rising yoy) dominates the surprise, I weight headline heavily for the next print despite core's gentle cooling. The policy language shift (dropping "likely to weigh" for "are weighing," adding "strongly committed") signals persistent concern, not resolution. Next headline CPI y/y is more likely to rise or stay elevated than to fall meaningfully.
Would change its mind if: A headline MoM print below ~0.4%, with core PCE continuing its decline, would confirm the turn and flip me to down/flat.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, headline_mom, headline_3m_annualized, core_pce_yoy, core_3m_annualized, core_yoy_change_3m, yoy_range_position, mom_gap_vs_outgoing

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: down, conviction 0.30
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 22 days ago.
Report:
  The unemployment rate printed 3.60%, unchanged from the prior reading and at the 12-month low, confirming my prior "down" call and its stated falsifier condition (print at or below 3.60% = continued tightening). The market is at its tightest point in the observed window, sitting 0.40pp below the assumed natural rate of ~4%. Momentum measures show continued mild tightening: -0.10pp over 3m, -0.30pp over 6m, and a steep -2.30pp over 12m, though the pace of decline has clearly decelerated compared to the earlier part of the series (which fell from 5.90% to 3.90% rapidly, then flattened into a narrow 3.60-4.00% band over the last seven readings). The Sahm gap is near zero (+0.03pp), so there's no recession-signal loosening underway. Policy language reinforces this, noting "unemployment rate has declined substantially" and upgrading job gains from "strong" to "robust," suggesting continued labor market strength. With the rate now flat at its floor for two consecutive readings and range position at 0 (the low end), the most likely next print is either another 3.60% or a marginal uptick, but there's no evidence yet of a turning point. I lean toward flat-to-slightly-down, but the room for further declines is shrinking given proximity to structural floors.
Would change its mind if: A print above 3.70% would signal a turn toward loosening; continued readings at or below 3.60% confirm tightening persists.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_12m, unrate_12m_low, sahm_gap, unrate_range_position, unrate_distance_from_natural

=== term_premium ===
Call: up, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last print shows dgs10 dropping to 2.98% from 3.10%, a notable single-step decline, but this follows a sharp multi-month climb: 21d change is still +0.13pp and 63d +0.63pp, with 252d up a full +1.49pp. The yield sits at 0.78 of its 12-month range — still in the upper portion, well above the 12m low. The recent pullback is a partial retracement, not a trend reversal: my prior falsifier (a confirmed move back toward 2.10-2.30 with negative 21d momentum and flattening 30s10s) has not been met — 21d momentum is still positive and slope_30s10s is a healthy +0.16pp, not flattening. My previous "up" call at 0.50 conviction was directionally right through most of the window but the latest single observation is a reversal signal worth respecting. Balance sheet runoff language remains hawkish for term premium (continuing QT), which is a structural upward pressure on the long end even as the last print eased. Given the tension between still-elevated momentum measures and the recent one-observation drop, I lean toward continued modest upward pressure but with reduced conviction given the fresh pullback.
Would change its mind if: A confirmed multi-session move below 2.90 with negative 21d momentum and a flattening/inverting 30s10s slope would signal the uptrend has reversed.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_change_252d, dgs10_range_position, slope_30s10s

The trend remains firmly negative across horizons — 21d change -0.26pp, 63d -0.44pp, 252d -1.27pp — all confirming persistent, accelerating flattening pressure with no sign of stabilization. The last print did bounce from -0.17 to -0.22, i.e., a fresh push flatter after two prints near -0.17/-0.18 that had looked like a pause. That pause failed; the curve made a new low instead of rebounding, which is the opposite of what would have triggered a reversal in my framework. Continued Fed tightening language (further target range hikes "anticipated") is consistent with front-end yields (dgs2 +2.89%) outrunning the back end (dgs10 +2.67%), keeping the curve pinned near cycle-flat levels. The 10s30s slope at +0.33pp shows the long end itself is not inverted, so this flattening is concentrated in the belly/front relationship, but that's outside my read. The three most recent prints (-0.17, -0.18, -0.20) show that trend has reversed: two consecutive weekly declines (increasingly negative, i.e. easing) after the -0.16 high, which is exactly the falsifier I specified. So the prior "up" call was wrong for this most recent leg — conditions eased over the last three readings rather than continuing to tighten. That said, the level remains near the top of its 52-week range (range_position 0.93) and well above the 52w low, so conditions are still historically loose in absolute terms but the momentum has clearly turned. Given the multi-week reversal and the explicit falsifier being triggered, I now lean toward continued easing (nfci moving lower) over the next month, though conviction is moderate given only three data points confirm the reversal and the longer-term trend was tightening.

Breakeven_10y has risen from 2.36 to 2.42 to 2.48 to 2.53 over the last four readings — a sustained, accelerating move well above the 2.45 threshold I set as my falsifier, and for more than two observations. The 21-day change is now +0.17pp, reversing the prior 63-day decline of -0.44pp. This is a clear trend reversal, not noise: three consecutive increases of similar magnitude (0.06, 0.06, 0.05) suggest momentum rather than a single data blip. Breakeven_range_position at 0.35 shows we're still below the 12-month high (3.02%) but well above the 12-month low (2.27%), and the distance from the 2% target has widened to +0.53pp, the highest in this window. === balance_sheet ===
Call: down, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The latest reading confirms the prior "down" call: total assets fell to 8,851,436 from 8,849,762 two observations back, essentially flat over the very last week but still deeply in runoff mode over the medium term. The 4-week change is -38,568M and the 13-week change is -62,845M (-0.70%), both negative, and the series sits 114,051M below its 52-week high. The 52-week change remains positive (+518,693M), a legacy of earlier expansion, but that is stale relative to the current trend — the recent pace is what matters per mandate, and that pace is clearly one of continued balance-sheet contraction. The statement language is unchanged, explicitly reaffirming continued reduction of Treasury and agency holdings per the announced runoff plan, with no hint of tapering the cap or pausing. There is some week-to-week noise (e.g., a modest uptick in the last observation vs. the one before), but the multi-week trend and unchanged policy language both point the same direction. I see no evidence to overturn last period's call; the rebound-above-13w-average condition I set as a falsifier has not occurred. I maintain a down call for month-end, with conviction similar to before given persistent noise around the exact weekly pace.
Would change its mind if: Fed_assets rebounding above the 13w average for multiple consecutive weeks, or statement language pausing/ending balance-sheet runoff.

Call: flat, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior call (down/flattening) played out further before stabilizing: the series moved from -0.24/-0.25 through -0.32/-0.33 before settling at -0.30/-0.35/-0.30 in the last three prints. That was consistent with the flattening view, though the most recent reading of -0.30 is actually a slight retracement off the -0.35 trough, and slope_change_21d is now essentially flat at +0.01pp, showing the 21-day trend has stalled rather than continuing to compress. However, the medium/long-term trend remains firmly flattening: slope_change_63d is -0.58pp and slope_change_252d is -1.39pp, both large negative moves. The spread sits at -0.30pp, deep in inverted territory, and slope_range_position of 0.10 confirms it's near the bottom of its 12-month range (low -0.48, high +1.29). The 10s30s slope at +0.12pp is modestly positive, suggesting long-end steepening isn't spreading into the belly/front relationship. With the 21-day momentum flat and no clear reversal signal (spread still well below the -0.10pp threshold I set), I don't see strong evidence of a turn yet, but the deceleration in the flattening pace argues against a strong continuation call either. This looks like consolidation near cycle-inverted lows rather than a fresh leg down or a genuine steepening.
Would change its mind if: A break above -0.15pp with slope_change_21d turning consistently positive (>0.10pp) would confirm a genuine steepening reversal rather than range-bound consolidation.

My prior call was 'down' (easing) with 0.45 conviction, but the actual trailing move was up (tightening) — I misread the momentum; the 4w change is now negative (-0.04) reflecting the most recent leg lower after that tightening run, but the 13w change is positive (+0.04), and range position is high (0.85), meaning the index sits near the tight end of its 52-week range. The last two prints are flat at -0.24, suggesting the recent easing impulse has stalled. Given the flattening at the tail, and conflicting 4w vs 13w signals, I don't see strong momentum in either direction right now — the series just leveled off after a down-leg. Distance from zero remains meaningfully negative (looser than average), but that's a level, not a directional signal for the next month. I'll call this flat with modest conviction, acknowledging the previous down call was wrong on the intervening tightening.
Would change its mind if: A resumption of consecutive weekly increases toward the 52w high (-0.16) would signal renewed tightening; a break below -0.26 would confirm renewed easing.

The series peaked at 2.62 several observations back and has since declined for three consecutive readings (2.60→2.57→2.58→2.55→2.48), with the most recent print at 2.48 marking a fresh local low, not a resumption of the prior uptrend. The 252-day change remains mildly positive (+0.09pp), but that longer lookback is stale relative to the sharp recent drop. Range position of 0.27 (within a 2.28-3.02 12-month range) confirms breakevens are now in the lower third of their annual range, well off the highs. Distance from the 2% target is still +0.48pp, so inflation expectations remain anchored above objective, but the direction of travel over the past month is unambiguously down. I was wrong last time to expect continuation of the uptrend; the reversal did fail, and the breakdown below 2.50 confirms a new declining trend rather than noise.

=== inflation ===
Call: down, conviction 0.50
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 16 days ago.
Report:
  The prior "down" call is validated: headline CPI y/y fell from 8.98% to 8.46% to 8.22%, and the last two MoM prints were -0.01% and 0.06% — a sharp deceleration from the 0.95-1.26% pace two months prior. Headline 3m annualized (5.32%) is now below the trailing y/y, and headline_yoy_change_3m is -0.32pp, both confirming cooling momentum, likely driven by energy price relief given the gap between headline and core. Core PCE y/y also ticked down to 5.11% from 5.32%, with core_yoy_change_3m at -0.24pp, though core remains far above target (+3.11pp) and the deceleration there is much more modest than in headline — core_3m_annualized at 4.75% is still uncomfortably high and only slightly below the y/y rate. So underlying persistence hasn't broken, but the immediate one-month trend in headline MoM (two consecutive near-zero prints) suggests the next headline y/y print is more likely to fall or stay roughly flat than to rebound. Policy language changes are cosmetic (invasion→war, added "food") and don't signal a change in the Fed's inflation assessment. Given two months of very soft MoM prints, I lean toward continued softening in headline y/y, but with weaker conviction than last time since a base-effect rebound is possible if energy reverses.
Would change its mind if: A headline MoM print rebounding above ~0.6-0.7% (e.g., from energy price reversal) or core PCE y/y reaccelerating above 5.3% would flip this call.

  The unemployment rate ticked up to 3.60% from a 12-month low of 3.50%, matching one prior reading of 3.60% two months back. My prior "flat" call held: the print did not exceed 3.70%, staying within the recent 3.50-3.60% band. The level remains well below the ~4% natural rate (distance -0.40pp), and the Sahm-rule gap is trivial at +0.07pp, far from the ~0.5pp threshold that signals recession-onset loosening. The 3m change is exactly 0.00pp and 6m change is still negative (-0.30pp), meaning the broader trend over the past six months is still tightening, not loosening, despite this single uptick. Range position is very low (0.08 of the 12-month range), confirming the labor market sits near its tightest point in the observed window. This one-month uptick could be noise around a still-tight labor market rather than a turn. Job gains are described as "robust" and unemployment "remained low" in policy language, reinforcing that officials see no meaningful loosening yet. I lean toward flat-to-slightly-up for the next print, since 3.60% has already recurred twice, but the overall trend and low level argue against calling this a genuine loosening signal yet.
Would change its mind if: A print above 3.70% would confirm a turning point toward loosening; a print at or below 3.50% would suggest tightening is still active.

=== term_premium ===
Call: up, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  My prior call (down, 0.40) was wrong: the 10Y has continued to climb, now at 3.15 versus 3.03-3.11 previously, confirming the multi-session move above 3.00 with positive momentum that I set as my own falsifier. The level sits at 0.85 of its 12-month range, near the highs, and both 21d (+0.40pp) and 63d (+0.21pp) changes are positive, indicating sustained upward pressure rather than a reversal. The 252d change of +1.84pp shows this is part of a large, ongoing trend higher, not noise. Slope_30s10s at +0.12pp is modestly positive (mild long-end steepening within the long end), consistent with term premium building rather than compressing. Balance sheet runoff continues per the unchanged policy language, which mechanically adds duration supply pressure to the long end, reinforcing the upward drift. There is no flight-to-quality signal here — quite the opposite, yields are grinding toward the top of their range. Given the consistency of momentum across 21d/63d windows, the elevated range position, and continued runoff, I now flip to up with moderate conviction. I don't see evidence of exhaustion or reversal in this data.
