arbitrated.notes:
The direct curve_slope read (2s10s at -0.30, consolidating after a flattening run) is the most on-mandate input, and it explicitly says momentum has stalled (21d change ~flat) even as the medium-term trend (63d/252d) remains flattening. That's a genuine "no fresh signal" read, not a reversal — I weight it heavily but temper the flattening bias slightly given the stall.

Cross-checking against the front-end and long-end drivers: term_premium is up with real conviction (0.55), driven by 10Y momentum grinding toward range highs, explicitly reinforced by ongoing balance-sheet runoff (a long-end supply story). Balance_sheet itself confirms continued contraction (down/runoff, 0.55 conviction) with no tapering signal — this is a genuine long-end steepening pressure via term premium/supply, independent of the front end. Labor_tightness is flat (0.30, low conviction, single-month uptick within a still-tight range) — this gives no strong front-end anchor pulling the other way, so it doesn't offset the long-end pressure. Inflation and inflation_expectations are both trending down, which argues for lower front-end (policy-path) yields more than long-end, which is disinflationary for term premium too, but term_premium's own analyst already sees the long end climbing despite this — meaning the supply/duration-premium force is currently dominating the disinflation drag on the 10Y.

Netting this out: term_premium's independently-confirmed upward push on the long end, reinforced by ongoing balance-sheet runoff, is a steepening force against a front end anchored by tight-but-stalling labor conditions and softening inflation (which cap front-end policy-rate expectations). This tilts the reconciled call modestly toward steepening, overriding the raw curve_slope "flat" print on the grounds that its own momentum measure was stalling right as a fresh long-end driver (term premium/supply) is building — a case where the direct curve reading is lagging the driver-level cross-read.

discounted.why: Low conviction, single-month noise within a stable range; no clear front-end signal to weigh against long end.

arbitrated.falsifier: A break in 10Y momentum back below 3.05 with negative 21d change, or curve_slope breaking below -0.35 with slope_change_21d turning sharply negative, would flip this to flattening.

risks.text: Term premium call could reverse quickly if 10Y momentum exhausts near range highs, undermining the steepening thesis.
Softening inflation and flat labor data could pull front end down faster than expected, exaggerating steepening beyond this call.
Direct 2s10s reading itself says flat/consolidating; overriding it on driver cross-read carries model risk.
Balance sheet runoff pace could decelerate or pause, removing the long-end supply pressure underlying this call.

trade.rationale: Modest steepener: long-end term premium building on continued runoff/supply while front end anchored by stalling labor tightening and softening inflation caps near-term policy repricing.

why.term_premium: 10Y momentum and range position both point to rising long-end term premium, reinforced by supply/runoff.
why.curve_slope: Own measure stalled near flattening lows; medium-term trend flattening but momentum dead — near-neutral, slight steepening tilt from cross-read.
why.balance_sheet: Runoff continues unabated, confirming long-end supply pressure that supports term-premium-led steepening.
why.labor_tightness: Flat, low-conviction uptick; not enough to anchor front end lower, so doesn't offset long-end steepening force.

Balance_sheet continues to run off aggressively (confident -0.75 call) which is a long-end supply-side force that historically steepens the curve via term premium — but term_premium itself reports a recent pullback (-0.34pp over 21d) with a still-positive 30s10s slope, so the QT-driven long-end pressure has not shown up as a term-premium breakout recently; if anything long yields have eased faster than short-end expectations, which is flattening, not steepening. This resolves the apparent tension: QT is a slow-moving supply story that hasn't overridden the immediate flattening dynamic from long yields pulling back while front-end (labor-anchored) yields stay firm on Fed-hold expectations.

Financial conditions easing (looser) modestly argues for less near-term stress-driven curve inversion pressure, but this is a second-order effect versus the direct slope and term-premium readings. === balance_sheet ===

The policy language explicitly reaffirms continued runoff of Treasury and agency MBS holdings per the announced plan, with no mention of tapering the cap or pausing reinvestment. There is no evidence of deceleration in the weekly decrements — the last several weekly drops (508,587→489,039→470,557) show pace roughly $18-19B/week, consistent with prior weeks. Nothing here suggests a reversal or plateau is imminent for next month-end. Absent any statement change signaling a slowdown or halt, the mechanical, policy-conforming runoff should continue at a similar pace into the next observation month-end. I maintain a confident "down" call, slightly below previous conviction only because the pace seems slightly more volatile week-to-week than perfectly linear, but directionally unambiguous.

The last ten observations oscillate in a narrow band (-0.65 to -0.70) with no clear reversal, but the drift within that band is toward the more negative end (ending at -0.69 after touching -0.65). Policy language shows another rate hike (to 4-1/4–4-1/2%) with continued "ongoing increases" language, which historically pressures front-end yields up relative to long end, consistent with the flattening pressure already evident in dgs2 (4.21%) exceeding dgs10 (3.52%) by the full inverted spread. Given the continued restrictive tilt and multi-horizon negative momentum, I lean toward modest further flattening rather than flat, though the band-bound behavior limits conviction.

The series is still up considerably from its 12-month low (be_above_12m_low +0.12pp) and sits only 13% into its 12-month range, but the near-term trajectory is now clearly softening. Distance from the 2% target remains material at +0.24pp, so expectations are still anchored above objective, but the direction of travel over the last several readings is down, not up. Given two consecutive down-prints and negative short/medium-term change measures, I read this as a modest downward lean for the next month rather than a continuation of new highs. Report:
  The prior "down" call was directionally correct: headline y/y fell from 7.12 to 6.33 (-0.79pp), continuing the deceleration trend, and my stated falsifiers (MoM above 0.6% or core PCE back above 5.5%) did not trigger — outgoing MoM was 0.53% and core PCE fell to 4.97%, its lowest in the series. That said, the outgoing MoM of 0.53% is a step up from the prior month's 0.02%, and the 3m annualized rates (headline 3.28%, core 4.08%) show core running hotter than the y/y trend suggests, with core_yoy_change_3m only -0.64pp versus -1.43pp for headline. This gap (core_minus_headline -1.36pp) means headline's recent favorable readings are more energy/base-effect driven, while core PCE, though improving, remains far above target (+2.97pp) and its momentum is less convincingly decelerating. The yoy_range_position at 0.00 confirms headline is at its 12-month low, which is constructive, but the single-month MoM uptick warrants caution against expecting another sharp drop next month. Net: disinflation trend intact but momentum has softened compared to the pace of decline seen in prior months, so I expect continued but slower improvement.
Would change its mind if: A headline MoM print above 0.6% or core PCE y/y rising back above 5.2% would signal stalling/reacceleration and invalidate continued deceleration.

Call: flat, conviction 0.60

Report:
  The unemployment rate has been essentially flat at cycle lows, printing 3.50% in the most recent observation, matching the trailing 12-month low and sitting well below the ~4% natural rate estimate (distance_from_natural -0.50pp). My prior flat call was correct: the series has oscillated narrowly between 3.50-3.60% for the last six readings with no directional break, and the latest print confirms that range-bound behavior rather than the loosening or renewed-tightening thresholds I set. The 3m change (-0.10pp) and 6m change (0.00pp) both indicate no meaningful momentum in either direction, and the Sahm gap remains negligible at +0.03pp, far from the ~0.5pp threshold that would signal recessionary loosening. Policy language continues to describe the labor market as robust with low unemployment, consistent with the data. With the rate pinned at its 12-month low and no acceleration in either direction visible in the last several prints, the most defensible call remains flat. There is a slight asymmetry — the level is very tight, and historically such tightness eventually gives way to gradual normalization — but nothing in the recent trend supports calling a turn yet. I maintain flat with slightly higher conviction than before given the confirmed stability.
Would change its mind if: A print above 3.70% would confirm loosening; a print at or below 3.40% would suggest renewed tightening.

I largely accept this but temper it slightly. Meanwhile labor_tightness is flat at cycle-low unemployment, meaning the Fed has no labor-market signal to ease off hikes soon — front-end pressure from policy stays intact, reinforcing the flattening case rather than offsetting it. Financial conditions easing (down/looser, -0.29 NFCI) is a bit of a mixed cross-read for slope: looser conditions sometimes accompany curve steepening (risk-on, growth optimism pulling long yields up), but here it's occurring alongside disinflation and stalling term premium, suggesting it's not translating into long-end selling pressure. Inflation and inflation expectations both softening reinforces anchored/falling long-end yields, capping any steepening from term premium. Net: front-end stays pinned high by continued hikes and tight labor, long end lacks the term-premium or inflation-expectation impulse to rise in tandem, so the curve should continue grinding modestly flatter/more inverted, but I shade conviction down from the analyst's 0.35 given the offsetting (if weak) balance-sheet steepening signal and low slope conviction overall.

arbitrated.notes: The direct curve_slope read is the most relevant single input: 2s10s inversion has deepened to -0.58, with the last three prints monotonically more negative and both 63d and 252d momentum confirming re-flattening (more inversion), despite a stale positive 21d reading that has since fully reversed. That's a coherent, fresh flattening signal I weight heavily. Cross-checking against front-end and long-end drivers: labor_tightness is flat/pinned at cycle lows, implying no near-term pressure for the Fed to ease off the front end — front-end yields stay anchored high, consistent with flattening pressure from the short end. term_premium (10y) shows recent softness (yields pulling back from 3.57-3.59 to 3.48) — falling long-end yields with an anchored front end is itself a flattening/inversion-deepening dynamic, corroborating curve_slope rather than contradicting it. financial_conditions tightening (NFCI rising toward zero) typically accompanies flight-to-quality flows into the long end and a Fed still restrictive at the front — also consistent with continued flattening. balance_sheet's reversal to "up" (surge in assets) is a long-end-relevant supply/liquidity story, but it's a single-month spike the analyst himself flags as possibly technical/reversing, and it lacks corroboration from financial_conditions; I discount its impact on the slope call given its freshness/reversibility. Inflation and inflation_expectations are drivers I read as level, not slope, absent a clear front-vs-back differentiation. Taken together: front end stays sticky-high (tight labor, hawkish policy language), long end drifting lower on momentum and flight-to-quality — that's a flattening (more inversion) environment. I side with curve_slope's own re-flattening call, reinforced by labor_tightness and term_premium's directional cross-read, for a moderate-conviction flattener.

The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: down, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior call (up, 0.45) was wrong: assets have now fallen for four consecutive prints (8639300→8733787 peak, then 8705942, 8632384, 8614797, 8593263, 8562768), a steady decline of roughly $171M over the last five weeks. The earlier spike appears to have been a temporary technical add (likely repo/funding related) that is now unwinding, exactly the scenario I flagged as a falsifier. The 4-week change is -143174, clearly negative and accelerating relative to the mixed 13w figure (+92211, still positive only because it captures the spike's peak). Assets are now below the 13w average and well below the 52w high (-383130). Policy language confirms continued balance-sheet reduction (QT) as the operative framework, consistent with the renewed decline. With the spike fading and QT language unchanged, the path of least resistance is further runoff at least at the announced pace, absent another funding-market disruption. I'm shifting to a lower/runoff call with moderate conviction, tempered by the still-elevated 13w average and the possibility of another transitory add.
Would change its mind if: A reversal back above the recent peak (~8.7T) or a new print showing assets flat/rising for two consecutive weeks would signal the runoff has paused or another repo-type add occurred.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_below_52w_high, assets_13w_avg

=== curve_slope ===
Call: down, conviction 0.40
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior call (down/flattening, 0.45 conviction) was correct in direction: the spread moved from -0.46/-0.47 back out to -0.54 and now -0.60, re-flattening after a brief steepening bounce. Current level (-0.60) matches where the series started 10 observations ago, so net change is essentially flat over that window, but the most recent print shows renewed inversion after touching -0.46. slope_change_21d is slightly negative (-0.05), while slope_change_63d is still positive (+0.10), reflecting that the recent flattening reverses only part of the prior quarter's steepening. The spread sits at 0.31 of its 12-month range (0-1), still closer to the deeply inverted end (-1.07) than the high (+0.44), reinforcing that inversion is the dominant regime. Policy language shifted to "additional policy firming may be appropriate" combined with continued hikes to 4-3/4–5%, i.e., front-end pressure continuing, which mechanically weighs on the belly/front more than the long end, consistent with further flattening or at least stable deep inversion rather than a steepening reversal. My falsifier condition (sustained move above -0.45 with both 21d/63d positive) was not met — the latest print reversed back toward -0.60. This favors continued flattening or flat-to-down, not a steepening breakout.
Would change its mind if: A sustained move above -0.45 with slope_change_21d and slope_change_63d both turning positive would signal a genuine steepening reversal.
Leaned on: slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d, slope_range_position, slope_12m_low, slope_12m_high

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last two prints show conditions easing back slightly after a multi-month tightening trend: -0.15, -0.17, -0.18, with the 4-week change now -0.02 (i.e., conditions loosened marginally over the past month) rather than continuing to rise. My previous "up" call was based on the strong 13-week uptrend (+0.11) and the falsifier I set was exactly this: a stall or reversal with the 4-week change turning flat/negative. That condition has now been met — the index peaked around -0.15 and has ticked back down for two consecutive readings. That said, the level remains well above the 13-week average (-0.22) and near the top of its 52-week range (range_position 0.66), so this is not a resumption of easing, just a pause/mild giveback in the tightening trend. Policy language flags "recent developments" likely to tighten credit further, which argues against calling this a durable reversal. Given the two-observation reversal is modest and could be noise within a broader tightening regime, I shift my call toward flat with a slight easing lean, acknowledging my prior up call is now under pressure but not yet decisively refuted.
Would change its mind if: A resumption of the rise toward zero (e.g., back above -0.15 with positive 4-week change) would confirm renewed tightening; further decline toward -0.22 would confirm easing.
Leaned on: nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_range_position, nfci_distance_from_zero

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: down, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "down" call played out: breakeven fell further to 2.18 from 2.27, a sharp -0.09pp single-period drop and the lowest print in the 10-observation window. The series has been grinding lower for most of the window (2.32→2.18), with 21d change now -0.16pp, 63d -0.11pp, and 252d -0.67pp — all negative, confirming a persistent, not just noisy, downtrend. The range position (0.09 of the 12-month range) shows breakevens sitting near the bottom of their 12-month band, just 0.08pp above the 12-month low, with no sign of the rebound-above-2.34 condition that would have falsified the prior call. Distance from the 2% target has compressed to +0.18pp, the smallest gap in this window, meaning market-implied inflation expectations are now much closer to anchoring at target than they were. Given the accelerating pace of decline in the most recent print (largest one-period drop in the window) and the absence of any stabilizing policy language shift, I expect the drift lower to continue or at least persist near these depressed levels into month-end. I don't see strong evidence of an imminent reversal, though the pace of decline could moderate as the series approaches the 12-month low.
Would change its mind if: A rebound back above ~2.27-2.30 over the next few observations, breaking the accelerating downtrend, would revive an up/flat view.
Leaned on: breakeven_10y, be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_change_252d, be_range_position, be_above_12m_low, be_distance_from_target

=== inflation ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.40
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 15 days ago.
Report:
  The last release actually validated the prior "down" call: headline y/y ticked up only marginally (4.92→4.95, essentially flat) after a large multi-month decline, and the mom print (0.34%) stayed well below my 0.5% trigger. Base effects have done most of the work — headline_yoy_change_3m is still -1.38pp and the series sits near its 12-month low (yoy_above_12m_low +0.03pp). However, the momentum underneath is less reassuring: outgoing_mom of 0.95% will roll off the base next month, and headline_mom_3m_avg (0.27%) combined with core_3m_annualized (4.74%) shows sequential price pressure is still running well above target, not decelerating further in the near term. Core PCE y/y has ground down only slowly (5.59→4.78) and remains 2.78pp above target, with core_minus_headline now negative — core is the sticky, persistent read and it is not falling fast. Given that a very favorable base-effect comparison is exhausted, and monthly momentum has stabilized rather than continued falling, I expect headline y/y to be roughly flat to slightly up at the next print rather than resuming its steep drop. This is a shift from my prior stance given the flattening in the yoy series and firming momentum data.
Would change its mind if: A headline MoM print below 0.15% or a fresh drop in core PCE y/y below 4.7% would confirm renewed disinflation and reverse this flat/up lean.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, headline_mom, core_pce_yoy, headline_yoy_change_3m, core_3m_annualized, headline_mom_3m_avg, outgoing_mom, core_distance_from_target

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.55
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 22 days ago.
Report:
  The previous flat call held: the newest print (3.40%) sits at the 12-month low, not the loosening print that would have falsified it. In fact the series has moved tighter, not looser. Unemployment has drifted from 3.60-3.70% down to 3.40%, with unrate_change_3m/6m/12m all negative and the rate now at its 12-month low with zero range position (unrate_range_position=0.0) and 0.6pp below the ~4% natural rate estimate. Sahm-gap is a trivial +0.10pp, far from any recession-signal threshold, and job gains are described as robust and picking up, consistent with continued tightness rather than cooling. There is no evidence of loosening momentum anywhere in the data — the direction of travel over the past year has been toward a tighter labor market, not looser. Given the rate is already at the low end of its recent range and policy language emphasizes robust hiring, the most likely near-term outcome is unemployment holding near 3.40-3.50%, i.e., essentially flat, with perhaps a slight tilt toward staying low or ticking down marginally. I do not see support for a meaningful rise next month. I'll call this flat-to-slightly-tightening, leaning toward flat given one-month noise dominates at these small margins.
Would change its mind if: A print above 3.60% would confirm loosening momentum; a print at or below 3.30% would suggest further tightening beyond recent range.

=== term_premium ===
Call: down, conviction 0.25
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 10y yield sits at 3.44, down from the prior print's trajectory and squarely mid-range (range_position 0.51). My prior call for "down" was directionally correct on net over the past month (21d change -0.11pp, 63d change -0.11pp), though the path was choppy — yield dipped to 3.40 then bounced to 3.53 before settling at 3.44. Neither of my stated flip triggers (rebound above 3.60, or break below 3.35) was hit, so the down lean stays intact but unconfirmed with force. The 10s30s slope at +0.23pp is modest and not signaling a sharp steepening that would indicate fresh term-premium building at the long end. Balance-sheet runoff continues per unchanged policy language, which is a modest structural upward pressure on long-end supply/term premium, but it hasn't been enough to push yields to new highs — the 252d change of +0.67pp shows the larger uptrend from a year ago, but the recent 21/63d windows show consolidation-to-mild-decline. Absent a decisive break either way, I lean toward continued mild softening but with reduced conviction given the choppiness and lack of a confirmed trend.
Would change its mind if: A rebound above 3.60 with positive 21d momentum and flattening 30s10s slope would signal renewed term-premium rise; a break below 3.35 confirms decline.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_range_position, slope_30s10s

The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: down, conviction 0.65
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The latest print (8,436,255) extends a clear declining run: the last six weekly observations are monotonically lower (8,562,768 → 8,503,994 → 8,503,017 → 8,456,760 → 8,436,255), and the 4-week change is -126,513 $M, a pace consistent with ongoing balance-sheet runoff. My prior "down" call is confirmed — there was no reversal or two-week flat/rising pattern that would have falsified it. The 13-week change (+54,065) and 13w_avg still reflect the earlier spike/bulge in the series (the run-up to the 8.7T peak), but that is now well behind the recent trend; the most recent weeks show steady, uninterrupted decline. Assets remain -498,091 $M below the 52-week high, and the policy language explicitly reaffirms continued reduction of Treasury and agency holdings, consistent with QT proceeding at its announced pace. There is no language suggesting a pause, taper, or reinvestment change. Given the consistency of the recent weekly declines and unchanged reduction language, I expect total assets to be lower again at month end, roughly in line with the recent ~$30-40M/week pace of decline, absent any policy signal of a slowdown.
Would change its mind if: A reversal back above ~8.5T, or two consecutive weekly prints flat/rising, or explicit policy language announcing a pause/end to runoff.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_below_52w_high

=== curve_slope ===
Call: down, conviction 0.45
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "down" call was correct: the spread fell further, from -0.56 to -0.76 over the last few prints, deepening the inversion. The most recent three observations (-0.67, -0.74, -0.76) show continued flattening/deepening inversion, and slope_change_21d is -0.21pp, confirming recent momentum is negative. However, slope_change_63d is essentially flat (+0.05pp), suggesting the medium-term trend is not accelerating, just the last few weeks. The spread sits at -0.76pp, near the bottom of its 12-month range (range_position 0.22, only 0.31pp above the 12-month low of -1.07 and far below the high of +0.32). Language shows the Committee continuing to hike (5-5.25%) while signaling data-dependence for "additional policy firming," consistent with a still-inverted, front-loaded tightening posture that keeps the front end anchored high while the long end lags — supportive of continued inversion. The 10s30s slope is modestly positive (+0.21), suggesting the long end itself isn't broadly steepening. Given the recent acceleration lower and proximity to 12-month lows, further flattening/deeper inversion is more likely than a reversal, though the 63-day trend is flat, capping conviction.
Would change its mind if: A sustained move above -0.60 with slope_change_21d and slope_change_63d both turning positive would signal a steepening reversal.
Leaned on: slope_change_21d, slope_range_position, slope_12m_low, slope_above_12m_low, slope_10s30s

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: down, conviction 0.40
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The latest print (-0.21) continued the easing drift I flagged as a risk last time: the series has fallen for five straight readings from -0.15 to -0.21, and nfci_change_4w is -0.03, confirming renewed loosening rather than the flat/tightening resumption I was watching for. My prior "flat" call was too cautious given this move — conditions are clearly loosening on a 4-week basis. That said, the 13-week change is still +0.08 (net tightening over the quarter) and the index sits at -0.21, well below zero (nfci_distance_from_zero -0.21) and closer to the 52-week low (-0.31) than the high (-0.10), with range_position at 0.47 — roughly mid-range, not extreme. Policy language softened from "cumulative tightening" framing toward "tighter credit conditions... likely to weigh," dropping explicit reference to further policy firming needs, consistent with a plateau/easing bias rather than active tightening. Given the consistent recent decline and negative 4-week momentum, I lean toward further mild easing (lower NFCI) into month end, though the longer-term trend and still-below-average level argue against a strong move. This is a moderate-conviction lean, not a high-conviction call.
Would change its mind if: A reversal back above -0.15 with positive 4-week change would signal renewed tightening; continued decline below -0.22 would confirm the easing lean.
Leaned on: nfci, nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_distance_from_zero, nfci_range_position

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: down, conviction 0.60
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "down" call was correct and the trend has continued to weaken. The 10-year breakeven fell to 2.18 in the latest print from 2.22, extending a clear downtrend: -0.05pp over 21d, -0.30pp over 63d, -0.44pp over 252d. The series shows accelerating deterioration rather than stabilization — the last two readings (2.22, 2.18) are the lowest in the 10-observation window, and the range position of 0.12 (near the 12-month low of 2.10, far from the high of 2.76) confirms market-implied inflation expectations are compressing toward multi-month lows. Distance from the 2% target has narrowed to +0.18pp, the smallest gap in this window, meaning breakevens are converging toward — not diverging further above — the Committee's objective. There is no evidence of the rebound above 2.27-2.30 that would have falsified the downtrend; instead the trend accelerated further down. With no sign of stabilization in the short-window changes and the level sitting just 0.08pp above the 12-month low, momentum favors continued softening or at least no reversal. I maintain a down call, with slightly higher conviction given the accelerating pace and absence of any countervailing signal in the data shown.
Would change its mind if: A rebound back above ~2.24-2.27 over the next few observations, breaking the accelerating downtrend, would revive a flat/up view.
Leaned on: breakeven_10y, be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_change_252d, be_range_position, be_distance_from_target

=== inflation ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.35
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 16 days ago.
Report:
  The last print (4.13% y/y headline, +0.16% m/m) came in below my prior falsifier threshold (0.15% m/m was the bar, and 0.16% is essentially at it), and importantly the y/y series shows a large drop from 4.95% to 4.13% — a 0.82pp deceleration in one month, driven by base effects as a high year-ago comparison rolled off. My previous flat call was too cautious; the disinflation continued more than I weighted. However, looking ahead one month, base effects matter enormously: the outgoing_mom being dropped from the y/y window was 1.26%, a very high comparison, which is why this print fell so much. The next comparison month is unlikely to be as favorable (headline_mom_3m_avg is 0.21%, not extreme), so the pace of y/y decline should moderate sharply from here — the easy base-effect gains are largely exhausted. Core PCE is at 4.79%, barely moved (core_yoy_change_3m only -0.15pp) and running 4.25% annualized over 3m, still well above 2% and above the 3m headline pace, indicating underlying persistence. Given headline momentum has stabilized (0.12-0.34-0.16% m/m range) rather than continuing to collapse, I expect next month's y/y print to be roughly flat to modestly higher, not a continuation of the sharp drop.

My seat is 2s10s slope only. The curve_slope analyst directly reports the spread deepening its inversion (-0.56 → -0.76), with negative 21d momentum, near 12-month lows, and a flat-not-accelerating 63d trend capping conviction at 0.45. Cross-checking against level drivers: balance_sheet reports continued QT (assets down, high conviction 0.65) — ongoing runoff is a long-end supply factor that term_premium's own analyst explicitly said they lack visibility into, and which should, if anything, add modest upward pressure on 10y term premium relative to the front end (front end anchored by Fed policy rate, not by SOMA composition effects as directly). This is consistent with — not contradicting — continued flattening pressure being capped rather than reversed, since long-end supply pushes long yields up, which would argue for steepening, but term_premium's own reading of 10y level is flat/consolidating, not rising confidently (conviction only 0.20, prior "down" call was wrong but no strong up-conviction either). Labor_tightness is flat/rangebound, offering no front-end catalyst either way — the front end anchor (Fed funds expectations) is stable, consistent with the curve_slope analyst's read that the front end stays anchored high while the long end lags, sustaining inversion. Inflation and inflation_expectations both point toward continued disinflation, which supports a dovish repricing further out the curve eventually, but near-term this is more a level story (lower yields broadly) than a clean slope signal, and inflation analyst's flat call at higher conviction moderates that.

With m/m running near 0.22-0.24% and less favorable base comparisons ahead, I expect headline y/y to be roughly stable to modestly higher, not a continued sharp decline. Core's stickiness reinforces that underlying inflation isn't decelerating much further, so I lean flat-to-up on headline for the next print.

Labor_tightness, the front-end anchor here, reports a genuinely flat, rangebound labor market (3.40-3.60% for 13 months, zero 6m/12m trend) — no reacceleration or loosening signal that would move front-end Fed pricing decisively either way. === balance_sheet ===
Call: down, conviction 0.80
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead

Report:
  The series continues its steady, uninterrupted decline: 13 consecutive weekly prints falling from 8,121,316 to 7,810,814. The most recent 4-week change (-97,016) and 13-week change (-328,252, -4.03%) confirm runoff is proceeding at a consistent pace, roughly 90-100bn per 4-week window, in line with the announced caps. There is no sign of deceleration or reversal in the print history — each successive observation is lower than the last, with no flat or up weeks appearing. Assets remain far below the 52-week high (-922,973), reinforcing the one-way trend. Policy language is unchanged, reiterating that the Committee "will continue reducing its holdings of Treasury securities and agency debt and agency mortgage-backed securities, as described in its previously announced plans" — no mention of pause, taper, or end of runoff. My previous down call at 0.80 conviction was correct: the newest print (7,810,814) is lower than the prior newest (7,814,991), continuing the trend exactly as expected. With no change in policy language and the pace of decline consistent with recent weeks, I maintain the same call and conviction. The only risk would be a technical/liquidity-driven pause, but nothing in the current evidence suggests that.
Would change its mind if: A weekly print flat or rising, sustained reversal above ~8.3T, or policy language announcing a pause/taper/end of runoff.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_pct_change_13w, assets_below_52w_high

=== curve_slope ===
Call: up, conviction 0.45
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last three prints (-0.45, -0.39, -0.37, -0.36) show a clear steepening move off the recent trough, with the spread now at -0.36pp, near the top of its 12-month range (range_position 0.76, only 0.72pp above the 12m low of -1.08). The 21-day change is -0.17pp (a recent wobble), but 63-day (+0.42pp) and 252-day (+0.37pp) changes confirm the dominant trend over the last few months has been steepening, not flattening. My prior "flat" call was wrong — the data since then has continued the steepening trajectory I should have weighted more heavily; the multi-month trend was already steepening before the last release and has persisted. The 10s30s slope (+0.17pp) staying positive suggests the long end isn't inverting further, consistent with a broader de-inversion theme across the curve. The negative 21d reading is worth watching but doesn't yet break the multi-month uptrend, and the newest three observations (-0.39, -0.37, -0.36) show renewed steepening within that 21-day window itself. I'll call this steepening (up) with moderate conviction, expecting the spread to continue drifting toward less negative territory into month-end, barring a sharp reversal.
Would change its mind if: A break back below -0.45 sustained for multiple sessions with slope_change_21d and 63d both turning negative would confirm renewed flattening.

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: down, conviction 0.45
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.

The 13-week change is still slightly positive (+0.02) reflecting the earlier rise, but momentum has clearly turned over the most recent weeks. Level remains deeply negative (-0.31), near the 52-week low (-0.34) and far below zero, so conditions are loose in absolute terms and financial conditions are easing, not tightening. Policy language added "financial" alongside "credit conditions" — a minor broadening of the tightening narrative, but the actual index data show conditions loosening in the most recent weeks. Given the clear recent downturn in the index after a brief rise, I now expect the index to continue drifting lower or flat-to-lower into month end, reversing my prior tightening call.

Call: flat, conviction 0.35

Report:

My prior "flat" call anchored on a 2.35-2.48 range no longer fits: the series has clearly migrated lower, printing 2.21-2.23 for three of the last four observations versus 2.25-2.30 earlier in the window. That prior call was wrong in framing — the range itself shifted down, so "flat" understated the drift. Still, looking at the last 5-6 prints (2.26, 2.26, 2.21, 2.22, 2.21, 2.23), the series has essentially stabilized at a new, lower plateau rather than continuing to fall — the 21d change of -0.19pp reflects the step down already completed, while 63d and 252d changes are both near zero (-0.03, -0.02), meaning this isn't a fresh sustained downtrend but a leveling-off after an earlier drop. Distance from the 2% target remains modest at +0.23pp, well within normal anchoring. Absent a new catalyst, I expect breakevens to hold near current levels rather than resume falling or bounce back sharply.
Would change its mind if: A break below 2.18 or a sustained move back above 2.28 over several prints would signal renewed directional momentum rather than plateau.

=== inflation ===
Call: down, conviction 0.35
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 15 days ago.
Report:
  Last time I called "down" on continued disinflation momentum, and the newest print vindicates that: headline yoy fell from 3.25 to 3.13, and the month/month print was 0.15%, below the 0.35% threshold that would have flagged renewed acceleration. Core PCE continues its steady grind lower (3.69 to 3.47 yoy), and the 3-month annualized core (2.31%) is now essentially at target, with headline 3m annualized also at 2.73%. Momentum indicators (headline_yoy_change_3m -0.59pp, core_yoy_change_3m -0.83pp) confirm the trend is still disinflationary, not stalling. However, headline yoy is now sitting near its 12-month low (yoy_above_12m_low only 0.06pp), which means further big yoy declines require continued soft base effects — the low-hanging fruit of easy comparisons may be diminishing, and outgoing_mom (0.02) being low means the base being dropped is easy to beat, which could make next month's yoy comparison marginally harder if new prints run at the 3m average pace (0.22%). Still, with mom prints modest and no signs of reacceleration in either headline or core, I lean toward continued flat-to-slightly-lower readings rather than a reversal upward. Conviction is moderate given the yoy is near cycle lows and base effects are less favorable going forward, tempering the disinflation call.
Would change its mind if: A headline m/m print above 0.35% or yoy rising back above 3.4% would signal renewed acceleration rather than continued disinflation.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, headline_mom, core_pce_yoy, headline_3m_annualized, core_3m_annualized, headline_yoy_change_3m, core_yoy_change_3m, outgoing_mom, yoy_above_12m_low

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.30
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 22 days ago.
Report:
  The latest print pulled back to 3.70% from the 3.90% spike, exactly within the 3.60-3.70% range I flagged as the "noise" scenario for the prior 3.90 reading. This confirms my prior up call was too aggressive in conviction — the 3.90 print looks like a one-month outlier rather than the start of accelerating loosening. That said, the broader trend still shows some drift: the 3m avg (3.77%) sits above the 12m low (3.40%), the unrate is up 0.30pp from that low, and the range position (0.60) shows we're in the upper half of the past year's range. The Sahm gap of 0.37pp is still meaningfully below the 0.50 trigger threshold, so no recession-signal loosening is confirmed. Policy language changes are modest — "moderated since earlier in the year" versus "slowed in recent months" is essentially a rewording, not a signal of a labor-market shift, and both versions still describe the unemployment rate as "remained low." Given the pullback from 3.90 to 3.70, and the still-low absolute level (-0.30pp from natural rate), I see a modestly loosening bias remains from the 12-month trend, but with much reduced urgency after the retracement. This is a borderline call between flat and a mild up-lean.
Would change its mind if: A further rise back to 3.90%+ would revive the loosening signal; a drop to 3.50-3.60% would confirm the market remains genuinely tight.
Leaned on: unrate_change_12m, unrate_change_6m, unrate_change_3m, sahm_gap, unrate_range_position, unrate_distance_from_natural, unrate_12m_low

=== term_premium ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.35

  The prior "up" call has been overtaken by the data. The 10y last printed 4.37, down from readings near 4.42-4.47 a few observations earlier, and the 21-day change is -0.51pp — a fairly sharp decline. That is exactly the reversal condition I set previously (pullback below ~4.45 with negative 21d momentum), so I was wrong to hold an "up" bias into this window; the near-term trend has clearly turned lower even though the 63d and 252d changes remain positive, showing the longer-run uptrend is still intact but has stalled/reversed recently. The yield sits at 0.64 of its 12-month range — above the midpoint but well off the highs — consistent with a level that has room to ease further before hitting the low end. The 30s10s slope at +0.17pp is modest and not flattening aggressively, so no acute term-premium compression signal there, but it's not steepening either. Balance-sheet runoff continues per the unchanged policy language, which is a mild structural upward pressure on term premium, but it's being outweighed right now by the recent price action. Net: momentum has turned down after a multi-month climb, so I lean toward a flat-to-lower yield at month end rather than continuing the "up" call.
Would change its mind if: A rebound back above ~4.45 with renewed positive 21d momentum, or a steepening 30s10s beyond +0.25pp, would revive the upward call.

That said, the move is small and headline yoy remains within a tight recent range (3.07-3.72 over the last several prints), well off the 12-month high. Core PCE continues to decelerate steadily (3.83→3.69→3.47→3.29), and the core_yoy_change_3m of -0.54pp shows genuine underlying disinflation momentum. The headline/core gap has closed to near zero, meaning headline is no longer being flattered by falling energy — it's now tracking core more closely, and core is still falling. The 3m annualized rates (headline 1.92%, core 2.31%) are both consistent with target, suggesting the trend is disinflationary even though the single yoy print ticked up. Given headline momentum is choppy month to month but the underlying core trend and 3m annualized readings still point down, I lean toward a flat-to-slightly-down call for next month rather than continued acceleration, but with reduced conviction given this print's surprise.
Would change its mind if: A headline m/m print above 0.35% or two consecutive yoy increases above 3.4% would confirm renewed acceleration rather than noise.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, core_pce_yoy, core_yoy_change_3m, headline_3m_annualized, core_3m_annualized, core_minus_headline, headline_mom

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: up, conviction 0.45
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 23 days ago.
Report:
  The last print rose to 3.80% from 3.70%, so my prior "flat" call undershot — the loosening drift continued rather than stabilizing. Looking at the full run: unemployment has climbed from a 3.40% trailing low to 3.80%, a 0.40pp rise that sits right at the Sahm-rule trigger threshold (0.40-0.50pp is the classic recession-signal band), with the 3m change (+0.10pp) and 6m change (+0.20pp) both positive and the 12m change (+0.30pp) confirming a sustained, not one-off, drift higher. The range position (0.80 of the 12-month range) shows the rate is near the top of its recent band, not bouncing at the bottom. That said, the series is noisy month to month (3.70→3.90→3.70→3.80 zigzag over the last four prints), and the level is still only 0.20pp below the ~4% natural-rate anchor, so this isn't a decisively loose market yet — just a market that has clearly stopped tightening and is drifting looser. Given the accumulating rise off the low and proximity to the Sahm threshold, I lean toward another modest uptick or at least no reversal, rather than flat.
Would change its mind if: A drop back to 3.60-3.70% would confirm mean-reversion and genuine labor-market tightness; a further rise above 3.90% would confirm accelerating loosening.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_6m, unrate_change_12m, sahm_gap, unrate_range_position, unrate_12m_low, unrate_distance_from_natural

=== term_premium ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 10-year yield last printed 3.88%, up modestly from 3.79% two readings back but still well within a declining trend over the past 21d (-0.39pp) and 63d (-0.71pp). My prior "flat" call undersold the extent of the decline that had already occurred; looking at the full picture, the driver has clearly been falling over the past one to three months, not flat. The yield sits at only 0.35 of its 12-month range, closer to the low (3.30%) than the high (4.98%), consistent with a falling term premium regime rather than a stable one. The last two prints (3.79→3.84→3.88) show a small bounce off the recent local low, but this is minor relative to the larger multi-week decline and doesn't yet constitute a trend reversal. The 30s10s slope is modest at +0.15pp, not signaling aggressive long-end cheapening from steepening. Continued balance-sheet runoff is a structural upward force on term premium, but it hasn't been enough to offset the recent risk-off/flight-to-quality-type pull lower. Given the recent bounce, I'll shade toward flat-to-slightly-up for the month-ahead horizon, but with low conviction given the conflicting recent-multi-week decline versus last-3-day uptick.
Would change its mind if: A break back below 3.79% with renewed negative 21d momentum would confirm the down move; a push above 4.0% with steepening 30s10s beyond +0.25pp would confirm up.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_range_position, slope_30s10s

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: down, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last 13 weekly prints show continued balance sheet runoff, though the pace has decelerated markedly. Total assets fell from ~7.867T to 7.677T over the window, with 13w change -230.6B and 52w change -793.3B confirming a sustained downtrend. However, the most recent 4-week change is only -35.6B (about -8.9B/week average), noticeably slower than the 13w average pace of roughly -17.7B/week, and the last two prints (7,673,741 then 7,677,230) show a small uptick week-over-week. This could reflect normal noise around a slowing runoff cap, month-end settlement effects, or early signs of reserve-scarcity-driven deceleration, but it is not yet a reversal. Policy language remains unchanged, explicitly reaffirming continued balance-sheet reduction per previously announced plans — no taper or pause has been announced. My prior "down" call was directionally correct: assets did continue falling. Given the unchanged policy language and still-negative 4w/13w/52w trends, I maintain a down call for month-end, but with reduced conviction given the recent deceleration and the tiny uptick in the last observation, which warrants watching for a possible pace change or pause signal.
Would change its mind if: Two consecutive weekly rises above recent levels, or policy statement announcing pause/taper/end of runoff.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_change_52w, assets_pct_change_13w

Call: down, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead

Report:
  The prior call (steepening/up, conviction 0.50) was largely wrong for the most recent leg: after peaking near -0.14, the spread fell back to -0.30 then -0.28, a renewed flattening over the last two prints. The 21d change is still marginally positive (+0.07) but the 63d change is now negative (-0.13), showing the steepening impulse has faded and partially reversed. The 252d change (+0.42) confirms the multi-quarter steepening trend from deep inversion is intact structurally, and slope_range_position (0.84) shows we're still near the high end of the 12-month range (least-inverted levels), well above the 12m low. However, the most recent two observations are the least steep in the last several prints, indicating short-term momentum has turned down. Policy language shifted from emphasizing "slowed growth" and "additional policy firming" to "solid pace" expansion and no imminent cuts until greater confidence on inflation — this leans toward the Fed holding higher for longer, which typically supports front-end anchoring but doesn't clearly dictate long-end direction. Given the conflicting signals — longer-term steepening trend vs. recent 2-print pullback and negative 63d momentum — I lean toward a modest flattening bias near-term, but without high conviction given the level is still far from extreme inversion and range position is high.
Would change its mind if: A move back above -0.16 with slope_change_21d and 63d both turning positive would confirm resumed steepening and reverse this call.
Leaned on: slope_2s10s, slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d, slope_change_252d, slope_range_position, slope_12m_high

Call: down, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The NFCI series continues its steady, uninterrupted grind lower, now at -0.39, a fresh 52-week low, with nfci_range_position at 0.00. My prior call (down) was correct: the index has moved from -0.28 to -0.39 over this window, and the last observation (-0.39) is the lowest of the entire 13-print run, showing no stalling or reversal — the falsifier condition (a sustained rise back toward -0.32+) did not occur. Momentum remains negative: 4-week change is -0.03 and 13-week change is -0.10, both consistently negative with no deceleration visible in the last few prints (-0.36, -0.36, -0.37, -0.38, -0.39). Conditions are now well below the zero average, indicating looser-than-average financial conditions, and the trend shows no sign of bottoming. The policy language describes past tightening effects still working through the economy but does not itself signal a shift in current financial conditions data. Given the persistence and lack of any inflection, I expect this easing trend to continue into month end, though I temper conviction slightly since the pace of decline is modest and could flatten given how far the index already sits from its average.
Would change its mind if: A rise back toward -0.34 or higher sustained over 2+ weeks would signal the easing trend has stalled or reversed.
Leaned on: nfci, nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_52w_low, nfci_range_position, nfci_distance_from_zero

Call: flat, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last two prints are flat at 2.26, following a small step down from 2.30. My prior "down" call at 0.35 conviction was directionally consistent with the 63-day and 252-day trends (-0.18pp, -0.03pp), but the most recent data shows stabilization rather than continued decline: the series has been essentially pinned at 2.26-2.30 for the last several observations, and the 21-day change is actually +0.10pp, reflecting the earlier bounce before settling. The breakeven remains 0.26pp above the 2% target, sitting at 0.38 of its 12-month range (2.10-2.52), which is roughly the middle-to-lower-middle of the band — not signaling strong momentum in either direction. There's no evidence of a break below the 12-month low or a sustained push higher; the two most recent readings being identical suggests the series has found a local equilibrium after its modest pullback from 2.34. Given the flattening in the last two prints and the absence of fresh momentum signals in either direction, I'm revising toward flat. The prior down call is losing support as the decline appears to have stalled rather than accelerated.
Would change its mind if: A move below 2.20 sustained over multiple prints would revive the downside case; a push above 2.34 would signal renewed upside momentum.

=== inflation ===
Call: down, conviction 0.35
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 16 days ago.
Report:
  Last release: headline CPI y/y ticked up to 3.09% from 3.32%... wait, actually the newest value (3.09) is below the prior (3.32), so headline actually fell, not rose — my prior "flat" call undershot the disinflation that continued. The latest m/m print (0.31%) was below my 0.35% acceleration threshold, so no trigger was hit, consistent with holding flat-to-down. Headline y/y sits at 3.09%, near its 12-month low (3.07%), and core PCE y/y continues a steady descent to 3.11% from 3.29%, a -0.58pp move over three months. Core 3m annualized (1.79%) is now below target-consistent pace, and headline 3m annualized (2.63%) is also much cooler than the trailing y/y suggests. The mom_gap_vs_outgoing is negative (-0.13pp), meaning the latest month's pace decelerated versus the recent average. Policy language was revised to acknowledge "inflation has eased over the past year," a explicit acknowledgment of the disinflation trend, though "remains elevated" is retained. Combining soft 3-month annualized rates, continued core y/y decline, and the low position in the 12-month range, the momentum is disinflationary. Headline could still tick up modestly on base effects (given the sequence of past m/m prints rolling off), but the underlying trend and 3m pace point to flat-to-down for the headline next print.
Would change its mind if: A headline m/m print above 0.40% or core PCE y/y ticking back up above 3.3% would confirm reacceleration rather than continued disinflation.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, core_pce_yoy, core_3m_annualized, headline_3m_annualized, core_yoy_change_3m, mom_gap_vs_outgoing, yoy_range_position

The series is noisy around 3.5-3.9% with no clean trend: 3m change is now -0.20pp (tightening), while 6m and 12m changes are still +0.20pp (mild loosening from a year ago). The Sahm gap of 0.33pp is well below the 0.50 trigger threshold, and the level itself (3.70%) is only modestly above the 4% natural-rate anchor (-0.30pp distance), so this is not a tight labor market by historical standards but also not showing accelerating deterioration. Range position (0.60) shows we're in the upper-middle of the 12-month band, consistent with oscillation rather than a breakout. Given the whipsaw pattern (3.9→3.7→3.8→3.7), the most defensible read is that the market is bouncing within a narrow band rather than trending decisively either way. I'll shade slightly toward flat/up given the still-positive 6m/12m drift, but with much lower conviction than before since the last move confirmed noise, not signal.
Would change its mind if: A print below 3.60% would confirm renewed tightening; a print above 3.90% would confirm a genuine breakout and accelerating loosening.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_6m, unrate_change_12m, sahm_gap, unrate_range_position, unrate_distance_from_natural

=== term_premium ===

Looking at the recent path: 4.14→4.15→4.11→4.14→4.18→4.14→4.15→4.08→4.06→3.99 — a clear downtrend in the last four readings, with the level breaking below 4.0%. My prior 'flat' call was wrong — the yield broke decisively below 4.0% with clear negative recent momentum, which is closer to my own down-confirmation criterion (albeit not quite through 3.79%). Given the fresh break below 4.0% and negative momentum in the last three observations, I lean toward continued softness in the term premium proxy through month end, though I stop short of high conviction since the longer trend and range position don't scream extreme.
Would change its mind if: A rebound back above 4.10% with 21d momentum turning positive and 30s10s steepening further would reverse this down call.

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: down, conviction 0.78

The most recent 4-week change is -$82.3B and 13-week change is -$274.8B, both consistent with continued QT at (or slightly above) the announced monthly cap pace. There is no sign of deceleration — the last few prints (7,439,558 → 7,438,176 → 7,405,506 → 7,402,434) show the runoff continuing smoothly, with the latest weekly decline (~$3B) smaller than some prior weeks but still negative, not a reversal. Assets remain $1.1T below the 52-week high, and the pct_change_13w of -3.58% confirms an ongoing, non-trivial contraction. Policy language explicitly reaffirms continued reduction of Treasury and agency holdings per previously announced plans — no taper or pause signaled. My prior down call was correct: the four weeks since have shown continued declines, matching expectations. Given unchanged policy language and a consistent multi-week downtrend with no stabilization or reversal signal, I expect total assets to be lower again at next month-end, likely by a similar magnitude to recent 4-week changes (roughly $60-90B), absent any new policy announcement.
Would change its mind if: Two consecutive weekly rises above recent levels, or a policy statement announcing pause/taper/end of runoff.

The spread sits at -0.35, near the middle-to-upper part of its 12-month range (range_position 0.77, well off the -1.08 low), so the curve remains meaningfully inverted but not near its worst level. The most recent two prints (-0.34, -0.35) suggest the flattening move has resumed and is not yet reversing. With policy language unchanged (Fed on hold, data-dependent, no explicit signal), there's no fresh catalyst. Given the choppy, non-trending pattern within a fairly narrow band over the last few readings, I lean toward continued modest flattening but with low conviction given the whipsaw.

The move is too small and too recent to call a trend; it needs to persist or accelerate to be meaningful. Given the tiny 4-week change (+0.02) against a 13-week change of -0.02, the signal is genuinely mixed — a short-term uptick within a longer flat-to-slightly-easing window. I'll lean toward flat again, with a slight tilt toward up given the recent 3-observation uptick, but conviction remains modest.

Call: flat, conviction 0.30

My prior "up" call was directionally weak — headline y/y actually ticked down from 3.49 to 3.36, so I was wrong to expect further acceleration; the sequence topped out and is now easing back. Core PCE y/y also ticked up marginally to 3.12 from 3.06, essentially flat and still hovering near 3.1%, not falling through 3.0% as my falsifier specified, but not accelerating either. The 3m annualized rates (headline 4.36%, core 4.78%) remain well above the 2% target and above the recent y/y readings, meaning underlying momentum is still running hot relative to the trailing year-over-year figure — this argues for some upward or sideways drift in coming prints rather than renewed disinflation. However, the single-month deceleration in headline m/m (0.22% vs 0.36% avg) and the pullback in headline y/y from its recent peak both point toward flat-to-slightly-down near-term. Net: momentum indicators are mixed — 3m annualized still elevated, but latest monthly print decelerating. I'll call this closer to flat, leaning down slightly, given the topping pattern in y/y and cooling in the latest month.
Would change its mind if: A rebound in headline m/m above 0.35% or core PCE y/y breaking above 3.3% would revive the acceleration case; sustained sub-0.20% prints would confirm disinflation.

The unemployment rate sits at the top of its 12-month range (range_position=1.00), 0.40pp above the 12-month low, with a Sahm gap of 0.40pp — close to but not yet crossing the classic 0.50 recession-signal threshold. The 3-month change is +0.20pp (mild upward drift) while the 6-month change is flat at 0.00pp, suggesting the rise has stalled rather than accelerated. Policy language actually turned slightly more upbeat, dropping the "moderated" qualifier and simply stating job gains "have remained strong" alongside a still-low unemployment rate — a modestly hawkish-on-labor shift in tone that argues against imminent further loosening. Level (3.90%) remains close to the natural rate (-0.10pp distance), not stretched. Given the plateau at the high end of the range with no fresh deterioration and firmer policy language, I lean toward flat, with a slight tilt up given proximity to the Sahm threshold and the recent 3m uptick.
Would change its mind if: A print at 4.00%+ would confirm renewed loosening; a drop back to 3.70-3.80% would confirm stabilization/reversal rather than plateau.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_6m, unrate_3m_avg, sahm_gap, unrate_range_position, unrate_distance_from_natural

=== term_premium ===
Call: up, conviction 0.45
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 10-year yield has drifted higher over the last ten readings, from 4.59 to 4.69, with the last print at 4.69 near the top of the recent cluster (4.61-4.70 range). Momentum measures confirm this: +0.36pp over 21 days, +0.63pp over 63 days, and +1.16pp over 252 days — all positive and accelerating in the shorter windows, indicating sustained upward pressure rather than a one-off move. The yield sits at 0.82 of its 12-month range (position near the high end), well above the 12-month low (+1.32pp above it) and close to the 12-month high. The 30s10s slope is positive at +0.10pp, consistent with modest additional long-end premium building relative to the 10-year itself, not a flattening that would signal receding term premium. Continued balance-sheet runoff (still-active QT language) provides an ongoing structural tailwind for higher long-end yields via increased net supply absorption needs. My prior "flat" call was too cautious given this data was already trending up — the level and momentum evidence look more consistent with a genuine, if moderate, upward drift than noise. I'm revising to a modest up call.
Would change its mind if: A drop back below 4.55% with negative 21d momentum and 30s10s flattening would reverse this call toward flat or down.

Front-end drivers (labor_tightness flat/plateaued, inflation flat-to-slightly-down) give no reason to expect the front end pricing in cuts or hikes aggressively — Fed on hold, unemployment plateaued near cycle high but not deteriorating further, core inflation still hot on 3m annualized basis. The curve_slope analyst's own recent-print flattening looks like it's fighting the more fundamentally-grounded long-end story; I weight term_premium and balance_sheet's coherent, higher-conviction, freshly-formed reads over curve_slope's low-conviction whipsaw call. === balance_sheet ===

I see no catalyst in the policy language (unchanged boilerplate) to alter this. Policy language shift is notable — dropping "moving into better balance" for backward-looking "moved toward better balance over the past year" alongside a new line about "lack of further progress" suggests the Committee itself sees stalled progress, a mild hawkish tilt in rhetoric. But the hard data — especially the low outgoing m/m and continued core PCE decline — argues for at least a modest downward drift in next month's headline y/y, absent a rebound. I lean modestly toward a lower headline print next month, tempered by base-effect and monthly noise risk.

The level itself (3.90%) is essentially at the 4% natural-rate benchmark, not stretched either way. With three consecutive flat prints at the cycle high, the signal is one of stabilization at a slightly elevated level rather than acceleration in either direction. Absent a fresh move, the most defensible call for the next report is flat, though the plateau at the range ceiling means any tick up would confirm renewed loosening while any tick down would suggest genuine improvement.

The level (4.51%) remains elevated versus the 12-month low (3.61%) and closer to the high (4.98%), so the medium-term uptrend in term premium is intact, but short-term momentum has clearly cooled. The Fed's announced slowing of Treasury runoff (cap cut from $60bn to $25bn) reduces net supply pressure from balance-sheet reduction, which should modestly ease upward pressure on long-end yields going forward. Given the conflicting signals — medium-term uptrend vs. short-term reversal plus reduced runoff pressure — I lean toward a flat-to-slightly-lower call for the next month, with low-to-moderate conviction given the mixed picture.

The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: down, conviction 0.62
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The previous "down" call was correct: total assets fell again to 7,231,163 from 7,252,542, continuing an unbroken multi-week decline. The 4-week change (-53,156) and 13-week change (-253,576, -3.39%) confirm steady runoff, with assets now over $1.07T below the 52w high. The policy language explicitly continues QT, though it announces a slowing of pace: the Treasury redemption cap drops from $60B to $25B/month starting in [date] while the agency MBS/debt cap stays at $35B with excess reinvested into Treasuries. This is a deceleration of runoff, not a reversal — total assets will still decline, just more slowly going forward. Given the cap cut is roughly 60% reduction in the Treasury runoff pace, the actual monthly decline in fed_assets should shrink from recent ~$50-60B/4wk readings toward a lower rate, though MBS runoff and Treasury reinvestment-into-Treasuries provisions complicate the net pace. Directionally, assets remain firmly in runoff with no signal of pause or expansion. I maintain a "down" call but trim conviction to reflect that the shrinking cap will likely produce a smaller decline next month than the trailing pace suggests, and reinvestment mechanics add some uncertainty to the exact magnitude.
Would change its mind if: Two consecutive weekly rises in fed_assets, or a statement announcing pause/end of runoff/reinvestment exceeding caps into growth.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_pct_change_13w

  The 2s10s spread has steepened steadily over the series, moving from -0.47 to -0.35, with the most recent print making a fresh high in this window. My prior "flat" call was too cautious — the slope has continued to steepen since, and the -0.35 print is the least-inverted level in the sample. slope_change_63d (+0.04) and slope_change_252d (+0.62) both confirm the medium-term steepening trend is intact, even though slope_change_21d is flat at 0.00, suggesting the latest move was a discrete step rather than a smooth grind. slope_range_position at 0.77 shows the spread sits well into the upper part of its 12-month range (low -1.08, high -0.13), meaning it has room to keep steepening toward the top of the range before hitting resistance. The slowing of balance-sheet runoff (redemption cap cut) is consistent with modestly greater front-end anchoring relative to duration supply concerns, a mild steepening bias. With no clear reversal signal and the trend line pointing up, I favor continued steepening, though the flat 21-day change urges some caution against calling it a strong unambiguous move.
Would change its mind if: A drop back below -0.42 with slope_change_21d turning clearly negative would signal renewed flattening and invalidate this call.

This is a slow, grinding tightening rather than a sharp move — the index remains deeply negative (-0.39, well below zero), so conditions are still much looser than average. My prior flat call undersold the persistence of this drift; the trend has been consistently one-directional (never reversing) across the last several prints, which is a stronger signal than I gave credit for. There's no fresh acceleration or policy commentary to suggest a regime shift, so I don't expect a sharp move, but the steady grind toward less-negative territory looks likely to continue modestly through month end. Core PCE y/y also decelerated to 2.77% from 3.01%, continuing a steady downtrend and now within 0.77pp of target. The 3-month annualized headline pace (0.90%) is well below the trailing y/y rate, and the 3-month average m/m (0.07%) is far under the outgoing print, both signaling further disinflation momentum rather than a rebound. Core's 3m annualized (2.81%) is still a bit above target but consistent with continued gradual progress, and policy language upgraded from "lack of further progress" to "modest further progress," corroborating the data turn. None of my reversal triggers (m/m above 0.35% or core PCE y/y above 3.2%) were hit — in fact both moved further in the disinflationary direction. Given a negative m/m print and broad-based deceleration in both core and headline gauges, I expect headline CPI y/y to be flat-to-slightly-lower next month, though the very low base for m/m comparisons a year ago could produce some year-over-year noise in either direction, tempering confidence in a strong further decline.
Would change its mind if: A headline m/m print rebounding above 0.30% or core PCE y/y ticking back above 3.0% would reverse this toward flat/up.

  The latest print jumped to 4.10% from a plateau of 3.90% held over the prior four readings, which is exactly the loosening confirmation I flagged as my falsifier last time (4.00%+). This was not a one-off wobble: the 3m change (+0.20pp), 6m change (+0.30pp), and 12m change (+0.50pp) all point the same direction, and the rate is now sitting at the 12-month high, at the top of its range (range_position 1.00). The Sahm-rule-style gap (current vs 3m avg trough) is 0.47pp, uncomfortably close to the classic 0.5pp recession-signal threshold, indicating this is a rapid rise off the trailing low rather than noise. Distance from the ~4% natural rate is now +0.10pp, meaning we've crossed from tight into modestly loose territory. Policy language still describes job gains as "strong" and unemployment as "low," but the subtle rewording — risks "have moved toward better balance over the past year" (past tense) versus previously "are moving" (present, ongoing) — reads as an acknowledgment that the labor side has already shifted, consistent with the data. I was wrong to call flat last time; the plateau broke to the upside. Given acceleration and level both now unfavorable, I lean toward further softening next month, though one print is not yet a firm trend.
Would change its mind if: A pullback to 3.90% or below would suggest the 4.10% print was noise rather than a genuine trend break.

The policy shift — slowing balance-sheet runoff by cutting the Treasury redemption cap from $60bn to $25bn — is a reduction in intended net Treasury supply pressure from the Fed's own portfolio, which is a modest disinflationary/dovish-for-duration signal for the long end (less runoff-driven supply hitting the market), arguing against a sharp continued rise. My prior flat call was roughly right in direction (yield only modestly higher, still within recent range), though the last print's move to 4.36 is a bit firmer than a pure flat reading would suggest. Net: slight upward drift in evidence but tempered by reduced QT pace working against it — I lean toward a small further increase but without strong conviction.
