user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: down, conviction 0.75
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The previous "down" call was correct and the trend has if anything accelerated. Total assets fell every single week across the 13-week window, with no counter-week at all. The 4-week change (-108,350) is larger in magnitude than one-third of the 13-week change annualized pace would suggest, indicating the runoff pace picked up rather than decelerated — consistent with continued balance-sheet normalization at or near the announced monthly caps. The 52-week change (-891,005) confirms a sustained, large-scale multi-quarter drawdown, and assets sit ~834,426 below the 52-week high with no sign of basing. Policy language remains unchanged: the Committee continues to reduce holdings of Treasuries, agency debt, and agency MBS, with no mention of tapering, pausing, or ending runoff. Given the steady weekly declines, an accelerating 4-week pace, and stable policy language reaffirming continued reduction, the highest-probability outcome for month end is another decline in total assets, roughly in line with or slightly larger than the recent 4-week pace (~$100-110B). No evidence of a policy pivot or reserve-scarcity concern is present. I hold conviction fairly high given the unanimity of the weekly declines and unchanged guidance.
Would change its mind if: Two consecutive weekly rises in fed_assets, or a statement announcing pause/end of runoff/reinvestment exceeding caps into growth.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_pct_change_13w, assets_change_52w, assets_below_52w_high

=== curve_slope ===
Call: down, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 2s10s spread has drifted down to 0.05 from a recent range near 0.09-0.13, with slope_change_21d at -0.12pp — a clear negative move over the past month. This is exactly the falsifier condition I set previously: a drop to/below 0.05 with 21d change clearly negative. My prior "up" call was wrong; the curve has flattened, not steepened, over the last month. Longer-term context still shows steepening (slope_change_252d +0.50pp, range_position 0.73 — spread sits in upper part of 12-month range), so this isn't a collapse back toward deep inversion, but the recent trend is a pause/reversal in the steepening trend rather than continuation. The 63d change is only +0.04pp, essentially flat, confirming the steepening has stalled. The Fed's shift to smaller rate cuts (1/4 point vs prior 1/2 point) suggests a slower, more gradual easing path, which could keep front-end yields somewhat supported and cap further curve steepening near-term, consistent with the recent flattening tick. Absent a clear catalyst to resume steepening, I expect the spread to stay near current depressed levels or drift slightly lower over the next month, with genuine uncertainty given the choppy recent action (0.09→0.06→0.05).
Would change its mind if: A rebound above 0.09 with slope_change_21d turning positive would signal resumed steepening and invalidate this flat/down call.
Leaned on: slope_2s10s, slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d, slope_range_position, slope_10s30s

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: down, conviction 0.60
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The NFCI series continues its steady, low-volatility decline, printing a fresh 52-week low at -0.47, well below the historical zero average and below its own 13-week average (-0.43). The 4-week change (-0.03) and 13-week change (-0.09) both confirm a persistent, monotonic easing trend rather than a stall — the last observation is the lowest in the window and the series has fallen in nearly every consecutive reading with no reversal. My prior "down" call at 0.60 conviction was correct: conditions kept easing, not tightening. The range_position of 0.00 confirms we are sitting at the bottom of the observed 52-week range, and there's no sign of flattening — the 4-week change magnitude is similar to prior periods, suggesting the trend has momentum rather than exhaustion. Absent any policy language pointing to a shift, and with no measurement showing deceleration in the pace of decline, the most reasonable one-month-ahead call remains continued easing (lower NFCI). I'd flag that the pace (-0.01/week average) is modest, so this is a clear but not extreme signal — a plateau near current lows is plausible, but the data gives no actual evidence of one yet.
Would change its mind if: A reading back above -0.43 (13-week average) or a flattening of the 4-week change toward zero would signal the easing trend has stalled or reversed.
Leaned on: nfci, nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_distance_from_zero, nfci_13w_avg, nfci_52w_low, nfci_range_position

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: down, conviction 0.45
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last leg of the series shows a fairly clear downward drift: after holding near 2.34 for several readings, breakeven has stepped down to 2.26 over the last three observations, with the 21-day change now -0.04pp (having flipped negative) even though the 63-day and 252-day changes remain modestly positive at +0.11pp and +0.05pp. This suggests the recent short-term momentum has turned lower after an earlier stabilization/uptick phase. My prior "flat" call is now looking stale — the sequence 2.34,2.34,2.29,2.28,2.26 is a persistent, monotonic decline over the last five prints, not noise within a range. Range position at 0.59 (of the 12-month 2.02-2.43 band) is still mid-range, not at an extreme, and the level remains 0.26pp above the 2% target, so this isn't a dramatic dislocation. But the direction of travel over the most recent weeks is unambiguously down, and there's no sign of reversal in the last three points. I'll shift from flat to a modest downward lean, since the move is real but not yet large enough (still within 12-month range, not breaching 2.20) to warrant high conviction of a sustained trend break.
Would change its mind if: A bounce back above 2.30 over the next couple of readings would indicate the recent dip was noise, not a new downtrend.
Leaned on: breakeven_10y, be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_range_position, be_above_12m_low

=== inflation ===
Call: up, conviction 0.55
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 15 days ago.
Report:
  The prior "up" call was correct: headline CPI y/y rose from 2.58 to 2.72 (+0.14pp), continuing the bottoming-out pattern that began after the 2.43 trough. Momentum confirms this isn't noise: headline mom printed 0.28% (above both outgoing 0.19% and the 3m avg 0.26%), and headline 3m annualized sits at 3.18%, well above the current y/y rate — meaning the y/y figure has further upside baked in mechanically as soft months roll off. Core PCE y/y also climbed to 2.99 from 2.84, its third consecutive increase off the 2.75 low, with core 3m annualized at 3.03%, reinforcing that underlying persistence has stopped decelerating. The removal of "further" and "gained greater confidence" from the policy language (softened confidence framing) is consistent with officials recognizing this stall/reacceleration rather than continued disinflation. Both headline and core now agree on direction, which strengthens conviction versus prior instances of divergence. Level is still moderate (yoy_range_position 0.28, core still ~1pp above target) so this isn't alarming, but momentum clearly points up for the next print given the annualized run-rate gap versus y/y.
Would change its mind if: A headline mom print below 0.15% or core PCE y/y reversing back under 2.85% would flip this toward flat.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, core_pce_yoy, headline_mom, headline_3m_annualized, core_3m_annualized, headline_yoy_change_3m, core_yoy_change_3m, outgoing_mom, mom_gap_vs_outgoing

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: up, conviction 0.45
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 22 days ago.
Report:
  The last print rose to 4.20% from 4.10%, matching the cycle high and triggering the falsifier I set previously (a print above/at the prior high after two prints at 4.10%). My prior "flat" call was too sanguine given the data was already at the top of its range with a 0.43pp Sahm gap. The 12-month trend is clearly up (+0.50pp), the 3m average sits at 4.13% versus a 12m low of 3.70%, and the rate is now pinned at the top of its 13-month range (range_position = 1.00). While the 3m change is technically flat (4.10→4.10→4.20 oscillation), the level has now printed at the cycle high twice in the last four readings, and policy language has shifted to acknowledge labor conditions have "generally eased" — a more explicit softening characterization than the prior statement's narrower framing. This is consistent with a gradual loosening trend rather than a stable plateau. I do not see evidence of re-tightening (no move back toward 3.9% or below). Given the persistent upward drift, proximity to the Sahm threshold, and confirmatory policy language, I lean toward another modest increase or at least a hold at the elevated level, but conviction is moderate since the 3m change is flat and one print isn't decisive confirmation of accelerating deterioration.
Would change its mind if: A drop back to 4.0% or below would signal the rise was noise rather than a genuine loosening trend.
Leaned on: unrate_change_12m, unrate_range_position, sahm_gap, unrate_3m_avg, unrate_change_6m

=== term_premium ===
Call: down, conviction 0.40
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 10-year has drifted down over the last several observations, from 4.41 to 4.18, with the most recent print (4.18) the lowest in the sample and approaching the threshold (4.15) I flagged as a reversal signal last time. The 21-day change is -0.10pp and the trend within the last few readings shows a clear step-down (4.41→4.27→4.30→4.25→4.18), suggesting downward momentum has picked up even though the 63-day change is still positive (+0.34pp) reflecting the earlier run-up. Range position at 0.51 is mid-range, not extreme, so this isn't a low-yield extreme, just a fresh pullback. The 30s10s slope at +0.18pp is modest, not showing aggressive steepening that would signal renewed term-premium building at the long end; 10s2s at +0.05 is flat. My prior 'flat' call was too cautious — the recent run of lower prints is a real, if not yet dramatic, down-move. Given continued balance-sheet runoff pressure as a persistent upward force, I don't expect a large decline, but the immediate trend and proximity to the 4.15 reversal marker argue for leaning down rather than flat for the coming month.
Would change its mind if: A rebound back above 4.35 with steepening 30s10s would confirm reversal upward; a break clearly below 4.15 would confirm this down call.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_range_position, slope_30s10s, slope_10s2s

Labor tightness is flat/noisy and doesn't clearly hit front vs. long differently — a soft labor read would typically support front-end anchoring (Fed on hold, no cuts needed), which is neutral-to-mildly-steepening but low conviction given "flat" call. Inflation and inflation expectations are drivers I'm discounting per mandate (level-focused, not directly in my remit), though the modest up-drift in both is broadly consistent with a term-premium/steepening bias rather than flattening. Net: converging evidence — direct slope reading, long-end term premium, and balance-sheet runoff — all point to gentle continued steepening, but with capped conviction given the slope's proximity to its range top and stalling momentum.

arbitrated.falsifier: A drop in 2s10s back below 0.28 with negative 21d change, or 10Y breaking below 4.45 with 30s10s flattening, would flip this to flat/flattening.

risks.text: Spread already near 12-month highs, leaving limited room for further steepening before mean-reversion.
Fed holding rates removes front-end downward pressure that had been driving steepening, could stall the trend.
Term premium's 21d flatness could presage a broader stall in long-end yield rise, weakening steepening thesis.
Crowded steepener positioning into month-end could unwind sharply on any dovish surprise.

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: down, conviction 0.55

Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior call (down, 0.75) was correct: total assets fell further, from 6,755,982 to 6,740,253, continuing the steady runoff pattern. The 4-week change (-25,848) and 13-week change (-145,710) confirm QT is ongoing, with assets 699,305 below the 52-week high and no sign of reversal in the weekly series - every observation in the last 13 weeks is at or below the prior one. The key new information is the policy language: effective the following month, the Treasury redemption cap is being cut from $25B to $5B, a sharp slowing of the pace of decline, while the agency MBS/debt cap stays at $35B (though actual paydowns rarely hit that cap). This is not a pause or reversal - the Committee explicitly continues to reduce holdings - but the pace of runoff should decelerate materially going forward. So for month-end, I expect assets to still be lower than the current reading, since redemptions continue, but the magnitude of decline should shrink noticeably compared to the recent ~$25-30B/4wk pace, dominated by the now-larger relative role of agency/MBS runoff and slower Treasury paydowns. Direction remains down, but conviction is lower than before given the deliberate slowing.
Would change its mind if: A weekly rise in fed_assets, or a statement pausing/ending runoff entirely, would change this view.
Leaned on: fed_assets, assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_below_52w_high

=== curve_slope ===
Call: down, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last observation shows slope_2s10s pulling back to 0.34 from a peak of 0.41, the second consecutive down-tick (0.41→0.38→0.34). My prior "flat" call is now looking stale: the series clearly steepened over the past year (slope_change_252d +0.66pp, slope_change_63d +0.03pp still positive) and broke well above my 0.27 trigger, confirming the steepening case I said I'd revive on. But the most recent two prints show that steepening losing momentum and reversing modestly. The spread sits at 0.92 of its 12-month range (near the high), which increases the chance of near-term consolidation or a pullback rather than fresh steepening, simply on mean-reversion/positioning grounds. The slowing of balance-sheet runoff (redemption cap cut from $25bn to $5bn) is a technical long-end supportive/duration-reducing move that plausibly caps further steepening pressure by easing bill/coupon supply dynamics, consistent with the recent flattening in the last two prints. Net: the trend this month has flipped from steepening to a mild flattening within an otherwise steep range. I'll shift to a modest "down" lean rather than flat, given the two-period reversal, but keep conviction limited since the longer-term trend and range position argue against a large move.
Would change its mind if: A move back above 0.38-0.41 with slope_change_21d re-accelerating positive would confirm renewed steepening, negating this flattening lean.

Call: up, conviction 0.55

Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The NFCI series has moved steadily off its 52-week low of -0.51 toward -0.42, with the last several readings each higher than the prior one (-0.46, -0.44, -0.42). The 4-week change (+0.07) exceeds the 13-week change (+0.05), meaning the tightening trend is accelerating, not decaying. Range position at 0.55 shows the index is now past the midpoint of its 52-week range, consistent with a genuine shift rather than noise. My prior call (up, 0.40) was correct: conditions continued tightening from -0.44 to -0.42 rather than reverting toward the low. Conditions remain historically loose (distance from zero -0.42, still well below the long-run average of zero), so this is tightening from an easy baseline, not tight conditions outright. But the direction of travel is unambiguous and consistent across multiple recent prints, with no sign of the accelerating 4-week momentum stalling. Absent any offsetting signal, I expect the index to continue drifting higher toward zero over the next month, continuing the established short-term trend. The move is gradual (single-digit-hundredths per week) so this is a clear but not extreme signal.

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: up, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 10Y breakeven has drifted from 2.30 to 2.38 over the last ten readings, with the most recent print (2.38) up slightly from the prior (2.37) and roughly flat over 21 days (+0.00pp) but up modestly over both 63d (+0.04pp) and 252d (+0.06pp). My prior "flat" call was directionally reasonable in the very short window (21d change was indeed ~0) but the series has continued its gentle grind higher rather than stalling outright — the range position (0.82) shows breakevens sitting near the top of their 12-month band (2.02–2.46), well above the 12m low. Neither of my stated triggers (break below 2.35 sustained, or push above 2.42) has fired, so this remains a low-conviction call, but the persistent slow upward creep across multiple horizons and the elevated range position tilt slightly toward continued firmness rather than genuine flatness. Distance from the 2% target is still meaningfully positive (+0.38pp), consistent with inflation expectations remaining anchored somewhat above objective, but not accelerating sharply. I'll lean slightly up rather than flat, given the consistent multi-horizon positive drift, though conviction stays low given the tiny recent 21d move and no threshold breach.

=== inflation ===
Call: up, conviction 0.35
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 16 days ago.
Report:
  Last release: headline CPI y/y fell sharply to 2.38% from 2.80%, and the monthly print was just 0.03% m/m — well below my falsifier threshold of 0.35%, so the prior "down" call was validated, not falsified. That said, core PCE y/y actually rose to 2.97% from 2.78%, back above my other falsifier level (2.95%), which is a genuine cross-current. Core PCE has been oscillating in a tight 2.75-3.12% band with no clear trend (3m annualized core running hot at 3.87%), while headline's 3m annualized is a much cooler 2.78% and the y/y series just hit a fresh 12-month low. The divergence is real: headline is being flattered by favorable base effects/energy, while core — the better persistence signal — shows no decisive progress and if anything ticked up. Given the single very low 0.03% mom print, some mean-reversion in the next headline y/y reading is likely (base effects won't repeat, and momentum had been running ~0.22-0.23% for months prior). I weight core's stickiness as a caution against extrapolating the drop, but for the specific headline y/y horizon, a bounce off an unusually soft month is the more probable near-term move.
Would change its mind if: A headline mom print again below ~0.10% or core PCE y/y falling back under 2.85% would confirm continued disinflation, not a bounce.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, headline_mom, core_pce_yoy, headline_3m_annualized, core_3m_annualized, headline_yoy_12m_low

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.45
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 23 days ago.
Report:
  The latest print holds at 4.20%, matching the prior month rather than breaking above it — so my falsifier condition (a second consecutive print above 4.2%) was not met; the series is sitting at the range ceiling but not exceeding it. Over the last 13 months the rate has drifted from 3.90% to 4.20%, a +0.30pp 12-month change, but the 3m and 6m changes are both only +0.10pp, indicating the drift has largely stalled at this plateau. The range position sits at the top of its 12-month band (1.00) and the Sahm-style gap (+0.23pp) is still well below the ~0.5pp threshold that typically signals a genuine recessionary turn. Policy language has shifted constructively, now describing conditions as "stabilized at a low level" and "solid," a clear softening from the prior "generally eased" language — corroborating the flattening in the hard data. Given the rate is unchanged month-over-month, the pace of increase has decelerated to near-zero over the past couple of readings, and the qualitative language reinforces stabilization, I read this as a flat labor market at a slightly elevated but not deteriorating level. That is a long-end-specific force (less coupon supply pressure), which would compress term premium and disproportionately pull DOWN the long end relative to the front end — i.e., flattening, consistent with curve_slope's own read. Given the acceleration in the 21-day change and the proximity to the 12-month high (2.46) rather than the low (2.02), the more consistent read now is that inflation expectations are stabilizing or drifting mildly higher, not continuing to fall. I do not have strong conviction this is a sustained re-acceleration, but the flat-to-down thesis from last month is no longer well supported by the most recent print.
Would change its mind if: A drop back below 2.32 sustained for multiple readings, or be_change_21d turning negative again, would revert this to a down/flat call.
Leaned on: breakeven_10y, be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_change_252d, be_range_position, be_distance_from_target

=== inflation ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.35
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 16 days ago.
Report:
  Last release (newest headline_cpi_yoy = 2.38%) rose slightly from 2.33% the prior month, and my flat call was roughly correct — the move was small and within noise. Looking at the full picture now: headline y/y sits at 2.38%, near the bottom of its 12-month range (yoy_range_position 0.08, only 0.05pp above the 12m low), and headline_yoy_change_3m is -0.42pp, showing clear deceleration over the past quarter. The most recent mom print (0.10%) is below the 3m average (0.10% avg is actually equal, but well below the 0.43% peak seen a few months back), and headline_3m_annualized is just 1.18%, well under target-consistent pace. Core PCE has also cooled — 2.61% latest vs 2.99% three months prior, a -0.17pp drift down, though it remains 0.61pp above the 2% target and above headline (core_minus_headline +0.24pp), consistent with somewhat elevated underlying pressure. Policy language shifted to explicitly flagging inflation risk alongside employment risk, but this is a balance-of-risks statement, not evidence of re-acceleration. Given the deceleration trend in both headline 3m annualized and core y/y, plus the low mom print, I lean toward continued softness or a flat-to-slightly-lower headline print next month, not a rebound.
Would change its mind if: A headline mom print above 0.30% or core PCE y/y rebounding above 2.9% would signal renewed acceleration, invalidating this call.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, headline_3m_annualized, headline_yoy_change_3m, core_pce_yoy, core_yoy_change_3m, headline_mom, yoy_range_position

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: up, conviction 0.45
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 23 days ago.
Report:
  The latest print rose to 4.30%, a new 12-month high, up from 4.20% and confirming the loosening scenario I flagged as my falsifier last time. My prior flat call at 0.40 conviction was wrong: the rate did not stabilize at 4.1-4.2 as it had for months, it broke higher. The 3m change is now +0.10pp with the level moving to the top of its 12-month range (unrate_range_position = 1.00), and the 12m change is +0.40pp, a nontrivial cumulative drift away from the 4.0% recent low. Sahm-gap of 0.23pp is still below the classic 0.5pp recession-signal threshold, so this isn't a sharp acceleration, but it's not noise either — it's a second consecutive move to a fresh high after months of a tight 4.0-4.2 band. The policy language shift is notable: from "stabilized at a low level" to explicitly flagging "risks of higher unemployment... have risen," suggesting the committee itself sees the trend as tilting toward loosening rather than range-bound stability. Given the level is now 0.3pp above the 4% natural-rate anchor and the trend has broken out of its prior band, I lean toward continued modest loosening over the next month, though the magnitude is still modest.
Would change its mind if: A print back at or below 4.2% would suggest this was noise; a print at/above 4.4% would confirm accelerating loosening.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_12m, unrate_12m_high, unrate_range_position, sahm_gap, unrate_distance_from_natural

=== term_premium ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last print (4.41) is down slightly from the recent local peak of 4.58 but still sits well above the 4.35-4.40 zone I flagged as my reversal trigger, and the 12-month range position (0.67) shows yields in the upper third of the past year's range. My prior "down" call was wrong in direction over the last month: dgs10_change_21d is +0.24pp and change_63d is +0.17pp, both showing a net rise, not a decline, even though the very latest few observations have ticked down modestly (4.47→4.43→4.41). The 30s10s slope remains elevated at +0.51pp, consistent with continued long-end steepening pressure rather than compression, which typically signals rising term premium. The policy language reinforces this: the Fed is slowing the pace of balance-sheet runoff (redemption cap cut from $25bn to $5bn), which is a modest disinflationary-for-term-premium force (less supply pressure from the Fed side), but Waller's dissent preferring to continue the faster runoff signals some committee tension and the slowing is not a reversal of runoff, just deceleration. Net: yields have oscillated but remain elevated versus 12-month lows, slope is steep, and the recent pullback is minor. I lean toward marginal upward or flat, not a resumption of the downtrend.
Would change its mind if: A sustained break below 4.35 combined with 30s10s flattening would confirm renewed term-premium compression and validate a down call.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_range_position, slope_30s10s

  term_premium lacks balance_sheet — Pace and market absorption of Treasury issuance/runoff changes directly affect long-end supply pressure.
  term_premium lacks financial_conditions — Broader risk-on/risk-off flows affect flight-to-quality demand for long bonds.

user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: down, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The latest print (6,662,200) fell sharply from the prior week (6,681,056), a drop of ~18,856M in a single week — larger than the trailing 4-week change of -11,044M, confirming the runoff is still active and even accelerating somewhat versus the immediately preceding weeks. The 13-week trend remains firmly negative (-78,053M, -1.16%), and assets sit well below the 52-week high (-561,879M). There was some noisy sideways/up movement a few observations back (e.g., 6,673,244→6,672,885→6,677,155→6,681,056 showed a brief uptick), which had tempered my prior conviction, but the most recent observation reverses that bounce decisively to a new series low. Policy language is unchanged: continued balance-sheet reduction (QT) with no signal of tapering or halting caps. There is no evidence in front of me of runoff flattening toward zero — the opposite, a fresh series low, is what I see. Given the announced caps and the plain confirmation of continued decline in the newest data point, I maintain a down call, and with slightly higher conviction than before since the most recent print resolved the intra-month noise in the direction consistent with ongoing QT.
Would change its mind if: A weekly rise in fed_assets, or confirmation that actual runoff has flattened to near-zero under current caps.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_pct_change_13w, assets_below_52w_high

=== curve_slope ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.25
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last print shows a pullback: slope_2s10s eased from 0.56 to 0.52 after five straight prints in the 0.55-0.56 range, giving the newest reading a small down-tick. My prior "up" call was directionally right through most of the window (spread rose from 0.44-0.49 base to the mid-0.50s, slope_change_252d +0.94pp, slope_change_63d +0.14pp) but the most recent single-period move is a modest reversal, not a confirmed break — slope_change_21d is still slightly positive (+0.01pp), well above the -0.10pp threshold I set for calling a genuine reversal. So the flattening signal from the last data point is real but weak and could easily be noise/curve-normalization pause after a steep run. The spread remains near the top of its 12-month range (range_position 0.86, only 0.15pp below the 12m high of 0.67) and 0.93pp above the 12m low, so the dominant one-year trend is still steepening from deeply inverted territory. Given the recent tick-down, I'll moderate conviction and shade toward flat-to-slightly-up rather than repeating a firm "up" call, since momentum has clearly stalled even if the reversal isn't confirmed.
Would change its mind if: A further drop below 0.48 with slope_change_21d turning below -0.10pp would confirm a genuine flattening reversal rather than a pause.
Leaned on: slope_2s10s, slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d, slope_change_252d, slope_range_position, slope_12m_high, slope_12m_low

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: down, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The NFCI series shows a persistent, steady easing trend: from -0.41 down to -0.49 over 13 weeks, with the most recent prints (-0.45, -0.46, -0.47, -0.48, -0.49) showing consistent, unbroken monthly declines. The 4-week change (-0.03) and 13-week change (-0.06) both confirm continued easing momentum, and the pace of decline in the most recent weeks looks slightly faster than the trailing 13-week average would suggest, since the last five readings alone account for half of the 13-week move. The index sits at -0.49, near its 52-week low of -0.51 (only 0.02 above it) and far below the zero average, meaning conditions are already loose and getting looser. Range position of 0.12 (out of 1) confirms the index is near the bottom of its 52-week range. My prior call (down) was correct — there was no reversal above -0.40, and the 4-week change remained negative throughout. With no offsetting signal in the latest statement and momentum still pointing lower, I expect continued easing into month-end, though the pace of decline is modest and could stall as the index approaches new lows.
Would change its mind if: A reversal back above -0.45 with a flat-to-positive 4-week change would signal the easing trend has stalled or reversed.
Leaned on: nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_52w_low, nfci_above_52w_low, nfci_range_position, nfci

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "up" call was wrong: breakeven_10y has stabilized/drifted slightly lower, not higher. The series shows a peak of 2.33 mid-window, then a decline to 2.28-2.29, where it has held for the last three readings. be_change_21d (-0.03) and be_change_63d (-0.08) are both negative, while be_change_252d remains positive (+0.07), indicating the longer-term uptrend has stalled and reversed modestly over the past one to three months. The level has settled at 2.29, comfortably within the 12-month range (61st percentile) and 0.29pp above the 2% target — not extreme, but not falling toward target either. The last three prints (2.28, 2.28, 2.29) show flattening rather than continued decline, suggesting the recent drop has found a floor. Given the negative short and medium-term momentum but the apparent stabilization in the very latest readings, I see this as essentially flat with a slight downward tilt rather than a resumption of the prior uptrend. My falsifier condition (21d change turning negative) has now been met, which per my own criteria reverts this call away from "up."
Would change its mind if: A move back above 2.32 sustained for multiple readings, or be_change_21d turning positive again, would revert this to an up call.
Leaned on: be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_change_252d, be_range_position

=== inflation ===
Call: up, conviction 0.45
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 15 days ago.
Report:
  The last print rose to 2.68% y/y headline from 2.38% prior month, with headline_mom jumping to 0.25% from 0.10% — a clear reacceleration in the monthly pace, though not above my 0.30% falsifier threshold. Core PCE ticked up to 2.78% from 2.61%, also below the 2.9% rebound threshold I'd flagged, so neither trigger was strictly hit, but both moved in the accelerating direction. My prior "flat" call undershot this bounce: headline_yoy_change_3m is now +0.30pp, confirming upward momentum over the recent quarter even as core_yoy_change_3m is still -0.18pp (base effects still working through core). The 3m annualized rates for both headline and core sit at 2.08%, right near target-consistent pace, which tempers alarm — the underlying trend isn't runaway, but the single-month jump in mom is notable and outgoing_mom equals the 3m average, suggesting the higher pace is not just a one-off outlier. Given headline is coming off a 12m-range low reading two months prior and has now bounced meaningfully, I lean toward another firm or slightly higher headline print next month, driven partly by base effects unwinding. Core remains the better persistence signal and is only modestly elevated, so I don't see a sharp acceleration, just a mild upward drift.
Would change its mind if: A headline mom print below 0.10% or a drop in headline yoy back toward 2.3-2.4% would indicate the bounce was noise, not trend.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, headline_mom, headline_yoy_change_3m, core_pce_yoy, core_yoy_change_3m, headline_3m_annualized, core_3m_annualized

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.20
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 22 days ago.
Report:
  The latest print fell to 4.10% from 4.30%, undershooting my falsifier threshold (4.2% or below) that I flagged as "noise" territory. My prior 'up' call at 0.45 conviction was wrong for this release — the rate did not continue rising, it reversed sharply. The 13-month series shows a bouncy but essentially range-bound pattern (4.00-4.30%) with no sustained trend: unrate_change_6m and unrate_change_12m are both flat at 0.00pp, and the 3m change is now -0.10pp. Sahm-rule spirit gap is a modest 0.20pp, well below recession-signal thresholds, and unrate_range_position at 0.33 shows the rate sits in the lower third of its recent range, not the upper end. The level (4.10%) is only 0.10pp above my assumed natural rate, essentially at equilibrium. Policy language has shifted to flag two-sided risk (higher unemployment now explicitly mentioned alongside inflation), which warrants some caution, but the unchanged language still calls conditions "solid" and "stabilized at a low level." Given the whipsaw within a narrow band and a fresh move back to the cycle's tighter end, I read this as broadly flat with a slight loosening risk bias given the new two-sided risk language.
Would change its mind if: A print at/above 4.3% would signal renewed loosening; a print at/below 4.0% would confirm continued tightening beyond noise.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_6m, unrate_change_12m, sahm_gap, unrate_range_position, unrate_distance_from_natural, unrate_12m_low, unrate_12m_high

=== term_premium ===
Call: down, conviction 0.40
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 10-year has drifted lower over the last 10 readings, from 4.46 to 4.24, with the most recent print making a fresh local low. The 21-day change of -0.19pp shows clear recent downward momentum, though the 63-day (-0.03pp) and 252-day (+0.01pp) changes show this is a fairly recent development rather than a sustained multi-month trend. My prior flat call is now looking wrong on the recent print: yields broke below the 4.35 threshold I had flagged as a trigger for a down call, and did so with the range position sitting at 0.53 (still mid-range, not extreme) but with the trajectory clearly pointing down. The 30s10s slope at 0.54pp has not meaningfully flattened, so the confirmation is partial, not full. Still, continued balance-sheet runoff should, per the standing framework, exert some upward pressure on long-end supply/premium, yet the measured yield has moved the other way, suggesting other forces (perhaps softer growth/inflation expectations or flight-to-quality flows) are dominating for now. Given the clear recent momentum lower and the break of my own stated trigger level, I am shifting from flat to a modest down call, though I temper conviction since the slope didn't flatten as much as I'd want and the longer-window changes are flat-to-slightly-up.
Would change its mind if: A rebound back above 4.35 with 30s10s steepening further would indicate the recent decline was noise, not a genuine premium shift.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_range_position, slope_30s10s

=== What the panel says it was never handed ===
  term_premium lacks balance_sheet — Pace/composition of runoff and Treasury issuance guidance would clarify supply pressure on long end.
  term_premium lacks financial_conditions — Broader risk sentiment would clarify if recent yield drop reflects flight-to-quality.

Term premium's softness is therefore not a contradiction; it's consistent with a front-end-led easing move that steepens the curve as long as front-end yields fall faster than the long end. user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  My prior call (down, 0.65) was wrong on the near-term print: the last three weekly readings show a modest but consistent rebound (6602071 → 6605962 → 6608597 → 6608395), and the 4-week change has flipped positive (+5011 $M) after months of decline. This is a small move against a backdrop of the 13w change still negative (-53805 $M) and the 52w change deeply negative (-471664 $M), so the multi-month QT trend remains intact and the level sits well below its 52w high (-438530 $M). However, the policy language still explicitly commits to continued runoff of Treasury and MBS holdings, which is inconsistent with sustained balance-sheet growth. The most plausible read is that weekly noise (settlement timing, coupon reinvestment lumpiness, or a temporary slowdown in redemptions) is producing a short-lived uptick rather than a genuine policy shift, since no language change accompanies it. Given the explicit continuation of runoff guidance, I lean toward flat-to-slightly-down for the next month-end print, but the recent 4-week reversal materially lowers my confidence in a clean "down" call versus my last release.
Would change its mind if: A continued multi-week rise in fed_assets above recent levels, or explicit policy language announcing a pause/end to runoff, would flip this to up.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_change_52w

=== curve_slope ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.25
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The previous call (up, 0.45) leaned on continued steepening, but the falsifier I set has now triggered: the spread printed 0.56, just barely above the 0.55 threshold, after touching 0.52 two prints back, and slope_change_21d is -0.08pp (negative), confirming the near-term momentum has stalled/reversed from the prior steepening impulse. The last observation (0.56) is a modest bounce off the recent low, but the series has been choppy in a 0.52-0.59 band for the last several prints with no clear trend — essentially oscillating rather than trending. Longer lookbacks still show steepening (63d +0.08, 252d +0.30) and slope_range_position at 0.83 shows the spread sits high in its 12-month range, so the broad trend since a year ago is toward steeper/less inverted. But the immediate 21-day signal is flattening, and the rate cut just delivered (with a dissent for a larger cut) suggests front-end rates falling further could still pull 2s10s wider — but that's a slope_slope_curve judgment, and current data doesn't show sequential confirmation. Given the choppiness and conflicting near vs long-term signals, I see this as close to flat with a slight tilt toward the recent stalling pattern rather than confident continuation of steepening.
Would change its mind if: A clean break above 0.60pp with slope_change_21d turning positive would confirm renewed steepening; a break below 0.50pp would confirm flattening resumption.
Leaned on: slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d, slope_change_252d, slope_range_position

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The NFCI series has been essentially pinned at -0.52 for the last seven readings after a gradual slide from -0.49. The 4-week change is flat (+0.00) and the index sits at its 52-week low, with range position near 0.01 (bottom of its recent band). This confirms the prior flat call was accurate: momentum has fully stalled after the earlier easing trend, with no fresh break lower or reversal higher. Conditions remain looser than the historical average (distance from zero -0.52), but there is no directional impulse currently — the series has plateaued rather than continuing to ease or beginning to tighten. Absent any new move in either direction, and with the latest policy statement silent on this driver, the most defensible call for month-end is unchanged. The flatness across seven consecutive prints is unusually persistent, which lowers the odds of a sudden move but also means any small wiggle could tip the reading marginally either way. I'll maintain a flat call, with slightly higher conviction than last time given the extended stability, though still capped since a plateau this long could break in a fresh direction on the next release.
Would change its mind if: A move back above -0.45 with flat-to-positive 4-week change signals reversal/tightening; a fresh break below -0.52 signals renewed easing.
Leaned on: nfci, nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_distance_from_zero, nfci_52w_low, nfci_range_position

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: down, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "flat" call was falsified on the downside: breakeven has drifted from 2.38-2.39 down to 2.36, printing a fresh 12-month-range low close (2.35 then 2.36) and confirming a break below my 2.39 threshold for more than two readings. The 21-day change is now -0.05pp, reversing what had been a positive 63-day (+0.07) and 252-day (+0.18) trend. This is a modest but real short-term downshift, not a collapse — breakeven remains 0.36pp above the 2% target-equivalent and well within the 12-month range (range_position 0.68, still closer to the high than the low). The move is small in magnitude (last two prints 2.35, 2.36 vs prior cluster at 2.37-2.39) and could be noise, but the consistency of the pullback across the last 3 readings and the negative 21d change versus positive medium/long-term changes suggests some loss of upward momentum. I'll shift toward a modest "down" lean for the next month, though conviction is limited given the small absolute moves (a few basis points) and the still-elevated range position.
Would change its mind if: A rebound back above 2.38 sustained for two readings would negate the downtrend; a further break below 2.34 would confirm it more strongly.
Leaned on: be_change_21d, be_12m_low, be_range_position

=== inflation ===
Call: up, conviction 0.55
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 15 days ago.
Report:
  Last release confirmed the prior "up" call: headline CPI y/y rose to 3.02% from 2.94%, and m/m came in at 0.30% — above my 0.15% stall threshold and above the 3m average of 0.29%, so acceleration continued rather than stalled. Headline is now at the top of its 12-month range (yoy_range_position=1.00), up 0.70pp from the 12m low, and the 3-month annualized pace (3.54%) is running well above the y/y rate, indicating momentum is still building, not fading. Core PCE also ticked up to 2.91% from 2.86%, and its 3m annualized pace (2.97%) is consistent with a core y/y still near 2.9%, well above the 2% target (core_distance_from_target 0.91pp). Core-minus-headline is only -0.11pp, so the two measures are telling a consistent story rather than diverging sharply. Weighting core for persistence, but headline's recent momentum is now the more informative signal for the next print given its faster 3m annualized pace. With outgoing momentum unchanged (mom_gap_vs_outgoing=0.00) and headline sitting at a 12-month high, I see continued mild upward pressure into the next release, though the pace of acceleration itself may be leveling rather than steepening further.
Would change its mind if: A headline m/m print below 0.15%, or headline y/y falling back below 2.9%, would signal the acceleration is stalling or reversing.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, headline_mom, headline_3m_annualized, core_pce_yoy, core_3m_annualized, headline_yoy_change_3m, yoy_range_position, core_distance_from_target

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: up, conviction 0.45
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 22 days ago.
Report:
  The latest print rose to 4.40%, up from 4.30% prior — confirming the loosening trend I flagged last time rather than mean-reverting. This is now the 12-month high, sitting at the top of the observed range (range_position=1.00) and 0.40pp above both the trailing 12-month low and the ~4.00% natural-rate anchor. The 3-month change (+0.30pp) and Sahm-style gap (+0.33pp) show the rise is not just a one-off blip but a sustained drift over the last quarter, even though the 6-month change (+0.20pp) is more modest, indicating the pace has picked up recently rather than being a steady multi-month grind. Since my prior threshold for "accelerating loosening" (a print above 4.30%) has now been triggered, I'm raising conviction on continued softening. Policy language remains generic ("labor market conditions remain solid") and unchanged, offering no independent signal — it reads as a lagging description rather than forward guidance. Absent a sudden reversal, the momentum and level both point to further mild upward drift in the unemployment rate over the next month, though the move is still modest in absolute terms (0.40pp off the low), keeping this well short of a sharp-recession-signal magnitude.
Would change its mind if: A print back at/below 4.20% would confirm stabilization; a print above 4.50% would confirm sharp acceleration and raise conviction further.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, sahm_gap, unrate_range_position, unrate_distance_from_natural, unrate_12m_high

=== term_premium ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.20
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 10-year sits at 4.16, essentially flat versus the last print (4.15) and within a tight 4.06-4.20 band over the last 10 readings. My prior "down" call was only partially validated: the 21d and 63d changes are indeed modestly negative (-0.07pp, -0.10pp), consistent with mild downward drift, but the level has not broken below the 4.15 threshold I set as confirmation — it's oscillating right around it, and the last two prints (4.15, 4.16) show stabilization rather than continuation lower. The 252d change (+0.37pp) reminds us the broader trend over the past year has been upward, and range position (0.40) is mid-range, not signaling stress in either direction. The 30s10s slope (0.57pp) is unchanged from typical recent readings — no steepening burst that would flag fresh term-premium pressure, but no flattening either. Ongoing balance-sheet runoff continues to be a structural upward force on long-end supply, but it's already priced into the level context rather than a new catalyst. With the short-term drift down but the medium-term trend up, and the yield hovering in a narrow range without a clear break, I see this as directionless near-term — a slight lean down given the recent 21d/63d momentum, but conviction should be low given the stall.
Would change its mind if: A clean break above 4.20 with 30s10s steepening past 0.65 would signal renewed upward pressure; a decisive move below 4.10 would confirm downside continuation.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_change_252d, dgs10_range_position, slope_30s10s

=== What the panel says it was never handed ===
  inflation_expectations lacks inflation — Recent realized CPI surprises often drive short-term breakeven moves and would clarify if this dip is data-driven.
  inflation_expectations lacks financial_conditions — Liquidity/risk-off episodes can compress breakevens independent of true inflation views.

=== balance_sheet ===

This is a clear, explicit announcement ending QT — exactly the kind of language trigger I said would flip my view from flat to up. My prior flat call (0.35 conviction) was reasonable given the runoff was ongoing, but the new statement is a decisive regime change: balance sheet runoff is set to stop, meaning assets should stabilize and likely tick up modestly from reinvestment/maturity proceeds being rolled over rather than drained. Given the announcement is fresh and the last few weekly prints already show the pace flattening to near-zero, I expect fed_assets to be flat-to-slightly-up at month end, with upside risk if reinvestment begins immediately.
Would change its mind if: A continued decline in fed_assets over the next few weekly prints despite the "conclude reduction" language, or a walk-back of the announcement.

Weighting core for persistence, but headline's recent momentum is now the more informative signal for the next print given its faster 3m annualized pace. With outgoing momentum unchanged (mom_gap_vs_outgoing=0.00) and headline sitting at a 12-month high, I see continued mild upward pressure into the next release, though the pace of acceleration itself may be leveling rather than steepening further.

The 3-month change (+0.30pp) and Sahm-style gap (+0.33pp) show the rise is not just a one-off blip but a sustained drift over the last quarter, even though the 6-month change (+0.20pp) is more modest, indicating the pace has picked up recently rather than being a steady multi-month grind. Since my prior threshold for "accelerating loosening" (a print above 4.30%) has now been triggered, I'm raising conviction on continued softening. Policy language remains generic ("labor market conditions remain solid") and unchanged, offering no independent signal — it reads as a lagging description rather than forward guidance. Absent a sudden reversal, the momentum and level both point to further mild upward drift in the unemployment rate over the next month, though the move is still modest in absolute terms (0.40pp off the low), keeping this well short of a sharp-recession-signal magnitude.

The 30s10s slope at 0.56pp is elevated versus my prior threshold of concern (0.65) but has been drifting up, suggesting some long-end-specific richness building. Policy language changed materially: the Fed announced it will conclude balance-sheet runoff, which removes a technical source of upward pressure on long-end supply/duration that had been previously cited. That is a mild negative for term premium looking forward, working against the recent modest uptick. My previous flat call was directionally fine — level barely moved net (change_21d only -0.01). Given the tension between recent modest upward drift and the balance-sheet language shift removing a upward driver, I lean flat-to-slightly-up but without strong conviction.

user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: down, conviction 0.45
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  My prior call (up, 0.55) was wrong: fed_assets fell further, from 6,552,419 down to... actually the latest print (6,552,419) is the newest and lowest in the series, continuing a clear downtrend. The 4-week change is -34,615M and 13-week change is -50,965M, both negative, and the series has been grinding lower almost every observation for the past several months, now 345,066M below the 52-week high. This is runoff, not expansion, and it was still running at the time of the latest data point despite the new statement language announcing an end to reduction "on [date] " — a forward-looking commitment whose effective date is not yet reflected in the actual asset data shown. The policy language changed to say QT will conclude, which is the key reason to expect the pace of decline to flatten going forward, but the measured data through the last print still shows active, uninterrupted runoff with no sign of stabilization yet. Since the graded measurement is total assets and recent pace dominates, and the announcement's effective date is unknown relative to month-end, I weight the still-declining actual data more heavily this month while acknowledging the language points toward an imminent halt.
Would change its mind if: A weekly print at or above the prior level (flat/positive change) confirming the runoff has actually stopped per the new statement.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_below_52w_high

=== curve_slope ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.25
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior call (up, 0.30) anticipated a push above 0.55pp with positive 21d momentum confirming steepening. The latest prints show slope ticking up to 0.58 twice before settling back to 0.55 in the last two observations — so the steepening impulse partially materialized then faded, landing flat versus the prior reading. The 21d change is a modest +0.03pp (essentially flat), while the 63d change is -0.10pp, showing a recent flattening drift over the medium term that has stalled. The 252d change of +0.51pp confirms the dominant one-year trend has been steepening from near-zero, and range position at 0.82 (near the 12m high of 0.67) shows the curve remains historically un-inverted relative to its own recent history. The Fed's continued rate cuts (25bp, following another 25bp) support front-end anchoring while removing some flattening pressure, but the curve has plateaued at 0.55 for two straight readings after a brief push to 0.58, suggesting consolidation rather than a fresh trend. With mixed short-term momentum (up 21d, down 63d) and the level stalling just off its highs, this looks like sideways drift rather than a resumption of steepening or a return to flattening. I revise down conviction on "up" given the failed follow-through above 0.58.
Would change its mind if: A clean break above 0.58pp sustained for multiple readings would confirm renewed steepening; a drop below 0.50pp would confirm flattening resuming.
Leaned on: slope_2s10s, slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d, slope_change_252d, slope_range_position, slope_12m_high

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.45
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The NFCI has ticked up marginally from -0.52 to -0.50 over the recent window, a very small +0.01 to +0.02 index move over 4 and 13 weeks. This is consistent with my prior flat call being roughly correct - the series has been essentially pinned near -0.52 for most of the window with only the last observation nudging higher. Conditions remain deeply loose relative to the zero average (distance from zero of -0.50), well below the historical mean, and the 52-week range (-0.52 to -038) shows we're still near the loose end (range position 0.14). The tiny uptick is within noise for an index that moved by hundredths over 13 observations, and it hasn't breached my stated reversal threshold of -0.45. There's no policy language pointing to a shift in financial conditions. I don't see enough momentum here to call a genuine tightening turn - this still looks like a flat, low-volatility regime with a marginal drift toward tightening that isn't yet confirmed. I'll shade my conviction slightly down from pure flat neutrality given the consistent (if tiny) uptick across both 4-week and 13-week windows, which is a mild lean toward gradual tightening rather than pure stasis.
Would change its mind if: A move back above -0.45 with flat-to-positive 4-week change confirms tightening; a fresh break below -0.52 signals renewed easing.
Leaned on: nfci, nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_distance_from_zero, nfci_range_position

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: down, conviction 0.40
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "flat" call has been overtaken by evidence: breakeven_10y has now printed below 2.25 for several consecutive readings (2.24, 2.24, 2.23, 2.22, 2.23, 2.23), satisfying my own stated falsifier for a downside break. The series has drifted from 2.28 to 2.23 over the last ten readings, with be_change_21d at -0.05pp and be_change_63d at -0.20pp, both negative and consistent in direction. The 252d change of -0.11pp confirms this is not just short-term noise but part of a longer grind lower. The range position of 0.21 (near the 12m low of 2.17) further shows breakevens sitting in the bottom quintile of their annual range, having compressed from a high of 2.46. That said, the last three readings (2.22, 2.23, 2.23) show the decline losing momentum and possibly stabilizing just under 2.25 — this is a shallow drift, not a sharp break. Distance from the 2% target remains a still-material +0.23pp, so anchoring is intact but expectations continue to soften. Given deceleration in the pace of decline but a clear established lower trend versus a month ago, I lean toward continued mild softening rather than reversal.
Would change its mind if: A move back above 2.25 sustained for two consecutive readings would signal stabilization rather than continued decline.
Leaned on: be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_change_252d, be_range_position, be_above_12m_low

=== inflation ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.35
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 15 days ago.
Report:
  Last release: headline printed 2.99% y/y (down slightly from 3.02%), with m/m of 0.25% — essentially matching the recent 3m average (0.30%) and not falling below my 0.15% threshold, though it did tick just under the 2.9% y/y trigger... actually it landed at 2.99%, right at my watch level. My prior "up" call was roughly correct in direction (headline had been climbing from 2.33 to 3.02 over the year) but the latest print shows the ascent stalling rather than continuing — a marginal decline, not a fresh high. Headline 3m annualized (3.64%) still runs hotter than the y/y rate, suggesting some near-term upward pressure remains in the pipeline. However, core PCE continues to decelerate (2.75%, down from 2.99% oldest, with core_yoy_change_3m at -0.11pp and core_3m_annualized at a tame 2.60%), indicating underlying persistence is easing even as headline stays elevated near the top of its 12-month range (yoy_range_position 0.95). The gap between core and headline (-0.23pp) reflects energy/food volatility inflating headline. Given headline is near cycle highs but momentum (mom_gap_vs_outgoing +0.01) is flat rather than accelerating, and core is softening, I see the headline y/y as more likely to hold flat-to-slightly-down next month rather than push to new highs.
Would change its mind if: A headline m/m print above 0.35% or a jump in headline y/y above 3.1% would signal renewed acceleration beyond the current plateau.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, core_pce_yoy, headline_3m_annualized, core_3m_annualized, core_yoy_change_3m, yoy_range_position, outgoing_mom, headline_mom_3m_avg

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: up, conviction 0.55
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 22 days ago.
Report:
  The latest print rose to 4.50% from 4.40%, extending the up-trend I flagged last time and confirming the "up" call rather than the stabilization scenario. Unemployment is now at its 12-month high, sitting exactly at the top of the range (range_position=1.00), with the rate up 0.4pp over 12 months and 0.2pp over just the last 3 months. That 3-month pace, if annualized, is a meaningfully faster deterioration than the 12-month trend, consistent with a loosening labor market rather than noise around a flat level. The Sahm-style gap (current minus 12m low) is 0.50pp — not yet at the classic 0.50 trigger threshold in a stricter reading, but close enough to be notable, especially given the monotonic recent rise (4.30, 4.30, 4.40, 4.50) rather than a choppy pattern. Policy language changes reinforce this: officials now describe unemployment risks as having "risen" (past tense, more definitive) versus "risen" as an ongoing assessment previously, and job gains "slowed this year" is a firmer, more historical framing. This supports continued upward drift rather than reversal. Level is now 0.5pp above my 4% natural-rate anchor, and combined with the accelerating recent pace, I lean toward another modest increase or at least no reversion in the next print.
Would change its mind if: A print back at/below 4.30% would signal stabilization; a print above 4.60% would confirm sharp acceleration and raise conviction further.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_12m_high, unrate_range_position, sahm_gap, unrate_distance_from_natural

=== term_premium ===
Call: down, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 10-year yield has drifted from 4.14 down to 4.02 over the recent window, sitting near the very bottom of its 12-month range (range_position 0.06, only 5bp above the 12-month low of 3.97). The 63-day and 252-day changes are both meaningfully negative (-0.22pp, -0.39pp), confirming a sustained downtrend, though the most recent 21-day change is slightly positive (+0.03pp), suggesting the decline has stalled and yields have ticked up modestly off the lows in the very latest readings (4.00→4.02). My prior flat call was a mismatch with the data - the trend was clearly down over the quarter, not flat, though the last few prints show tentative stabilization/reversal. The 30s10s slope is 0.65pp, right at my prior "steepening" trigger level, but this reflects long-end curve shape rather than a confirmed breakout in the 10-year itself. The policy language shift - ending balance-sheet runoff - removes a source of upward pressure on term premium (less net Treasury supply absorption needed from private market), which is consistent with the recent low yields and argues against a resumption of the uptrend. On balance, the dominant signal is a yield sitting at 12-month lows with a supportive policy shift, but the tiny recent uptick tempers conviction that the down-move continues cleanly into month-end.
Would change its mind if: A clean break above 4.15 with 30s10s steepening further past 0.70 would signal renewed upward term-premium pressure; a fresh low below 3.95 confirms continuation lower.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_change_252d, dgs10_range_position, dgs10_above_12m_low, slope_30s10s

why.balance_sheet: Still-declining assets per latest data, but forward guidance of halt is not yet reflected — kept as reported, low slope relevance this month.

user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: up, conviction 0.60
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last several weekly prints show total assets rising sharply: 6,535,781 → 6,539,303 → 6,556,861 → 6,581,231, a clear turn off the trough. The 4-week change is now +28,812$M, positive, reversing the prior downtrend, even though the 13-week and 52-week changes remain negative (-27,164$M and -304,732$M respectively) reflecting the tail of the runoff period. My prior call of "down" was wrong for this most recent stretch — the weekly data confirms the trigger I specified (a print at/above prior levels, in fact a multi-week rising streak). This aligns with the policy language shift: the Committee has moved from "concluding reduction of holdings" to explicitly stating it will "initiate purchases of shorter-term Treasury securities as needed to maintain an ample supply of reserves on an ongoing basis." This is a clear statement that QT has ended and modest reserve-management purchases (effectively QE-lite) are beginning. Given both the measurement turn and the explicit policy language change, I expect total assets to be higher at next month-end, continuing the recent rebound, though the pace is likely to be modest/gradual rather than a large-scale expansion given the "as needed" framing.
Would change its mind if: A weekly print falling back below ~6,556,861$M (reversing the recent rebound) would undercut the expansion read.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w

=== curve_slope ===
Call: up, conviction 0.50
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior call of "flat" was wrong: the spread has continued to steepen, moving from 0.67 to 0.71pp over the last ten readings, with the 21d and 63d changes both solidly positive (+0.16, +0.19pp) and the 252d change even larger (+0.43pp), confirming a durable multi-month steepening trend rather than noise. The spread now sits at 0.96 on the 12-month range position — essentially at its highs for the year — having moved well off its 12-month low of 0.20pp. That said, the very latest print (0.71) follows a small pullback sequence (0.73→0.70→0.68→0.68→0.67→0.69→0.71), so there's some choppiness within the uptrend rather than a clean monotonic climb. Policy language shows continued rate cuts (Fed lowering the funds rate range again) with an added phrase emphasizing "extent and timing" of further adjustments — consistent with an easing cycle that typically supports front-end anchoring and further curve steepening as long-end yields don't fall as fast. The 10s30s slope at 0.66pp, roughly in line with 2s10s, suggests the long end isn't independently inverting the picture. Given the trend strength and level near highs, but with some short-term chop, I lean toward continued steepening but temper conviction given the noisy last few prints.
Would change its mind if: A drop back below 0.65pp sustained for multiple readings would signal the steepening trend has stalled or reversed.
Leaned on: slope_2s10s, slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d, slope_change_252d, slope_range_position, slope_12m_low, slope_12m_high, slope_10s30s

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The NFCI series remains extremely stable and near its 52-week low, closing the latest observation at -0.52, matching the low end of the range. The index has been pinned between -0.50 and -0.52 for the entire 13-week window, with a 4-week change of only -0.02 and a 13-week change of essentially zero. Range position of 0.01 confirms conditions are sitting right at the loosest point of the past year. There is no momentum in either direction — this is a flat, low-volatility regime with financial conditions persistently looser than the historical average (distance from zero of -0.52). My prior flat call was correct: the small wiggle to -0.52 at the end is within the noise band that has characterized the entire period, not a genuine break to renewed easing. Absent any new catalyst (and the latest policy statement is silent on this driver), the most likely outcome for month-end is a continuation of this same tight band around -0.51 to -0.52. I don't see evidence of either a decisive move back above -0.45 (which would signal tightening) or a sustained break meaningfully below -0.52 (which would confirm fresh easing) — the series is essentially oscillating within a 0.02-wide band at its floor.
Would change its mind if: A move back above -0.45 with flat-to-positive 4-week change confirms tightening; a sustained break below -0.52 with negative momentum signals fresh easing.
Leaned on: nfci, nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_distance_from_zero, nfci_range_position, nfci_52w_low

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last observation printed 2.25, the high of the 10-point window, after a stretch of stability around 2.23-2.24. My prior "down" call (conviction 0.40) anticipated continued softening, but the latest reading actually moved up to the top of the recent range, effectively triggering my own stated falsifier (a move back to/above 2.25). That said, it's only one observation, not yet "two consecutive readings" sustained above 2.25, so I should not over-read it. Looking at the broader picture: be_change_21d is now slightly positive (+0.01pp), while be_change_63d and be_change_252d are both -0.10pp, showing the medium/longer-term drift has been down even as the very short-term has ticked up. Breakeven sits at 2.25%, only 0.25pp above the 2% target, well within its 12-month range (range_position 0.28, closer to the low than the high), suggesting no runaway expectations. Given the tension between the longer-term downward drift and the recent uptick back to range highs, I read this as a stabilization rather than a resumption of decline, but not yet strong enough evidence to call a clear reversal upward. I'll shift toward flat, with modest conviction, acknowledging my prior down call was wrong-footed by the latest print.
Would change its mind if: A drop back below 2.22-2.23 sustained over two readings would confirm renewed decline; a sustained push above 2.26 would confirm a genuine upward turn.
Leaned on: be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_change_252d, be_range_position, be_distance_from_target

=== inflation ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.35
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 16 days ago.
Report:
  The previous "flat" call largely held: headline y/y printed 3.00, essentially unchanged from 2.99, and the m/m gap vs. outgoing (-0.06pp) confirms no acceleration surprise. Headline sits at its 12-month high (yoy_range_position 0.97), having climbed steadily from the 2.33 trough, but the pace of increase has clearly plateaued over the last three prints (2.94, 2.99, 3.00) — a flattening curve rather than a new leg up. Momentum measures are mixed: headline_3m_annualized (3.43%) still runs hotter than the y/y print, suggesting some upward drift risk, but core_3m_annualized (2.40%) is decelerating and core y/y has actually ticked down 0.08pp over three months, with the latest print (2.83) below the recent local peak (2.91). Core running below headline (core_minus_headline -0.17pp) and decelerating is the more important signal for persistence, and it argues against imminent reacceleration in headline. Net: headline is near a local plateau at an elevated level (0.8-1.0pp above target on core), with recent momentum roughly flat-to-slightly-up on headline but cooling on core. I don't see enough evidence for a confident directional break either way — most likely essentially unchanged next print, perhaps a marginal uptick given the 3m annualized skew.
Would change its mind if: A headline m/m print above 0.35% or headline y/y above 3.15% would signal renewed acceleration; a drop below 2.85% y/y would confirm deceleration.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, core_pce_yoy, headline_3m_annualized, core_3m_annualized, core_yoy_change_3m, mom_gap_vs_outgoing, yoy_range_position

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: up, conviction 0.30
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 23 days ago.
Report:
  The latest print fell to 4.40% from 4.50%, a decline of 0.10pp — this is the "below 4.60% acceleration threshold" and close to my stated stabilization marker (4.30% or below), though it didn't quite reach that level. My prior "up" call was directionally too aggressive for this print; the rate actually ticked down last month rather than continuing to rise. That said, the broader trend over 12 months is still up (+0.20pp), the 3-month change is still positive (+0.10pp), and the level remains 0.40pp above the 12-month low and 0.40pp above the natural rate estimate, with the Sahm gap holding at 0.43pp — closer to conventional recession-signal territory than to full stabilization. Policy language changes are notable: officials now describe downside employment risks as having "risen in recent months" (upgraded from a static risk assessment), and note "more recent indicators are consistent with" the slowing/upward-edging narrative — suggesting they see continued softening beneath the single-month dip. Given the single-month decline could be noise around a still-elevated and drifting-up base, and the policy tone leans toward further loosening, I lean toward another modest uptick or at least sideways-to-up next report, but with reduced conviction since the immediate print surprised to the downside.
Would change its mind if: A print at or below 4.30% would confirm stabilization/reversal; a print above 4.60% would confirm renewed sharp acceleration.
Leaned on: unrate_change_12m, unrate_change_3m, sahm_gap, unrate_above_12m_low, unrate_distance_from_natural, unrate_range_position

=== term_premium ===
Call: up, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 10y has drifted sideways-to-slightly-up over the last month, closing at 4.18%, matching its recent high in this window. My prior "down" call was wrong in direction (though small in magnitude): the 21d change is now +0.09pp and the yield sits at the top of its recent range, not breaking lower toward 3.95 as I'd flagged. The 12m range position (0.26) shows yield still in the lower third of its 12-month band, but the short-term momentum is modestly up. The 30s10s slope at 0.66pp has not cleared my 0.70 steepening threshold, so the strongest upside trigger isn't confirmed, but it's closer than before. Policy language has shifted meaningfully: the Fed ending balance-sheet runoff and now signaling it will conduct purchases of shorter-term Treasuries to maintain reserve levels is a mild net negative for long-end term premium pressure (removes some supply overhang from runoff, and purchases are concentrated short-end, so less direct long-end support). This is a mixed signal — modest upward yield drift on measurements, offset by ballast from the end of QT/runoff. Net: modest, low-conviction lean toward continued slight upward drift or flat, not the down call I made last time.
Would change its mind if: A break below 4.05 with 30s10s flattening toward 0.55 would confirm renewed downward term-premium pressure; a clean push above 4.25 with slope above 0.70 confirms upside.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_range_position, slope_30s10s

=== What the panel says it was never handed ===
  term_premium lacks balance_sheet — Details on pace/size of new short-term Treasury purchases would clarify net duration supply impact on long end.
  term_premium lacks financial_conditions — Broader risk sentiment would clarify if flight-to-quality is suppressing or not suppressing long yields.

arbitrated.notes: The curve_slope analyst is the direct read on 2s10s and it's the most informative: durable multi-month steepening (21d +0.16, 63d +0.19, 252d +0.43), spread near 12-month highs (0.96 range position), corroborated by 10s30s at similar level so it's not a long-end-only distortion. I lean heavily on this. Cross-checking against the front-end/long-end drivers: labor_tightness (front-end proxy) shows a policy tone tilting toward further Fed easing (downside employment risk "risen"), which anchors the front end via cuts — consistent with steepening, not against it. term_premium (long-end proxy) shows the 10y drifting modestly higher (21d +0.09) but from a still-low 12m range position (0.26), and explicitly flags that QT-ending/short-end purchases are a mixed, modestly negative-for-long-end-premium signal — this doesn't undercut steepening, since a modestly firm-to-flat long end against a front end anchored lower by cuts is exactly a steepening setup. Balance_sheet's shift to reserve-management purchases concentrated in short-term Treasuries is itself a technical support for the front end (more demand there), which could modestly cap front-end yields further and add to steepening, though its direct read is on the level of assets, not slope — I use it only as corroboration, not a primary driver. No driver here points to flattening. The chop in the very latest few curve_slope prints (0.67-0.71) is real but insufficient to override the trend given 63d/252d strength and confirmation from 10s30s. Net: continued steepening into month-end, moderate-high conviction, tempered slightly for the noted short-term choppiness.

discounted.why: Level driver, not a direct slope signal; used only as secondary corroboration for front-end demand.

arbitrated.falsifier: A 2s10s print sustained below 0.65pp for multiple readings, or 30s10s flattening sharply, would flip this to a flattening call.

risks.text: Recent 2s10s prints show choppiness (0.67-0.71) that could presage stalling rather than continued steepening.
If Fed pauses cuts or signals hawkish shift, front-end anchor could lift, flattening the curve unexpectedly.
A long-end selloff on term-premium repricing could steepen for the wrong reason, complicating attribution.
Steepener trades are crowded; a squeeze could produce sharp reversal in the slope independent of fundamentals.

trade.rationale: Steepener: short DGS2 / long DGS10 equally weighted to isolate slope, expressing continued 2s10s steepening driven by front-end anchoring from cuts and stable-to-firm long end.

why.curve_slope: Direct 2s10s read: durable steepening trend, near 12m highs, confirmed by 10s30s; some near-term chop tempers conviction.
why.term_premium: 10y drifting modestly higher but still low in 12m range; mixed vs QT-end supply effect — used as long-end corroboration, not driver.
why.labor_tightness: Policy tone leans toward further cuts, anchoring front end lower — supports steepening via front-end channel.
why.balance_sheet: Assets turning up on reserve-management purchases concentrated short-end, modest technical support for front end, reinforcing steepening.
